   \n                            \n                        \n                    \n                \n            \n\n            \n            User flow\n            \n                \n                    \n                        The Non-Document Identity Verification process in Bangladesh consists of the following steps:\n                        \n                            Applicant provides their NID number and date of birth.\n                            Sumsub validates the provided data through the government database.\n                            Applicant is offered to pass Liveness if the data is valid.\n                            Applicant passes Liveness.\n                            Sumsub compares the Liveness results with the applicant’s photos from the corresponding database.\n                            Applicant is approved if there is the same person on both Liveness and the photo from the database, and rejected if not.\n                            \n                        \n                    \n                    \n\n                    \n                        \n                    \n                \n            \n\n            \n            Legal substantiation and limitations\n            \n                \n                    In Bangladesh, non-documentary identity verification is currently endorsed under the\n                    2019 Guidelines on Electronic Know Your Customer (e-KYC) (“Guidelines”), issued by the Bangladesh Financial Intelligence Unit (BFIU)\n                    and applicable to all reporting entities. However, it implies several restrictions:\n                \n                \n                    the Guidelines only apply to KYC conducted in respect of natural persons holding a valid national ID card (NID) of Bangladesh with biometric data stored therein;\n                    \n                        fully remote KYC (where the customer does not visit the premises of the reporting entity) prescribes a seamless procedure with the following steps: (i) the NID is captured from both sides, with the data extracted by\n                        OCR; (ii) the customer’s face is captured with a high-resolution camera; (iii) the necessary identity data (name, parental names, address, phone number, etc.) is collected in digital format; (iv) the client’s wet\n                        signature or electronic signature or digital signature or PIN is collected for future reference; (v) the data is authenticated against the official database held by the NID Wing of Election Commission; and (vi) AML\n                        screening is carried out (see Section 3.3 of the Guidelines).\n                    \n                \n                \n                    As for individuals that do not hold a NID, the document-based approach is predominant. For example, the\n                    Guidance Notes on Prevention of Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing for Financial Institutions by the BFIU and the Central Bank of Bangladesh suggest a\n                    photo-bearing ID (which, furthermore, has to be certified and, as per Section 7.3.5.1, supplemented with at least one additional check to “guard against impersonation”) is a necessary element in the KYC procedure:\n                \n                \n                    \n                        “The original, certified copy of the following Photo ID also plays vital role to identify the customer: (i) Current valid passport; (ii) Valid driving license; (iii) National ID Card; (iv) Employer provided ID Card,\n                        bearing the photograph and signature of the applicant; Identification documents which do not bear photographs or signatures, or are easy to obtain, are normally not appropriate as sole evidence of identity [...]”\n                    \n                    (Section 7.3.5).\n                \n                \n                    Likewise, in terms of confirming the customer’s address, one or more of the following steps is recommended:\n                \n\n                \n                    “provision of a recent utility bill, tax assessment or bank statement containing details of the address (to guard against forged copies it is strongly recommended that original documents are examined);\n                    checking the Voter lists;\n                    checking the telephone directory [the only reference to non-documentary evidence];\n                    visiting home/office;\n                    sending thanks letter” (Section 7.3.5).\n                \n                \n                    Accordingly, the only explicitly permitted electronic-based KYC solution is limited to NID holders and requires the customer to actually present the NID at the onboarding stage for capturing.\n                \n \n                \n                    \n                        The Non-Document Identity Verification process in Brazil consists of the following steps:\n                        \n                            Applicant provides their CPF number.\n                            Sumsub validates the provided number through the government database.\n                            Applicant is offered to pass Liveness if the CPF number is valid.\n                            Applicant passes Liveness.\n                            The government database compares the Liveness results with the applicant’s photos from the corresponding database.\n                            Applicant is approved if there is the same person on both Liveness and the photo from the database, and rejected if not.\n                            \n                        \n                    \n                    \n\n                    \n                        \n                    \n                \n  Manual\n                                            \n                                        \n                                    \n                                \n                            \n                        \n                    \n                \n            \n\n            \n            User flow\n            \n                \n                    \n                        The Non-Document Identity Verification process in Canada consists of the following steps:\n                        \n                            Applicant provides their Name, Date of Birth, and Address.\n                            Sumsub links the provided information to verified credit trade line records.\n                            Each identity element is automatically matched against the existing profile using either the Credit File or Dual-Process method.\n                            Applicant is approved if all identity elements successfully match, or rejected if the requirements are not met.\n                            \n                        \n                    \n                    \n                    \n                        \n                    \n          \n                                \n                            \n                        \n                    \n                \n            \n\n            \n            User flow\n            \n                \n                    \n                        The Non-Document Identity Verification process in Denmark consists of the following steps:\n                        \n                            Applicant provides their MitID User ID.\n                            Applicant authenticates themselves on the platform to achieve either a Substantial or High Level of Assurance.\n                            Applicant provides their personal identification (CPR) number.\n                            Applicant passes Liveness.\n                            The system instantly retrieves all personal data stored on the MitID platform and ensures its rightful ownership.\n                            Sumsub additionally cross-checks relevant applicant information to ensure a complete identity match.\n                            \n                        \n                    \n                    \n                    \n                        \n                    \n FTN Verification (finbankidftn)\n                                        \n                                            \n                                                \n                                                    FTN platform authentication\n                                                \n                                            \n                                        \n                                        \n                                            \n                                                \n                                                    First name\n                                                \n                                                \n                                                    Last name\n               \n                                            Manual\n                                        \n                                    \n                                \n                            \n                        \n                    \n                \n            \n\n            \n            User flow\n            \n                \n                    \n                        The Non-Document Identity Verification process in Finland consists of the following steps:\n                        \n                            Applicant selects the relevant ID provider (their bank from the FTN network).\n                            Applicant authenticates themselves in the selected bank’s system to achieve a Substantial Level of Assurance.\n                            The system instantly retrieves all available personal data stored on the FTN platform and ensures its rightful ownership.\n                            Optionally, applicant passes Liveness.\n                            Sumsub uses the retrieved data to verify the applicant’s identity.\n                            The applicant onboarding is complete.\n                            \n                100% coverage of all L’Identité Numérique holders.\n                        \n                    \n                \n            \n\n            \n            User flow\n            \n                \n                    \n                        The Non-Document Identity Verification process in France consists of the following steps:\n                        \n                            An applicant initiates the onboarding process by providing a phone number that is linked directly to the verified L’identité Numérique profile.\n                            The applicant authenticates via the La Poste application using a 4-digit PIN, confirming both their data ownership and real-time presence.\n                            Sumsub retrieves user data from the La Poste platform.\n                            The applicant onboarding is complete.\n                            \n                        \n                    \n                    \n\n               \n                                        India Aadhaar Verification via DigiLocker (indgovaadhaardigi)\n                                        \n                                            \n                                                \n                                                    Personal identification number (Aadhaar)\n                                                \n                                                \n                                                    Phone number authentication (OTP)\n                                                \n                                                \n                                                    Security PIN\n                                                \n                                                \n                                                    Selfie\n                                                \n                                            \n                                        \n                                        \n                                            \n                                                \n                                                    Complete name\n                                                \n                                                \n                                                    Aadhaar number (Masked)\n                                                \n                                                \n                                                    Date of birth\n                                                \n                                                \n                                                    Gender\n                                                \n                                                \n                                                    Address\n                                                \n                                                \n Match)\n                                                \n                                            \n                                        \n                                        \n                                            \n                                            Government\n                                        \n                                        \n                                            \n                                            95%\n                                        \n                                        \n                                            \n                                                Manual\n                                            \n                                        \n                                    \n                                \n                            \n                        \n                    \n                \n            \n\n            \n            User flow\n            \n                \n                    \n                        \n\n                        \n                        Digilocker verification process:\n\n                        \n                            Applicant provides their Aadhaar number on the Digilocker screen.\n                            If the Aadhaar number is valid and the phone number is associated, the government database sends the code to the applicant.\n                            Applicant provides the code and Sumsub sends it to the government database for verification.\n                            \n                                Applicant is suggested to input and validate the 6-digit Digilocker PIN associated with their Digilocker account. If the applicant does not have a Digilocker account yet, they can register and create a fresh\n                                6 -digit PIN on the same screen.\n                            \n                            Digilocker asks the applicant to give consent to use their 'Aadhaar' specific data for a certain period of time.\n                            Once the applicant consents, their Aadhaar details is finally verified and data is retrieved successfully.\n                            \n                        \n                    \n                    \n\n                    \n following steps:\n                        \n                            Applicant provides their KTP number and name.\n                            Sumsub validates the provided data through the government database.\n                            Applicant is offered to pass Liveness if the data is valid.\n                            Applicant passes Liveness.\n                            The government database compares the Liveness results with the applicant’s photos from the corresponding database.\n                            Applicant is approved if there is the same person on both Liveness and the photo from the database, and rejected if not.\n                            \n                        \n                    \n                    \n\n                    \n Sumsub.\n                            Applicant selects Kenya as their country of onboarding.\n                            Applicant provides their Passport or National ID number.\n                            Applicant passess Liveness.\n                            Sumsub extracts all personal data from the government database under the provided document number.\n                            Sumsub cross-matches the applicant selfie image with the photo image in the government database.\n                            Applicant is redirected back to the merchant website; onboarding is complete.\n     \n                            \n                        \n                    \n                \n            \n\n            \n            User flow\n            \n                \n                    \n                        The Non-Document Identity Verification process in Nigeria consists of the following steps:\n                        \n                            Applicant provides either their BVN (Bank Verification Number), NIN (National Identification Number), Phone Number or Driver's License number.\n                            Sumsub validates the provided number through a government database.\n                            Applicant is offered to pass Liveness if the data is valid.\n                            Applicant passes Liveness.\n                            Sumsub compares the Liveness results with the applicant’s photos from the corresponding database.\n                            Applicant is approved if there is the same person on both Liveness and the photo from the database, and rejected if not.\n                        \n                    \n                    \n\n                    \n                        \n                    \n their National identification number (NIN).\n                            Applicant authenticates themselves on the BankID application using their login credentials.\n                            The system instantly retrieves all personal data stored on the BankID platform and ensures its rightful ownership.\n                            Sumsub additionally cross-checks relevant user information to ensure a complete identity match.\n                            Applicant onboarding is successfully completed.\n                    \n                    \n                \n            \n     \n                    \n                \n            \n\n            \n            User flow\n            \n                \n                    \n                        The Non-Document Identity Verification process in Singapore consists of the following steps:\n                        \n                            Applicant initiates the verification session via Sumsub.\n                            Applicant selects Singapore as their country of onboarding and chooses identity document type.\n                            Applicant clicks the Retrieve Myinfo with Singpass button and then logs in to the Singpass using credentials or a QR code.\n                            Applicant agrees to retrieve personal details from their Singpass account.\n                            Sumsub transfers personal data from Singpass and populate it on the screen.\n                            Applicant reviews their personal data and decides whether to continue verification with the retrieved data or upload documents instead.\n                            Sumsub verifies transferred personal data; onboarding is completed.\n                            \n                        \n Verification process in the United Kingdom consists of the following steps:\n                        \n                            Applicant chooses their bank from the drop-down list.\n                            Applicant logs into their bank account and agrees to share their data.\n                            Sumsub transfers the data attributes to the merchant and confirms successful                    \n\n                    \n                        \n                    \n                \n                \n                \n                    \n                        The eVisa Non-Document Identity Verification process in the United Kingdom consists of the following steps:\n                        \n                            Applicant with a valid eVisa generates a share code to prove their immigration status.\n                            Applicant enters their date of birth and newly generated share code.\n                            Applicant performs a Liveness check to capture their biometrics and demonstrate real-time presence.\n                            The system instantly retrieves all personal data stored on the UKVI platform and confirms its ownership.\n                            Sumsub additionally cross-checks relevant user information to ensure a complete identity match.\n                            Applicant onboarding is successfully completed.\n                            \n                        \n                    \n                    \n\n                    \n                        \n                    \n process in the United States of America consists of the following steps:\n                        \n                            An applicant initiates the onboarding process by providing a phone number, SSN and date of birth.\n                            The applicant carries out Mobile Authentication using Sumsub OTP check.\n                            Sumsub extracts pre-verified user data relevant applicant information to ensure a complete identity match.\n                            Applicant onboarding is successfully completed.\n                            \n > 📘 Note
> 
> The data input method indicates how the applicant data required for verification can be submitted:
> 
> - Manual — manual filling by the user via the UI
> - OCR — automatic filling by extracting the information from supported identity document(s)
> - API **— automatic filling by submitting the information via API

# Explore alternative identity verification options

# Get started with Non-Doc Identity Verification
URL: https://docs.sumsub.com/docs/get-started-identity-verification

To start using Non-Doc Identity Verification, you can opt for:

- Code-free integration involving our SDKs.
- API integration.

> 📘 Note
> 
> Non-Doc Identity Verification comes at an additional cost regardless of the integration method. Make sure to contact us before starting the integration.

# Verify identity via Web and Mobile SDKs

Use our Web and Mobile SDKs to conduct Non-Doc Identity Verification code-free:

1. Set up the integration, as described in the WebSDK integration article and MobileSDK integration article.
2. Create a verification level and add a Non-Doc verification step.
3. Conduct verification and handle verification results.

# Use Sumsub API

You can utilize our public API endpoints to conduct identity verification using the Non-Doc solution:

1. Create a verification level with a Non-Doc step.
2. Create an applicant and pass the level name created at step 1 as the `levelName` query parameter.

```curl Request
curl -X POST \
  'https://api.sumsub.com/resources/applicants?levelName=doc-free-level' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
          "externalUserId": "someUniqueUserId",
      }'
```
```json Response
{
  "id": "65d72c063cf64929770e36c7",
  "createdAt": "2024-02-22 11:12:06",
  "key": "EJBDYWTFWAQXSH",
  "clientId": "clientid",
  "inspectionId": "65d72c063cf64929770e36c8",
  "externalUserId": "someUniqueUserId",
  "info": {},
  "applicantPlatform": "API",
  "requiredIdDocs": {
    "videoIdent": true,
    "videoIdentUploadTypes": [
      "IDCARD",
      "PASSPORT"
    ],
    "docSets": [
      {
        "idDocSetType": "IDENTITY",
        "types": [
          "IDCARD",
          "PASSPORT",
          "RESIDENCEPERMIT",
          "DRIVERS"
        ],
        "subTypes": [
          "FRONTSIDE",
          "BACKSIDE"
        ],
        "videoRequired": "disabled",
        "captureMode": "manualAndAuto",
        "uploaderMode": "always"
      },
      {
        "idDocSetType": "IDENTITY2",
        "types": [
          "VISA"
        ]
      }
    ]
  },
  "review": {
    "reviewId": "GwVKG",
    "attemptId": "VrrHy",
    "attemptCnt": 0,
    "levelName": "basic-kyc-level",
    "levelAutoCheckMode": null,
    "createDate": "2024-02-22 11:12:06+0000",
    "reviewStatus": "init",
    "priority": 0
  },
  "type": "individual",
  "inspectionMetadata": []
}
```

3. Submit the applicant data as the following example demonstrates.

```curl Request
curl -X POST \
  'https://api.sumsub.com/resources/applicants/631f268442d8290001e1eee9/ekyc/submit' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
        "info": {
            "country": "IND",
            "tin": "999999990019",
        }
    }'
```

You will receive one of the following answers, depending on the results and the need for confirmation: 

- The provided data is valid and no confirmation is required.
  ```json
  {
    "status" : "completed"
  }
  ```
- The provided data is valid and OTP confirmation is required.
  ```json
  {
      "status": "confirmationRequired",
      "confirmationId": "bzaPxnCpyxEqs0sJKASXxZIk",
      "confirmationStatus": "created",
      "confirmationType": "otp",
      "otpConfirmation": {
          "codeLength": "6",
      }
  }
  ```
- The provided data is valid and authentication through external services is required.
  ```json
  {
    "status" : "confirmationRequired",
    "confirmationType" : "oAuth",
    "confirmationId" : "wXhl47RZeJfoOSTmUFT9j5On",
    "confirmationStatus" : "created",
    "oauthConfirmation" : {
      "url" : "https://ext.auth.service.com/oidc/authorize?param1=value1&param2=value2",
      "redirectUrl" : "https://api.sumsub.com/resources/ekyc/redirect"
    }
  }
  ```

> If something goes wrong or the provided data is invalid, you will receive an error message with the error code and the applicant must resubmit their information.

4. [Optional] Confirm the applicant data if required. If in the previous step, you received `status : confirmationRequired`, you will need to confirm submission by one of the following methods, depending on the `confirmationType`.

```curl Request (OTP)
curl -X POST \
  'https://api.sumsub.com/resources/applicants/631f268442d8290001e1eee9/ekyc/confirm/bzaPxnCpyxEqs0sJKASXxZIk' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
        "otp": {
            "code": "123456",
        }
    }'
```
```http Request (oAuth)
curl -X POST \
  'https://api.sumsub.com/resources/applicants/631f268442d8290001e1eee9/ekyc/confirm/bzaPxnCpyxEqs0sJKASXxZIk' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
        "oauth": {
            "completeUrl": "https://in.sumsub.com/idensic/ekyc/redirect?code=rUKrpIoRZ44ENZnz8EC6FP",
        }
    }'
```
```json Response
{
    "status": "confirmationRequired",
    "confirmationType": "otp",
    "confirmationId": "bzaPxnCpyxEqs0sJKASXxZIk",
    "confirmationStatus": "retry",
    "otpConfirmation": {
        "codeLength": "6",
    }
}
```

5. Upload a selfie with `idDocType: "SELFIE"`.

```curl Request
curl -X POST \
  'https://api.sumsub.com/resources/applicants/5b75a5e80a975a3ef2102a87/info/idDoc' \
  -H 'Content-Type: multipart/form-data' \
  -H 'X-Return-Doc-Warnings: true' \
  -F 'metadata={"idDocType":"SELFIE","country":"USA"}' \
  -F 'content=@/SumSub/Example/name.jpg'

```
```json Response
{
  "idDocType" : "SELFIE",
  "country" : "USA"
}
```

6. Request an applicant check.

```curl Request
curl -X POST \
  'https://api.sumsub.com/resources/applicants/5b73b82f0a975a3b46429758/status/pending'
```
```json Response
{
  "ok": 1
}
```

7. Handle verification results.

# Enable Non-Doc Identity Verification at Identity document step

If you want to have both of the options—Non-Doc and Identity document—available as a single verification step in the WebSDK:

1. In the Dashboard, open the Supported ID Documents page and enable Non-Doc Check for countries where you want to make this option available.
2. Set up a verification level: 
   - In the General section, select the New version of WebSDK toggle to enable the WebSDK 2.0.
   - Add the following verification steps: Identity document, Selfie.

# Fallback to standard verification

In case something goes wrong during Non-Doc Identity Verification or you just want to verify applicants using the standard document verification approach:

- For SDKs, use the Workflow Builder to configure the verification flow as necessary.
- For the API, If you already have a complex logic on your side, move the applicants to a desired level by using this API method.

================================================================================

# Handle Non-Doc Identity Verification results
URL: https://docs.sumsub.com/docs/handle-results-identity-verification

Once the check is performed, you will receive a webhook with the verification status:

- The `reviewResult` object contains extra information about verification.
- The `reviewAnswer` field contains the final verification result, displaying the GREEN or RED status: 
  - GREEN — the applicant has passed Non-Doc Identity Verification, and their data has been successfully obtained from the government database.
  - RED — the applicant has not passed verification; the `reviewRejectType` is set to `RETRY` or `FINAL`. 

The possible reasons for the RED status are:

- Low face match score.
- Lack of information on the applicant (such as personal data, selfie) in the database.

# Get applicant data

To get a structured view of the applicant data, use this API method with the `type` parameter set to `EKYCCHECK`.

```http
curl --request GET \
     --url 'https://api.sumsub.com/resources/checks/latest?type=EKYCCHECK&applicantId=65c607aa36997b590dcdf127' \
```

# Check for obtained data

Make sure that you have received all the necessary information after the Non-Doc Identity Verification is complete. The name and date of birth are the basic fields that are always present in the response from the database. 

In some cases, however, some additional information may not be available; for example, the address in Brazil might be missing.

In such cases, you can perform an additional check to collect the missing data:

1. Use Workflow Builder to request an additional document if some data is missing. For example, request a proof of address document if the address is missing.
2. Request a new access token for a different level that contains additional steps. For example, you can collect additional data using a questionnaire.

# Clarify rejection reason

For every rejection reason, we have a particular `buttonId` attached to the applicant or photo. To get a list of rejection reasons based on the applicant ID, use this API method.

> 😀 Did you know?
> 
> When applicants skip or fail Non-Doc Identity Verification, they can be moved to another flow for passing verification using documents.

================================================================================

# Solution compliance assessment
URL: https://docs.sumsub.com/docs/solution-compliance-assessment

Sumsub offers its state-of-the-art Non-Doc Identity Verification solution to address the growing demand for automation, speed, and reduced manual intervention in KYC onboarding processes for both obligated financial institutions and their customers.

While Non-Doc Identity Verification is a relatively new user verification method, it has already been widely recognized by regulatory authorities on a global basis and made a significant operational impact for clients who have decided to implement it.

The following analyses elaborate on the compliance of Non-Doc Identity Verification with national AML/CTF regulations of multiple jurisdictions by examining in detail the requirements for customer risk assessment, data collection, and verification.

# Regulatory recognition

The table below provides a regulatory compliance scoring framework designed by the Sumsub's Legal team. Each in-scope jurisdiction is given an overall rating based on the degree to which it permits document-free methods for customer Identity Verification under the national AML/CTF laws.

Each individual country assessment offers detailed information on the nature of the findings, the rationale for the compliance score, the regulatory basis for the assessment, and a summary outlining Sumsub's interpretation and position.

For convenience purposes, the jurisdictions have also been segmented into tabs based on the overall score they were given.

| Score | Description                                                                                                                         |
| :---- | :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| 1     | The use of document-free identity verification as a standalone method is explicitly allowed either generally, or in specific cases. |
| 2     | The use of document-free identity verification as a standalone method is not explicitly prohibited.                                 |
| 3     | The use of document-free identity verification as a standalone method is explicitly ruled out.                                      |

> 📘 Note
> 
> In Brazil, both Score 1 and Score 2 are applied:
> 
> - Score 1 — for BACEN-regulated entities.
> - Score 2 — for CVM and SPA-regulated entities.
> 
> In Saudi Arabia, both Score 1 and Score 3 are applied:
> 
> - Score 1 — for SAMA-regulated entities.
> - Score 3 — for CMA-regulated entities and DNFPBs.
> 
> In Kazakhstan, both Score 1 and Score 3 are applied: 
> 
> - Score 1 — for Banks, Insurance companies, and Securities exchange participants.
> - Score 3 — for VASPs.

[block:html]
{
  "html": "\n    .line {\n        border-top: 1px solid rgb(0 0 0 / 10%);\n        margin-bottom: -32px;\n        margin-top: 50px !important;\n    }\n\n\n\n\n    \n    Score 1\n    \n        \n            Country:\n            \n                🇦🇺 Australia\n                🇧🇪 Belgium\n                🇧🇼 Botswana\n                🇧🇷 Brazil\n                🇨🇦 Canada\n                🇨🇾 Cyprus\n                🇨🇿 Czech Republic\n                🇩🇰 Denmark\n                🇪🇪 Estonia\n                🇫🇮 Finland\n                🇫🇷 France\n                🇬🇭 Ghana\n                🇭🇰 Hong Kong\n                🇮🇳 India\n                🇮🇩 Indonesia\n                🇮🇪 Ireland\n                🇮🇹 Italy\n                🇯🇵 Japan\n                🇰🇿 Kazakhstan\n                🇱🇹 Lithuania\n                🇲🇾 Malaysia\n                🇳🇱 Netherlands\n                🇳🇿 New Zealand\n                🇳🇬 Nigeria\n                🇳🇴 Norway\n                🇵🇭 Philippines\n                🇵🇱 Poland\n                🇸🇦 Saudi Arabia\n                🇸🇬 Singapore\n                🇿🇦 South Africa\n                🇰🇷 South Korea\n                🇪🇸 Spain\n                🇸🇪 Sweden\n                🇹🇭 Thailand\n                🇦🇪 UAE\n                🇬🇧 UK\n                🇺🇸 USA\n                🇺🇿 Uzbekistan\n          \n\n            \n                \n                    The anti-money laundering and counter terrorism financing legal framework in Australia is governed primarily by the\n                    Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006 (the \"AML/CTF Act\") and its related regulations. In turn,\n                    the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Rules Instrument 2007 (No. 1) (the \"AML/CTF Rules\")are subsidiary legislative instruments made under the AML/CTF\n                    Act and elaborating on the obligations set out therein.\n                \n                \n                    Specifically regarding customer identification and identity verification procedures, Part 4.2.3 of the AML/CTF Rules sets out the minimum KYC information to be collected about an individual customer: (i) full name, (ii) date of birth,\n                    and (iii) residential address; at least (i) and either (ii) or (iii) have to be subsequently verified, pursuant to Part 4.2.6.\n                \n                Further, Part 4.2.7 lists the acceptable methods of verifying the above-mentioned customer data:\n                    \n                        \"reliable and independent documentation;\n                        reliable and independent electronic data; or\n                        a combination of (1) and (2) above\".\n                    \n                \n                    The AML/CTF Rules offer different \"safe harbour\" verification approaches (documentation-based and electronic-based) depending on the risk profile of the customer. In cases where the risk is medium or lower, the procedure should involve,\n                    respectively:\n                \n                \n                    \n                        for the documentation-based approach: \"(a) an original or certified copy of a primary photographic identification document;1 or (b) both: (i) an original or certified copy of a primary non‑photographic identification document; and\n                        (ii) an original or certified copy of a secondary identification document\"2. The entity must also \"verify that any document produced about the customer has not expired (other than in the case of a passport issued by the Commonwealth\n                        that expired within the preceding two years)\" (AML/CTF Rules, Parts 4.2.10 - 4.2.11);\n                    \n                    \n                        for the electronic-based approach: use of reliable and independent electronic data from at least two separate data sources is required. The entity must also verify that the customer has a transaction history for at least the past 3\n                        years. (AML/CTF Rules, Parts 4.2.12 - 4.2.14).\n                    \n                \n                Accordingly, where the supervised entity relies on the electronic method only:\n                \n                    if the customer's name and date of birth are verified independently via different electronic sources, address does not need to be confirmed at all;\n                    conversely, if the date of birth is only collected and not verified, a reference to a single reliable electronic source should suffice for address validation, so long as the name is not checked against the same source.\n                \n                At the same time, pursuant to Part 4.10.2 of the AML/CTF Rules, when choosing an electronic source as a verification basis the reporting entity must determine:\n                \n                    \"whether the electronic data is reliable and independent, taking into account the following factors:\n                        \n                            (a) the accuracy of the data;\n                            (b) how secure the data is;\n                            (c) how the data is kept up‑to‑date;\n                            (d) how comprehensive the data is (for example, by reference to the range of persons included in the data and the period over which the data has been collected);\n                            (e) whether the data has been verified from a reliable and independent source;\n                            (f) whether the data is maintained by a government body or pursuant to legislation; and\n                            (g) whether the electronic data can be additionally authenticated; and\n                        \n                    \n                    what reliable and independent electronic data the reporting entity will use for the purpose of verification;\n                    the reporting entity's pre‑defined tolerance levels for matches and errors; and\n                    whether, and how, to confirm KYC information collected about a customer by independently initiating contact with the person that the customer claims to be\".\n                \n                As one of possible solutions, the Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre suggests the Document Verification Service (DVS):\n                \n                    \"One option for verifying individual customer and beneficial owner identification using electronic data is the Document Verification Service (DVS). This is a secure online system managed by the Department of Home Affairs. The DVS\n                    matches government-issued identity documents directly with the government organisation that issued them. This lets you check in real time that the document is current and not lost or stolen\".\n                \n                Consequently, an evaluation of the relevant client verification methods in Australia reveals the following:\n                \n                   Verifying a customer’s full name and date of birth using two separate electronic sources meets AML/CFT Rules (Part 4.2.6), and, especially, the requirements ​​of verifying the full name and either date of birth or address.\n                   Verifying the name and date of birth against one source, and name as well as the address against a separate source.\n                   Verifying the customer’s full name and residential address using two separate electronic sources fulfills the minimum requirement (Part 4.2.6) of verifying the name and one other element, with the compliance assessment noting that if the date of birth is verified together with the name, address verification is not needed. However, if the date of birth is collected but not verified, the name and address can be verified against a single source with different sources for the name and address; using two sources here exceeds the minimal threshold and meets regulatory standards, though omission of date of birth verification is permissible but less common than name plus date of birth combinations.\n                   \n                Further clarification indicates that verifying a name and date of birth using two sources removes the need for address verification,while verifying name and address against a single source is sufficient if date of birth is collected but not verified, provided name and address utilize different sources.\n                \n                    In conclusion, the current AML/CTF legislation of Australia allows the use of electronic data as a verification basis for both identity and address verification purposes so long as proper due diligence of the sources to be used is carried out. In practice, the electronic-based approach is arguably more viable (particularly for remote customer onboarding), as it may be impractical to obtain the originals or certified copies of identity documents. All verification methods that verify at least two required elements across two independent sources, align with the broader AML framework and requirements for low-risk and medium-risk cases, while being flexible.\n                \n                \n                \n                    \n                        1 — As defined in Part 1.2.1 of the AML/CTF Rules.\n                    \n                    \n                        2 — As defined in Part 1.2.1 of the AML/CTF Rules.\n                                        \n                                \n            \n          \n            \n                \n                    The main source of AML/CFT-related requirements for reporting entities in Belgium is the\n                    Law on the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing and on the restriction of the use of cash (the \"AML Law\"), last amended on\n                    February 8, 2023.\n                \n                Pursuant to Art. 27(1) of the AML Law, the reporting entities are required to verify the identity of the customers against:\n                \n                    \n                        \"1° one or more supporting documents or reliable and independent sources of information enabling them to confirm [the identification data listed in Art. 26 - for natural persons, this would include \"last name, first name, date and place\n                        of birth and, to the extent possible, address\". Regarding verification of address specifically, the National Bank of Belgium\n                        Object of the identification and identity verification guidance (\"NBB Guidance\") states that \"financial\n                        institutions' internal procedures should determine the measures to be taken to fulfill this legal obligation in a sufficiently precise manner\" without providing an exhaustive list of ways to do so];\n                    \n                    \n                        2° where applicable, the information obtained through electronic identification means such as those provided or recognised within the authentication service as referred to in Articles 9 and 10 of the Law of 18 July 2017 on electronic\n                        identification [implementing the eIDAS regulation and providing a regulatory framework for electronic identification in connection with digital public services in Belgium; the cited articles mostly refer to the data contained in the\n                        National Register], confirming the identity of persons online;\n                    \n                    3° where applicable, information obtained through relevant trust services referred to in Regulation 910/2014\".\n                \n                \n                    At the same time, Article 1 of Annex III to the AML Law defines non-face-to-face business relationships as a factor of potentially higher risk if conducted without certain safeguards, namely \"electronic means of identification or\n                    relevant trust services as defined in Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 or any other secure identification process that take place electronically or remotely and are regulated, recognised, approved or accepted by the relevant national\n                    authorities\".\n                \n                \n                    While there is no indication in the AML Law that alternative options (such as other external data sources or a combination of ID analysis and liveness / face match) are not permissible, the National Bank of Belgium (\"NBB\") states the\n                    following it its Guidance:\n                \n                \n                    \"[...] a simple copy or electronic image of a supporting document is insufficiently reliable in itself to be accepted as a supporting document in standard-risk situations without being verified through the\n                    National Register as stipulated in Article 28 of the Anti-Money Laundering Law\".1\n                \n                \n                    Still, this should not be read as a prohibition of any non-face-to-face onboarding mechanisms besides that stipulated in Art. 28 of the AML Law, since the NBB purposefully adopts a technologically neutral approach, emphasising that\n                    \"neither the Anti-Money Laundering Law nor the Anti-Money Laundering Regulation of the NBB lists in a precise, uniform and prescriptive manner the supporting documents or the reliable and independent sources of information that can be\n                    used to fulfil the obligation to verify the identity of the persons involved\", even though some of these sources are explicitly authorised. That said, the NBB strongly recommends regulated entities to: \n                \n                \n                \n                    implement different KYC flows\n                    depending on the customer's risk profile, including a \"correlation table of the supporting documents accepted for each risk class, as well as a list of the circumstances in which certain supporting documents need not be submitted\";\n                \n                \n                    when authorising the use of innovative technologies other than electronic identification means as referred to in the AML Law in high-risk situations, tighten the terms and conditions for the application of this authorisation and carry\n                    out a prior analysis of whether such technologies are reliable. The Guidance does, however, confirm that reliability is enhanced when \"electronic identification schemes notified in accordance with Article 9 of the eIDAS Regulation and\n                    meeting the requirements of \"substantial\" or \"high\" levels of assurance\". The two electronic identification schemes\n                    notified by Belgium, both with a \"high\" level of assurance, are Belgian eID Scheme FAS /\n                    eCards and Belgian eID Scheme FAS / Itsme. These should therefore be regarded as acceptable solutions for identity verification.\n                \n                \n                    consult certain official data sources in case verification is performed on the basis of documentation (e.g., FPS Home Affairs - when there is a suspicion the ID may be stolen or lost; the National Register - while processing the data\n                    registered on the microprocessor of the ID; etc.);\n                \n                when relying on a photocopy or electronic image of a supporting document, incorporate multiple checks (that the data has not been altered or manipulated, that the necessary security features are present, etc.).\n                \n                \n                \n                    Overall, reporting entities have relatively broad discretion in choosing the means of remote identity verification, as long as they are able to justify their sufficiency and compatibility with the customer's risk profile. However,\n                    solutions explicitly approved under the AML Act or the NBB Guidance (including, in particular, eCards and Itsme eID scheme, or any services leveraging data from the National Register) are more likely to be considered compliant.\n                \n                \n                \n                    \n                        1 — \"Upon request from an obliged entity,and solely for the purposes of the verification, by such an entity, of the identity of the customers and their agents who are natural persons and who are not present during their identification [...]\n                        the professional associations designated by the King shall be authorised to:\n                    \n                    \n                        1° use the identification number from the National Register;\n                        2° access the data of the National Register of natural persons referred to in Article 3 of the Law of 8 August 1983 establishing a National Register of natural persons;\n                        3° make a paper or electronic copy of the information consulted in said Register\".\n                    \n                                \n            \n\n            \n                \n                    The Financial Intelligence Act of 2022 (the \"FI Act\") provides a comprehensive legal basis for AML/CFT efforts in Botswana. The FI\n                    Act, along with the Financial Intelligence Regulations 2022 (the \"FI Regulations\"), outlines the obligations for\n                    accountable institutions.\n                \n                Article 20(1) of the FI Act sets out the general identification and identity verification requirement, with no detailed clarifications:\n                \n                    \n                        \"A specified party shall, where required to conduct customer due diligence in terms of section 16 and before establishing a business relationship or carrying out a transaction - (a) establish and verify the identity of a customer,\n                        unless the identity of that customer is known and has been verified by the specified party\".\n                    \n                \n                Nevertheless, Article 20(6) also emphasizes that identity verification should be conducted based on an official document:\n                \"Proof of identity of a customer under this section shall be through -\n                    \n                        (a) production of a National Identity Card for citizens;\n                        (b) production of a passport for non-citizens;\n                        (c) production of a refugee identity card issued under the Refugees (Recognition and Control) Act; [...]; or\n                        (f) such other identity document as the Minister may prescribe\".\n                    \n                This is further clarified in the FI Regulations:\n                \n                    \"14. (1) Any information or particulars ascertained by a specified party as required under Part II of these Regulations shall, be verified by the specified party by comparing such information obtained with the applicable and\n                    corresponding independent and reliable information set out in the following documentation -\n                \n                    \n                        (a) a trust instrument or deed of trust;(b) a national identification document issued by the person's country of origin, domicile or citizenship;\n                        (c) a passport;\n                        (d) a refugee identity card;\n                        (e) a birth certificate; ... or\n                        (h) any reliable document, data or information that reasonably serves to verify any of the information obtained by the specified party in ascertaining the information set out in Part Il of these Regulations.\n                    \n                \n                    (2) If it is deemed to be reasonably necessary, taking into account any guidance notes concerning the verification of identity that may apply to a specified party,the specified party shall, in addition to the verification undertaken in\n                    terms of subregulation (1), verify any of the information or particulars ascertained as part of establishing identity by comparing such particulars with any applicable and corresponding reliable document, data or information.\n                    \"\n                \n                \n                    Based on the above-cited provisions, taken cumulatively, the expectation appears to be that an individual customer's identity document needs to be processed as part of the KYC procedure unless there is a compelling reason for the\n                    obliged entity's inability to obtain it. However, there is no explicit requirement that the document must be collected from the customer directly.\n                \n                Meanwhile, the following data is subject to ascertainment in relation to individual customers pursuant to Part 2, Section 6(1) of the FI Regulations:\n                \n                    \"(a) the person's full name;\n                    (b) the person's nationality;\n                    (c) where the person is a citizen or resident of Botswana, the identity cardnumber and date of birth of such person;\n                    (d) where the person is not citizen or resident of Botswana, the passportnumber and date of birth of such person;\n                    (e) where the person is a refugee, a refugee identity card number and dateof birth of such person;\n                    (f) where the person is a citizen or resident of Botswana, the person'sresidential address in Botswana;\n                    (g) where the person is not a citizen or resident of Botswana, the residentialaddress in his or her country of domicile and physical address inBotswana;\n                    (h) the person's contact details;\n                    (i) the person's occupation or source of income;\n                    (j) nature and location of business activities, if any;\n                    (k) the source of funds involved in the transaction; and\n                    (l) an original of the recent council rate or utility bill receipt\".\n                \n                Furthermore, Section 15 of the FI Regulations provides additional requirements for non-face-to-face customer onboarding:\n                \n                    \"(1) Where a specified party ascertained information, in terms of these Regulations, about a customer without contact in person, with the natural person or with the representative of the customer, the specified party\n                    shall take reasonable steps to ensure the existence and to establish the identity of that customer, taking into account any guidance notes concerning the verification of identities that may apply to that specified party.\n                \n                (2) Where the customer referred to under subregulation (1) is a natural person, the specified party shall ensure the existence and to establish the identity of that customer by -\n                    \n                        (a) obtaining a reference from a well known professional, an employer of the customer of the specified party, or a known customer of the specified party who knows the natural person; or\n                        (b) requesting original recent council rates or utility bill receipt.\"\n                    \n                \n                    To conclude, the involvement of a digital source as the primary KYC method could be used for identity verification, provided that a copy of the customer's ID can be extracted from the source in question. However, additional documents\n                    would in any event be required for address verification (council rate or utility bill receipt specifically).\n                                \n            \n\n            \n                \n                    Chapter IV, Article 10 of the national AML Law N° 9.613 (\"Law\"), establishes the obligation for entities that fall under the regulation of the Council for the Control of Financial Activities Office (Conselho de Controle de Atividades Financeiras - COAF), \n                    including banks, financial institutions, virtual currency exchange platforms, insurance companies, casinos, card issuers, leasing companies, real estate companies, and companies that trade luxury goods, to “identify their clients and maintain an updated register, \n                    in accordance with instructions issued by the competent authorities” (Law, Art. 10, Par. I). In principle, this clause indicates that the specifics regarding customer due diligence operations are laid out by each relevant regulatory agency for the entities within its supervisory scope, \n                    rather than the general law.\n                                \n                \n                    In turn, the Securities and Exchange Commission of Brazil (Comissão de Valores Mobiliários - CVM), which oversees all financial institutions that engage with operations relating to securities, stocks, commodities, and futures exchanges, \n                    such as brokers and investment funds, provides the applicable guidelines for customer onboarding in the 2021 CVM Resolução 50 (“Resolution”): \n                \n                    \n                        \n                        \"The adoption of alternative registration systems is allowed, including by electronic means, provided that the solutions adopted meet the objectives of the current regulations and the procedures are subject to verification.\n                        [...]\n                        The customer records information related to customers classified in subsections II to V of article 1 of Annex B shall cover natural persons authorized to represent them, all their direct and indirect controllers, and natural persons \n                        who have significant influence over them until reaching the natural person characterized as the beneficial owner or any of the entities mentioned in paragraph 2” (Resolution, Art. 12-13).\n                        \n                    \n                \n                As per Annex B to the Resolution, at least the following data must by default be present in an individual customer’s records:\n                    \n                        \"a. full name;\n                        b. date of birth;\n                        c. birthplace;\n                        d. nationality;\n                        e. marital status;\n                        f. mother's name;\n                        g. identification document number and issuing body;\n                        h. registration number in the Registry of Natural Persons – (\"Cadastro de Pessoas Físicas\", CPF/MF);\n                        i. name and respective CPF/MF number of the spouse or partner, if applicable;\n                        j. place of residence (street, complement, district, city, federation unit, and ZIP code) and telephone number;\n                        k. email address for correspondence;\n                        l. professional occupation;\n                        m. name of the entity, with the respective customer records with the CNPJ, for which he/she works, when applicable;\n                        n. updated information on earnings and equity status;\n                        [...]\n                        v. whether the customer is considered a person politically exposed under this Resolution;\n                        w. copy of the following documents:\n                            \n                                    1. identification document; and\n                                    2. proof of residence or domicile.\n                            \n                        \n                    \n\n                \n                    In the case of institutions regulated by the Central Bank of Brazil (Banco Central do Brasil – BACEN), including banks, payment services providers, and exchange platforms, \n                    the following rules for customer identification apply:\n                     \n                        \n                            \"The institutions shall adopt identification procedures that allow verifying and validating the identity of the client. \n                            The procedures shall include obtaining, verifying and validating the authenticity of customer identification information, including, \n                            if necessary, by comparing this information with those available in public and private databases\" (BACEN/DC Circular No. 3978 OF 01/23/2020 (“Circular”), Art. 16(1)). \n                        \n                            \"In the customer identification process, at least the following information must be collected:\n                            \n                                I - the full name and taxpayer identification number (CPF), in the case of a natural person;\" (Circular, Art. 16(2)).\n                            \n                        \n                    \n                \n                \n                        Additionally, customer identity verification should also include a process of qualification that would be \"compatible with the client's risk profile and with the nature of the business relationship.\"\n                        Specifically, this requires:\n                        \n                            \"I – identification of the place of residence, in the case of a natural person;\n                            [...]\n                            III – assessment of the client's financial capacity, including income, in the case of a natural person, or revenue, in the case of a legal entity” (Circular, Art. 18(1). \n                        \n                   \n                \n                    Lastly, in the Ordinance Nº 1.231 (“Ordinance”), the Secretariat of Prizes and Bets (Secretaria de Prêmios e Apostas - SPA) establishes the customer identification guidelines for authorized betting operators. \n                    Article 31 of the regulation requires the collection of the following information from all players:\n                     \n                        \"I – full name;\n                        II – nationality;\n                        III – Individual Taxpayer Registration Number – CPF;\n                        IV – date of birth;\n                        V – identification of the bettor's gender;\n                        VI – complete address, which cannot be a PO Box;\n                        VII – country of domicile;\n                        VIII – telephone number;\n                        IX – email;\n                        X – data of the deposit or prepaid payment accounts registered;\n                        XI – prudential betting limit per time elapsed, linking to daily, weekly, monthly or other periods;\n                        XII – prudential limit of betting for financial loss, linked to daily, weekly, monthly or other periods;\n                        XIII – IP address registered at the time of registration; and\n                        XIV – scanned copy of a valid identification document with photo\" (Ordinance, Art. 31).\n                    \n                \n                \n                     Furthermore, Paragraph 2 of the same article mandates the acceptance of only the following identity document type copies for identification purposes:\n                     \n                            \"I - National Identity Card;\n                            II - General Registry - RG;\n                            III - National Driver's License - CNH; or\n                            IV - Passport\"(Ordinance, Art. 31, Par. 2).\n                    \n                \n                \n                     Paragraphs 3 and 5 then specifies the possible methods for user data ownership and real-time presence validation \n                     as well as additional tools that should be applied to ensure the legitimacy of the individual’s identity:\n\n                     \n                        \"I - facial recognition, with proof of life; and\n                        II - alphanumeric password with special characters (Ordinance, Art. 31, Par. 2).\"\n                        [...]\n                        “To verify the validity of the bettor's identity, confirmation must be made through the communication channels provided in the user's registration, such as:\n                        \n                            I. email;\n                            II. Short Message Service (SMS); or\n                            III. messaging apps” (Ordinance, Art. 31, Par. 3, 5).\n                        \n                    \n                \n                \n                    It is also important to note that all three aforementioned regulations require obligated entities to collect and verify an official travel document \n                    in cases where an individual has not obtained a Taxpayer identification number from the Natural Persons Register (Cadastro de Pessoas Físicas – CPF).\n                \n                \n                     Having assessed the relevant legal standards applicable to both financial institutions and betting companies operating in the Brazilian jurisdiction, \n                     it can be concluded that varying electronic identification methods for customer onboarding are permissible, as long as they are able to: \n                     \n                        1. retrieve a copy of an accepted identity document type;\n                        2. establish the legitimate ownership of the identity document; and\n                        3. validate the real-time identification presence of the end user. \n                    \n                \n                \n                    While the guidelines by the Central Bank of Brazil seem to suggest a more flexible approach towards end user verification, \n                    it is ultimately the responsibility of the regulated entity to assess the risk level of their operations as well as each individual customer. \n                    As a result, financial institutions might be expected to adopt such methods and tools that adhere to the common identification practices in the local market.\n                                       \n            \n\n            \n                According to section 6.1 of Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act (the “AML/CFT Act”) and sections 86-88 of the Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Regulations (the “AML/CFT Regulations”), a financial entity shall verify the person for whom it opens an account. Sections 12-14 of the AML/CFT Regulations specify the following scope of data shall be kept in respect of an account holder:\n                \n                 Name\n                 Address\n                 Date of birth\n                 Nature of the occupation.\n                \n                In addition, section 9.3 of the AML/CFT Act mandates that the financial entity shall determine whether it deals with a politically exposed person (the “PEP”).\n                AML/CFT Regulations provide for both documentary and non-documentary methods for identity verification.\n                Even though paragraph 105 of the AML/CFT Regulations lists several acceptable verification methods that seem to be non-documentary (e.g., referring to information from a federal or provincial government body that is authorized in Canada to verify the identity of persons; referring to information from reliable sources), at the moment, FINTRAC Guidance on the methods to verify the identity of persons and entities (the “FINTRAC Guidance”) mentions the only fully non-documentary verification method available, which is the credit file method. FINTRAC Guidance does not address at all the method of referring to information from a federal or provincial government body that is authorized to verify the identity of persons, so, we understand that there is currently no such process available in Canada. As to referring to information from reliable sources, FINTRAC Guidance addresses it as an acceptable identity verification method that, given the examples of such reliable sources provided for in the said guidance, seem to rely mostly on documentary sources. However, if a credit file is used for a dual-process method, under certain conditions this method could be used as a non-documentary option (for more information, please, refer to the section “Dual-Process Method” below).\n                Credit File Method\n                The credit file method is to conduct the search of the verified person’s credit file at the time the financial entity is verifying such a person’s identity and to confirm that the individual’s name, address, and date of birth match with the credit file data. When getting a credit file, the financial entity may request it without including a credit assessment part, as this information is not required for the purpose of verifying the person’s identity. This method may be implemented via an automated matching. It is also acceptable to refer to a third-party vendor authorized by a Canadian credit bureau.\n                For this method to be valid, the credit file shall meet all of the following requirements:\n                \n                 It is located in Canada (i.e., it is issued by a Canadian credit bureau).\n                 It has been in existence for at least three years.\n                 It contains information that is derived from more than one source (i.e. more than one tradeline).\n                 It matches the name, address and date of birth of the person being identified.\n                \n                If there is a minor discrepancy in the name or address (e.g., a typo or a secondary address), the reporting entity may still determine that the information matches. However, if there is a mismatch in the date of birth, it is more likely that the reporting entity will determine that the information does not match. In this case,  an alternative identity verification method or a different credit file will be used. The process and any remediation steps must be documented in the organization’s compliance policies and procedures.\n                Dual-Process Method\n                The dual-process method is to perform any two of the following actions:\n                \n                 Refer to the information from a reliable source that confirms the person’s name and address\n                 Refer to the information from a reliable source that confirms the person’s name and date of birth\n                 Refer to the information that confirms the person’s name and an account with a financial entity (e.g., deposit, prepaid, credit card, or loan account).\n                \n                Annex 5 of the FINTRAC Guidance provides examples of sources that can be used for the dual-process method: mostly, various types of documents (not necessarily an original version of a document, but, for example, its photocopy, scan, or electronic image will work). The only electronic source that is listed is a “Canadian credit file that has been in existence for at least six months”. \n                If a Canadian credit bureau acts as an aggregator and it can provide the financial entity with the information from at least two independent original sources (where each source will confirm one of the two types of information that shall be confirmed under the dual-process method), this will be considered as sufficient confirmation for the dual-process method.  However, if there is only one source on basis of which data the Canadian credit bureau issues the credit file or if the credit file does not comply with the requirements (e.g., it exists for less than six months), the sole utilization of the credit file will not suffice as the dual-process method is meant to use two different sources (i.e. the verified information must not be from, or derived from, the same source). In this case, the other source shall be used that will imply getting certain documents from the individual whose identity is being verified.\n                Similarly to the credit file method, if there is a minor typo in the name or address, the reporting entity may still determine that the information matches. However, if there is a mismatch in the date of birth, it is more likely to determine that the information does not match. In this case,  information from a different source under the dual-process method or an alternative identity verification method will be used.\n                To conclude, it may be possible to onboard individual customers using a non-documentary identity verification procedure under both the credit file and dual-process methods, provided the regulatory criteria referred above are satisfied. Otherwise, it will be needed to use a document-based method.\n\n            \n\n            \n                \n                    In Cyprus, the legal framework governing Anti-Money Laundering ('AML') and Combating the Financing of Terrorism ('CFT') is primarily set out by the\n                    Prevention and Suppression of Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing Laws of 2007,\n                    as subsequently amended (referred to as the 'AML/CFT Law'). Besides the stipulated obligations and requirements aimed\n                    at securing the financial environment from illicit activities, this law also outlines the key requirements for Customer Due Diligence ('CDD') and Know Your Customer ('KYC') procedures in Cyprus.\n                \n                The implementation, enforcement and the adoption of the various domestic and international AML/CFT legislative instruments are overseen by the local Regulatory Bodies, such as:\n                \n                    \n                        Central Bank of Cyprus ('CBC'): The country's central monetary authority, responsible for the enforcement of the provisions of the legislation, regulations and supervision of banks, Electronic Money Institutions\n                        (EMIs), and Payment Service Providers (PSPs), Bureaux de Change and Credit Institutions, under section 59 (1)(a) of the AML/CFT Law.\n                    \n                    \n                        Cyprus Securities and Exchange Commission ('CySEC'): It is a regulatory body that regulates Cyprus's financial services sector, overseeing entities like investment firms, financial institutions, and investment\n                        funds.\n                    \n                    Cyprus Bar Association ('CyBAR'): It oversees lawyers and law firms in Cyprus, ensuring compliance with AML and CTF regulations as designated non-financial businesses and professions (DNFBPs)\n                    \n                        Institute of Certified Public Accountants of Cyprus ('ICPAC'): It is the competent authority responsible for the regulation and supervision of certified public accountants and audit firms within the Republic of\n                        Cyprus.\n                    \n                    Cyprus Real Estate Agents ('CREAA): It oversees real estate agents in Cyprus, ensuring their compliance with AML and CTF regulations.\n                    Other relevant entities.\n                \n                However, this assessment is largely based on the requirements of CySEC and CBC.\n                Cyprus Securities and Exchange Commission (CySEC) CDD provisions\n                In 2016 the Directive DI144-2007-08 (as amended), Section 2 vi, specifically mentions the use of electronic verification and permits its use as long as the following conditions are met:\n                \n                    \n                        i. the electronic databases kept by the third party or to which the third party or the Financial Organization has access are registered to and/or approved by the Data Protection Commissioner in order to safeguard personal data (or\n                        the corresponding competent authority in the country the said databases are kept).\n                    \n                    \n                        ii. electronic databases provide access to information referred to both present and past situations showing that the person really exists and providing both positive information (at least the customer's full name, address and date\n                        of birth) and negative information (e.g. committing of offences such as identity theft, inclusion in deceased persons records, inclusion in sanctions and restrictive measures' list by the Council of the European Union and the UN\n                        Security Council).\n                    \n                    iii. electronic databases include a wide range of sources with information from different time periods with real-time update and trigger alerts when important data alter.\n                    \n                        iv. transparent procedures have been established allowing the Financial Organization to know which information was searched, the result of such search and its significance in relation to the level of assurance as to the customer's\n                        identity verification.\n                    \n                    v. procedures have been established allowing the Financial Organization to record and save the information used and the result in relation to identity verification.\n                \n                In addition, according to the above-mentioned directive, Section 2.3 information must come from two sources in the following manner:\n                \n                    vi. identification of the customer's full
