This should be done using documents, data or information from a reliable and independent source or any other source to verify the identity as\n                    follows:\n                \n                \n                \n                    \n                        For Natural Persons:The name, as in the identification card or travel document, nationality, address, place of birth, name and address of employer, attaching a copy of the original and valid identification card or travel document,\n                        and obtain approval from the senior management, if the Customer or the Beneficial Owner is a PEP\".\n                    \n                \n                \n                    In general, under Article 8.1 of the AML-CFT Decision and section 6.3.1 of the AML Guidelines, required identity attributes for CDD under UAE regulations and guidance include, for a natural person, the name (as in the passport or\n                    identity card, number, country of issuance, date of issuance and expiration date of the identity card or passport), the nationality, the address (i.e., the permanent residential address), the date and place of birth, and the name and\n                    address of employer (if applicable).\n                \n                Reinforcing this, Section 6.3.1 of the AML Guidelines further elaborates on the necessity to collect copies of identity documents:\n                \n                    \"The verification of a customer's identity, including their address, should be based on original, official (i.e. government-issued) documents whenever possible.1 When that is not possible, FIs should augment the number of verifying\n                    documents or the amount of information they obtain from different independent sources. In particular, when verifying the UAE ID card, FIs licensed by the Central Bank must use the online validation gateway of the Federal Authority for\n                    Identity & Citizenship and keep a copy of the UAE ID and its digital verification.2 They should also identify the lack of official documents and the use of alternative means of verification as risk factors when assessing the customer's\n                    ML/FT risk classification\".\n                \n                \n                    At the same time, both Section 6.3.1 of the AML Guidelines and Section 3.1 of the\n                    Guidance for Licensed Financial Institutions ('LFI's) on Digital Identification for Customer Due Diligence\n                    (the \"Digital Identification Guidance\") seem to suggest that verification via electronic sources is an acceptable alternative to the documentary method:\n                \n                \n                    \"An example of alternative verification means is verification by way of digital identification systems. Such digital identification systems should rely upon technology, adequate governance, processes and procedures that provide\n                    appropriate levels of confidence that the system produces accurate results\";\n                \n                \n                    \"Under Article 8 of the AML-CFT Decision, LFIs are required to identify each customer and verify the customer's identity using documents, data, or any other identification information from a reliable and independent source. This\n                    requirement is technology neutral and expressly permits LFIs to use documentary as well as non-documentary sources (i.e., information or data) when performing identification and verification; it does not impose any restrictions on the\n                    form-physical or digital-that identity evidence must take, nor does it impose limitations as to the use of digital ID systems for the purpose of linking a customer's verified identity to a unique, real-life individual, provided this is\n                    done using a \"reliable\" and \"independent\" source. As such, LFIs are permitted to utilize digital ID systems as well as physical forms to perform customer identification and verification, consistent with the expectations set forth in\n                    this Guidance\".\n                \n                Section 5 of the Guidance further prescribes the mandatory assessments the FIs should conduct before choosing a digital identification system:\n                \"An assurance level assessment, through which the LFI can understand the assurance levels that the digital ID system provides based on its technology, architecture, and governance and determine its reliability and independence; and\n                \n                    An appropriateness assessment, through which the LFI can make a risk-based determination - given the digital ID system's assurance levels - of whether the digital ID system is appropriately reliable and independent for CDD in light of\n                    potential ML, TF, fraud, and other illicit financing risks\".\n                \n                \n                    Section 2.1 of the Guidance describes several national identification systems approved for use by AML-regulated entities, including UAE Pass, Emirates ID and Emirates Facial Recognition. UAE Pass, in particular, is the UAE's primary\n                    digital identity and signature solution with a high level of security.\n                \n                \n                    The interpretation of the above-mentioned provisions, taken cumulatively, appears to be that, while usage of digital identification systems is in principle permitted for KYC purposes, it does not negate the overall document-based\n                    approach adopted by the UAE financial regulators and, in particular, the requirement to obtain a copy of the customer's identity document under the AML-CFT Decision. Accordingly, digital ID systems may be relied on as a standalone\n                    solution when they allow access to all of the required customer data, including that related to the identity document and a copy of the identity document itself. Alternatively, they may be used for supplementary checks (which are\n                    sometimes mandatory, as in the case of Emirates ID).\n                      \n                \n                \n                    \n                        1 — For address verification, this could imply that geolocation detection alone would not be adequate; this is supported by the Digital Identification Guidance, referring to geolocation / IP address data mostly as supporting identity attributes to leverage for ongoing due diligence and transaction monitoring (see, e.g., Section 3.2). However, since there is no exhaustive list of documentation that can serve as proof of address, arguably records obtained from a reliable external database could suffice.\n                    \n                    \n                        2 — Section 2.2 of the Digital Identification Guidance also states that, “when verifying the Emirates ID card, either physically or by way of digital or electronic “Know Your Customer (“e-KYC”) solutions, LFIs should use the online validation gateway of the Federal Authority for Identity, Citizenship, Customs & Port Security, the UAE Pass Application, or other UAE Government-supported solutions, and keep a copy of the Emirates ID and its digital verification record”.\n                    \n                                      \n            \n\n            \n                \n                    The core legal sources of AML-related obligations in the UK, the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 and the\n                    Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 (\"MLR\"), do not specify the exact KYC\n                    procedures that may or should be implemented by regulated entities, granting them a broad margin of discretion. The MLR mostly set out the general criteria that identity verification processes must conform to; for example, paras. 18-19\n                    of Art. 27 provide the following guidance:\n                \n                \"(18) For the purposes of this regulation -\n                    \n                        (a) &lt;...&gt; \"verify\" means verify on the basis of documents or information in either case obtained from a reliable source which is independent of the person whose identity is being verified;\n                        (b) documents issued or made available by an official body are to be regarded as being independent of a person even if they are provided or made available to the relevant person by or on behalf of that person.\n                    \n                (19) For the purposes of this regulation, information may be regarded as obtained from a reliable source which is independent of the person whose identity is being verified where-\n                    \n                        (a) it is obtained by means of an electronic identification process &lt;...&gt;; and\n                        \n                            (b) that process is secure from fraud and misuse and capable of providing assurance that the person claiming a particular identity is in fact the person with that identity, to a degree that is necessary\n                            for effectively managing and mitigating any risks of money laundering and terrorist financing\".\n                        \n                    \n                \n                    The Financial Conduct Authority also makes references to electronic verification through its Rulebook, FCG 3.2.4, reiterating that \"an electronic identification\n                    process may be regarded as a reliable source for the purposes of CDD verification where that process is independent of the person whose identity is being verified, secure from fraud and misuse and capable of providing an appropriate\n                    level of assurance that the person claiming a particular identity is in fact that person with that identity\".\n                \n                \n                    Furthermore, the Guidance by the Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG), Part I (\"Guidance\"), which is widely regarded to establish the industry\n                    standards for compliance with AML/CFT requirements, confirms that non-documentary checks (in particular, those involving external databases) are permissible as the primary KYC measure, provided that at least the following identifying\n                    data is collected in respect of individual customers:\n                \n                \n                    full name;\n                    residential address;\n                    date of birth (para. 5.3.71).\n                \n                When opting for electronic verification, however, obliged entities are instructed to:\n                \n                    \n                        choose multiple data sources (or one single source where it \"has been issued by a government authority and contains cryptographic security features\") or to \"incorporate qualitative checks that assess the strength of the information\n                        supplied\" (para. 5.3.50);\n                    \n                    \n                        \"demonstrate that they have both verified that the customer exists, and satisfied themselves that the individual seeking the business relationship is, in fact, that customer\" (paras. 5.3.44, 5.3.79). To fulfill this requirement, the\n                        Guidance recommends various methods, such as the use of biometric information or private codes that \"incontrovertibly link the potential customer &lt;...&gt; to the electronic/digital identity information\" (para. 5.3.44);\n                    \n                    \n                        \"if suspicions are raised in relation to the integrity of any electronic information obtained, [...] take whatever practical and proportionate steps are available to establish whether these suspicions are substantiated, and if so,\n                        whether the relevant source should be used\" (para 5.3.45);\n                    \n                    \n                        when choosing the data providers, assess whether they are sufficiently robust, reliable, and accurate (e.g., if they are accredited for KYC purposes through a governmental or industry process; use both positive and negative\n                        information sources; maintain appropriate data retention procedures; etc.) (paras. 5.3.52-5.3.53).\n                    \n                \n                \n                    In relation to proof of address specifically, para. 5.3.29 of the Guidance emphasizes that \"knowledge of an individual's residential address is central to being reasonably satisfied that the customer is who they say they are\". However,\n                    no particular method of verifying address is explicitly promoted. Furthermore, para. 5.3.112 states that address does not even necessarily have to be verified in all cases (e.g., it may be omitted when the customer lacks a permanent\n                    place of residence); this is a matter within obliged entities' discretion. At the same time, as per para. 5.3.80, address - like any other identity attributes - may be confirmed via electronic checks. This may include, e.g., external\n                    databases maintained by private or government entities and, arguably, geolocation data (where the identity in general is verified via more robust sources and/or the customer's risk profile is low).\n                \n                \n                    It follows that, under the UK AML regulations, non-doc identity and address verification solutions may be relied upon as long as i) the solution is able to link the user to their claimed identity that has been confirmed as existent by\n                    an independent external data source and ii) additional security measures allowing to link the user to the claimed identity are involved.\n                                \n            \n\n            \n                \n                    The Bank Secrecy Act1 (BSA) imposes general AML obligations on financial institutions and other reporting entities and broadly outlines the customer due diligence obligation. \n                    In particular, 31 U.S.C. 5318(h)(1) prescribes that each financial institution shall establish anti-money laundering and countering the financing of terrorism programs. \n                    Furthermore, according to 31 U.S.C. 5318(l)(1), the regulations setting forth the minimum standards for financial institutions and their customers regarding the identity of the customer \n                    that shall apply in connection with the opening of an account at a financial institution shall be adopted.\n                \n                \n                    In order to specify the practical implementation of the BSA requirements, the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) developed its regulations2, \n                    which are incorporated as the 31 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) Chapter X. Section 31 CFR 1020.220(a)(2)(i)(A) lists the data to be collected in respect of every individual client3. \n                    At the same time, the first paragraph of section 31 CFR 1020.220(a)(2) states that regulated entities should adopt a risk-based approach when choosing a verification method for their customers and \n                    that different options (as well as their combinations) may be accepted so long as the chosen procedures \"enable the [obliged entity] to form a reasonable belief that it knows the true identity of each customer\".\n                \n                \n                Specifically, section 31 CFR 1020.220(a)(2)(ii)(B)(2), highlights the possibility for regulated institutions to use \"non-documentary procedures\", subject to the financial institution implementing additional safeguards to mitigate the ensuing risks, where: \n                \n                    an individual is unable to present an unexpired government-issued identification document that bears a photograph or similar safeguard;\n                    the institution is not familiar with the documents presented; the account is opened without obtaining documents;\n                    the customer opens the account without appearing in person at the institution; and\n                    where the institution is otherwise presented with circumstances that increase the risk that the institution will be unable to verify the true identity of a customer through documents.\n                \n                \n                    Several examples of non-documentary KYC processes are also given for reference in 31 CFR 1020.220(a)(2)(ii)(B)(1), \n                    such as \"contacting a customer; independently verifying the customer's identity through the comparison of information provided by the customer \n                    with information obtained from a consumer reporting agency, public database, or other source; checking references with other financial institutions; and obtaining a financial statement\".\n                \n                \n                    This approach is further confirmed in various explanatory or interpretative materials by the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN), for example,\n                    Guidance FIN-2018-G001:\n                \n                \n                    \"A financial institution's CIP must contain procedures for verifying customer identification, including describing when the institution will use documentary, non-documentary, or a combination of both methods for identity verification\";\n                \n                \n                    \"Non-documentary methods of verification may include contacting a beneficial owner; independently verifying the beneficial owner's identity through the comparison of information provided by the legal entity customer (or\n                    the beneficial owner, as appropriate) with information obtained from other sources; checking references with other financial institutions; and obtaining a financial statement\";\n                \n                \n                    \"&lt;...&gt; covered financial institutions may verify the identity of a beneficial owner who does not appear in person, through a photocopy or other reproduction of a valid identity document, or\n                    by non-documentary means &lt;...&gt;\".\n                \n                \n                    Accordingly, the US AML regulations allow, in principle, non-documentary KYC methods within the risk-based approach. However, the obliged entity must be assured it knows the true identity of its customer, for which purpose additional\n                    KYC mechanisms aimed at connecting the user and the identity in question must be implemented.\n                \n                \n                    No specific procedures are prescribed for address verification; since information sources that could potentially be used for non-documentary checks are not restricted, both external databases and geolocation data (as well as other\n                    sources) could be suitable for this purpose. Notably, FinCEN has repeatedly encouraged IP address detection as an additional security measure to be\n                    incorporated into the KYC process.\n                \n                \n                \n                    \n                        1 — The Currency and Foreign Transactions Reporting Act of 1970, its amendments, and the other statutes relating to the subject matter of that Act,\n                        as codified at 12 U.S.C. 1829b, 12 U.S.C. 1951-1960,\n                        31 U.S.C. 5311-5314, 5316-5336.\n                    \n                    \n                        2 —  31 CFR Chapter X.\n                    \n                    3 —\n                    \n                        Name;\n                        Date of birth, for an individual;\n                        \n                            Address, which shall be:\n                            \n                                (i) For an individual, a residential or business street address;\n                                \n                                    (ii) For an individual who does not have a residential or business street address, an Army Post Office (APO) or Fleet Post Office (FPO) box number, or the residential or business street address of next of kin or of another\n                                    contact individual [...]; and\n                                \n                            \n                        \n                        \n                            Identification number, which shall be:\n                            \n                                (i) For a U.S. person, a taxpayer identification number; or\n                                \n                                    (ii) For a non-U.S. person, one or more of the following: A taxpayer identification number; passport number and country of issuance; alien identification card number; or number and country of issuance of any other\n                                    government-issued document evidencing nationality or residence and bearing a photograph or similar safeguard.\n                                \n                            \n                        \n                    \n                                          \n            \n\n            \n                \n                    The 2017 Resolution of Board of Central Bank of the Republic of Uzbekistan (as amended) (the \"CBU Resolution\") outlines a comprehensive approach to customer identity\n                    verification, emphasizing both document-based and electronic methods:\n                \n                \n                    \"Identification of an individual client by a commercial bank is carried out on the basis of an identitydocument (passport or ID card or a document replacing them) or biometric data. In\n                    this case, a commercial bank, when identifying an individual client:\n                \n                \n                    on the basis of an identity document (passport or ID card or a document replacing them) - must familiarize itself with the original of such document;\n                    on the basis of biometric data - must verify such data via the information system of the Ministry of Internal Affairs of the Republic of Uzbekistan\" (clause 26).\n                \n                Regarding the scope of data by which an individual customer must be identified, clause 25 of the CBU Resolution refers to Appendix 1 thereto, which, in turn, contains the following list:\n                \n                    Surname, first name and patronymic\n                    Date and place of birth\n                    Citizenship\n                    Place of permanent and (or) temporary residence\n                    Details of the passport or ID card or the document replacing them: series and number of the document, date of issue of the document, name of the authority that issued the document\n                    Personal identification number\n                    Home telephone number (if available).\n                \n                \n                    In parallel, the 2021 Central Bank Decision \"About the Approval of the Regulation on the Procedure for Digital Identification of Customers\" authorizes (i) digital identification with human\n                    interaction and (ii) digital authentication without human interaction via information systems for banks, microfinance organizations, pawn shops and payment organizations in relation to citizens of Uzbekistan, foreign citizens and\n                    stateless persons residing permanently or temporarily in Uzbekistan:\n                \n                (i) the procedure for digital identification is as follows (section 6):\n                \n                    \"the obliged entity receives from the customer photos of the parts of their identity document (biometric passport or ID card or driver's license of a new model) containing the relevant information;\n                    the obliged entity receives the customer's photo and (or) video1;\n                    the information obtained, including the photo and (or) video of the customer, is compared with that stored in the \"Electronic Government\" system (\"central database\");\n                    the obliged entity verifies the customer's mobile phone number (e.g., by sending an SMS message);\n                    the obliged entity checks whether the customer's risk profile is high (which would make them ineligible for the procedure);\n                    the obliged entity establishes an online video conference with the customer and checks that the submitted documents belong to them.\"\n                \n                (ii) the procedure for digital authentication is as follows (section 7):\n                \n                    \n                        \"the obliged entity receives from the customer the series and number of their identity document (biometric passport or ID card or driver's license or a new model), or personal identification number and date of birth, or all of these\n                        data, together with a photo or video of the customer taken in real time;\n                    \n                    \n                        the obliged entity sends a request to the central database and receives the following personal data of the customer:\n                        \n                            digital photograph (if available);\n                            personal identification number (\"ЖШШИР\");\n                            date of issue of biometric passport or ID card, its validity period and place of issue;\n                            surname, first name, patronymic in the state language (in Latin script);\n                            information about gender, country of birth, place of birth, nationality, citizenship and place of permanent or temporary residence;\n                        \n                    \n                    the obliged entity compares the customer's photo or a snapshot from the video taken in real time with the image extracted from the central database (if available) in an automated manner (without human involvement);\n                    the obliged entity verifies the customer's mobile phone number (e.g., by sending an SMS message);\n                    \n                        the obliged entity compares the received data with the List [the list of persons participating or suspected of participating in organized terrorist activities or proliferation of weapons of mass destruction, prepared by the\n                        Department for Combating Economic Crimes under the General Prosecutor's Office of the Republic of Uzbekistan] automatically (without human involvement)\".\n                    \n                \n                \n                    Therefore, the AML/CFT regulations of Uzbekistan currently provide for two options of fully non-documentary identity verification: (i) via the Ministry of Internal Affairs databases (the customer's biometric data being the input); and\n                    (ii) via the Electronic Government database (the customer's real-time photo / video, as well as certain non-biometric personal data (ID details or personal identification number and date of birth), being the input), subject to several\n                    procedural requirements, such as impersonation risk mitigation, obligatory consultation of specific AML screening sources, mobile phone verification, collection of all necessary attributes, etc.\n                   \n                \n                \n                    \n                        1 — As per section 10, in case of both digital identification and digital authentication, the photo / video: needs to be in color; the video must have sound; it is not allowed to have persons other than the customer in the photo and (or) video; the matching mechanism must allow for impersonation risk mitigation; etc.\n                    \n                                         \n            \n\n        \n    \n\n\n\n    \n    Score 2\n    \n        \n            Country:\n            \n                🇧🇷 Brazil\n                🇨🇴 Colombia\n            \n\n           \n                \n                    Chapter IV, Article 10 of the national AML Law N° 9.613 (\"Law\"), establishes the obligation for entities that fall under the regulation of the Council for the Control of Financial Activities Office (Conselho de Controle de Atividades Financeiras - COAF), \n                    including banks, financial institutions, virtual currency exchange platforms, insurance companies, casinos, card issuers, leasing companies, real estate companies, and companies that trade luxury goods, to “identify their clients and maintain an updated register, \n                    in accordance with instructions issued by the competent authorities” (Law, Art. 10, Par. I). In principle, this clause indicates that the specifics regarding customer due diligence operations are laid out by each relevant regulatory agency for the entities within its supervisory scope, \n                    rather than the general law.\n                                \n                \n                    In turn, the Securities and Exchange Commission of Brazil (Comissão de Valores Mobiliários - CVM), which oversees all financial institutions that engage with operations relating to securities, stocks, commodities, and futures exchanges, \n                    such as brokers and investment funds, provides the applicable guidelines for customer onboarding in the 2021 CVM Resolução 50 (“Resolution”): \n                \n                    \n                        \n                        \"The adoption of alternative registration systems is allowed, including by electronic means, provided that the solutions adopted meet the objectives of the current regulations and the procedures are subject to verification.\n                        [...]\n                        The customer records information related to customers classified in subsections II to V of article 1 of Annex B shall cover natural persons authorized to represent them, all their direct and indirect controllers, and natural persons \n                        who have significant influence over them until reaching the natural person characterized as the beneficial owner or any of the entities mentioned in paragraph 2” (Resolution, Art. 12-13).\n                        \n                    \n                \n                As per Annex B to the Resolution, at least the following data must by default be present in an individual customer’s records:\n                    \n                        \"a. full name;\n                        b. date of birth;\n                        c. birthplace;\n                        d. nationality;\n                        e. marital status;\n                        f. mother's name;\n                        g. identification document number and issuing body;\n                        h. registration number in the Registry of Natural Persons – (\"Cadastro de Pessoas Físicas\", CPF/MF);\n                        i. name and respective CPF/MF number of the spouse or partner, if applicable;\n                        j. place of residence (street, complement, district, city, federation unit, and ZIP code) and telephone number;\n                        k. email address for correspondence;\n                        l. professional occupation;\n                        m. name of the entity, with the respective customer records with the CNPJ, for which he/she works, when applicable;\n                        n. updated information on earnings and equity status;\n                        [...]\n                        v. whether the customer is considered a person politically exposed under this Resolution;\n                        w. copy of the following documents:\n                            \n                                    1. identification document; and\n                                    2. proof of residence or domicile.\n                            \n                        \n                    \n\n                \n                    In the case of institutions regulated by the Central Bank of Brazil (Banco Central do Brasil – BACEN), including banks, payment services providers, and exchange platforms, \n                    the following rules for customer identification apply:\n                     \n                        \n                            \"The institutions shall adopt identification procedures that allow verifying and validating the identity of the client. \n                            The procedures shall include obtaining, verifying and validating the authenticity of customer identification information, including, \n                            if necessary, by comparing this information with those available in public and private databases\" (BACEN/DC Circular No. 3978 OF 01/23/2020 (“Circular”), Art. 16(1)). \n                        \n                            \"In the customer identification process, at least the following information must be collected:\n                            \n                                I - the full name and taxpayer identification number (CPF), in the case of a natural person;\" (Circular, Art. 16(2)).\n                            \n                        \n                    \n                \n                \n                        Additionally, customer identity verification should also include a process of qualification that would be \"compatible with the client's risk profile and with the nature of the business relationship.\"\n                        Specifically, this requires:\n                        \n                            \"I – identification of the place of residence, in the case of a natural person;\n                            [...]\n                            III – assessment of the client's financial capacity, including income, in the case of a natural person, or revenue, in the case of a legal entity” (Circular, Art. 18(1). \n                        \n                   \n                \n                    Lastly, in the Ordinance Nº 1.231 (“Ordinance”), the Secretariat of Prizes and Bets (Secretaria de Prêmios e Apostas - SPA) establishes the customer identification guidelines for authorized betting operators. \n                    Article 31 of the regulation requires the collection of the following information from all players:\n                     \n                        \"I – full name;\n                        II – nationality;\n                        III – Individual Taxpayer Registration Number – CPF;\n                        IV – date of birth;\n                        V – identification of the bettor's gender;\n                        VI – complete address, which cannot be a PO Box;\n                        VII – country of domicile;\n                        VIII – telephone number;\n                        IX – email;\n                        X – data of the deposit or prepaid payment accounts registered;\n                        XI – prudential betting limit per time elapsed, linking to daily, weekly, monthly or other periods;\n                        XII – prudential limit of betting for financial loss, linked to daily, weekly, monthly or other periods;\n                        XIII – IP address registered at the time of registration; and\n                        XIV – scanned copy of a valid identification document with photo\" (Ordinance, Art. 31).\n                    \n                \n                \n                     Furthermore, Paragraph 2 of the same article mandates the acceptance of only the following identity document type copies for identification purposes:\n                     \n                            \"I - National Identity Card;\n                            II - General Registry - RG;\n                            III - National Driver's License - CNH; or\n                            IV - Passport\"(Ordinance, Art. 31, Par. 2).\n                    \n                \n                \n                     Paragraphs 3 and 5 then specifies the possible methods for user data ownership and real-time presence validation \n                     as well as additional tools that should be applied to ensure the legitimacy of the individual’s identity:\n\n                     \n                        \"I - facial recognition, with proof of life; and\n                        II - alphanumeric password with special characters (Ordinance, Art. 31, Par. 2).\"\n                        [...]\n                        “To verify the validity of the bettor's identity, confirmation must be made through the communication channels provided in the user's registration, such as:\n                        \n                            I. email;\n                            II. Short Message Service (SMS); or\n                            III. messaging apps” (Ordinance, Art. 31, Par. 3, 5).\n                        \n                    \n                \n                \n                    It is also important to note that all three aforementioned regulations require obligated entities to collect and verify an official travel document \n                    in cases where an individual has not obtained a Taxpayer identification number from the Natural Persons Register (Cadastro de Pessoas Físicas – CPF).\n                \n                \n                     Having assessed the relevant legal standards applicable to both financial institutions and betting companies operating in the Brazilian jurisdiction, \n                     it can be concluded that varying electronic identification methods for customer onboarding are permissible, as long as they are able to: \n                     \n                        1. retrieve a copy of an accepted identity document type;\n                        2. establish the legitimate ownership of the identity document; and\n                        3. validate the real-time identification presence of the end user. \n                    \n                \n                \n                    While the guidelines by the Central Bank of Brazil seem to suggest a more flexible approach towards end user verification, \n                    it is ultimately the responsibility of the regulated entity to assess the risk level of their operations as well as each individual customer. \n                    As a result, financial institutions might be expected to adopt such methods and tools that adhere to the common identification practices in the local market.\n                                       \n            \n            \n            \n                Overall, Colombian AML regulations do not impose any particular limitations on remote identity verification means.\n                \n                    External Circular 100-000005 by the Superintendencia de Sociedades, which oversees Virtual Asssets Service Providers (VASPs) and other companies above a certain\n                    threshold of annual income or total assets, allows non-doc KYC by giving obliged subjects a reasonable degree of liberty to verify their client's identity, as Art. 5.2(a) states:\n                \n                \n                    \"To strengthen the security of the process of KYC, and when the transaction allows it, it is recommended, as an example, the following: Get to know by any legal means the origin of resources, verify the customer's identity, their\n                    address and phone number, and according to the characteristics of the negotiation, request a certificate of good standing and power of representation in the case of corporations and any other additional documentation that is considered\n                    to be relevant. Information provided by the customer, as well as the name of the person that verified it, must be duly stored, with a time and date stamp, for evidence purposes of the due diligence\".\n                \n                \n                    Art. 5.2(f) of the same Circular authorizes remote methods of identity verification and reiterates the degree of liberty given to obliged subjects in verifying their clients' identity:,\"If a certain negotiation does not require the\n                    physical presence of the parties, it is essential that the company adopts the necessary measures for the full identification of the natural or legal person with whom the transaction will be carried out\".\n                \n                \n                    The Legal Basic Circular\n                    enacted by the Financial Superintendency of Colombia (SFC) also points to allow remote procedures for KYC. Article 4.2.2.2.1. of Part I chapter IV of said regulation states the following:\n                \n                \n                    \"The supervised institutions may carry out the\n                    KYC procedures in person or not, through the use of digital or electronic channels.\n                    (...)\n                    4.2.2.2.1.1.1.1. Client identification. The supervised institutions must have policies and procedures that allow them to identify and verify the\n                    identity of the potential client, whether it is a natural person, legal entity or structure without legal status, at the time of their linking in face-to-face environments or not\".\n                \n                \n                    As to the need of documentary evidence in the financial\n                    industry, in case of ordinary KYC, the instructions state that the procedure must verify the client's identity document:\"In the case of natural persons, such policies and procedures must consist in verifying the identity document issued by the competent authority\".\n                \n                    As to the way of verifying such document, the Circular states:\n                    Additionally, such policies and procedures must establish the effective verification of the identity of potential clients at the time of their\n                    linking using data and information from reliable and independent sources. For this purpose, the supervised institutions may use: (i) Digital signature certificates, in accordance with the provisions of Law 527 of 1999 and its regulatory\n                    decrees, or the regulations that modify, repeal or substitute it; (ii) biometrics, in accordance with the instructions given by this Superintendency in Chapter I, Title II, Part I of this CBJ; (iii) strong authentication mechanisms, in\n                    accordance with the instructions given by this Superintendency in Chapter I, Title II, Part I of this CBJ; (iv) the information available in the data banks administered by information operators (i.e. risk centers) in the terms set forth\n                    in Law 1266 of 2008 and its regulatory decrees, or the rules that modify, repeal or substitute it; and/or (v) any other technological mechanism that guarantees the effective identity verification of the potential client, in accordance\n                    with the provisions of Chapter I, Title II, Part I of this CBJ.\n                \n                \n                    The aforementioned provisions would allow identity verification, for instance, through a biometric check (such as with a selfie image) with a public registry where both the biometric data of the client and the information of the\n                    identity document are stored.\n                \n                \n                    E-KYC projects based on the national digital ID are actively being developed in Colombia, such as the National Registry Office conducting\n                    pilots of biometric authentication with several banks, with other businesses\n                    encouraged to follow the example. Therefore, in the absence of any explicit prohibition, it may be argued that Non-Doc KYC is generally acceptable under the local AML regulations. This is applicable to both identity verification in\n                    general and validation of isolated identity attributes such as the customer's residential address.\n                                \n            \n\n        \n    \n\n\n    \n    Score 3\n    \n        \n            Country:\n            \n                🇦🇷 Argentina\n                🇧🇩 Bangladesh\n                🇨🇮 Ivory Coast\n                🇰🇿 Kazakhstan\n                🇰🇪 Kenya\n                🇰🇬 Kyrgyzstan\n                🇲🇺 Mauritius\n                🇲🇽 Mexico\n                🇸🇦 Saudi Arabia\n                🇨🇭 Switzerland\n                🇹🇼 Taiwan\n                🇹🇿 Tanzania\n                🇻🇳 Vietnam\n            \n              \n            \n                The Financial Information Unit (FIU) is the Argentinian AML office that regulates banks, financial institutions, online casinos, public registries, insurance companies and other industries.\n                \n                    Each industry has its own regulation, notably FIU Resolution 14/2023 for the financial industry,\n                    FIU Resolution 49/2024 for Virtual Assets Service Providers,\n                    FIU Resolution 194/2023 for bets and the iGaming business, and\n                     FIU Resolution 78/2023 for crowdfunding institutions\n                \n                \n                    As per Article 22 of the UIF Resolution 14/2023 (that regulates the financial industry\"), all individual customers of a financial\n                    institution must be identified by at least:\n                \n                \n                    \n                        a) full name and surname, type and number of identity document. The client's identity must be verified using documents, data or information from public records and/or other reliable sources; with the corresponding evidence of such a\n                        process and a copy of the document that proves the identity presented by the natural person. For such purposes, the following will be accepted as valid documents to prove identity:\n                        \n                            the national identity document (DNI) issued by a competent national authority\n                            the Identity Card or Passport issued by the competent authority of the respective issuing country\n                        \n                    \n                \n                The above-mentioned acceptable documents should contain the following identity attributes:\n                \n                    b) nationality, date and place of birth;\n                    c) marital status;\n                    \n                        d) Unique labor identification code (CUIL), Unique tax identification code (CUIT), Identification code (CDI), or the identification code that may be created in the future by the Federal Public Revenue Administration (AFIP), or its\n                        equivalent for foreign persons, if applicable;\n                    \n                    e) actual address (street, number, town, province, country and postal code)1;\n                    f) telephone number and email address;\n                    g) main work or professional activity;\n                    h) compliance with the provisions of the UIF Resolution referring to PEPs;\n                    i) compliance with the provisions of the UIF Resolution referring to the prevention of terrorist financing.\n                \n                \n                    The UIF Resolution14/2023 also states that these requirements shall apply to the client's attorney, tutor, curator, representative, guarantor and authorized party, who must provide, in addition to the information and documentation\n                    contemplated in this article in order to identify them and verify their identity, the document that accredits such relationship or legal link to verify that the person who claims to act on behalf of the client is authorized to do so.\n                \n                Regarding regulations about specific industries, here is some important information:\n                \n                    \n                        In the case of Virtual Assets Service Providers, Article 23 of the FIU Resolution 49/2024, Article 23) states the same\n                        requirements, except that marital status.\n                    \n                    \n                        In the case of betting operators, Article 23 of FIU Resolution 194/2023 Article 23) also states the same requirements as Article 22 of\n                        FIU Resolution 14/2023, but there is no equivalent to the final paragraph of the latter, there being no mention of legal\n                        representatives.\n                    \n                    \n                        Finally, in the case of crowdfunding institutions, Article 22 of FIU Resolution 78/2023too states the same as in Article 22 of\n                        FIU Resolution 14/2023, but it only requires to declare a \"professional or economic activity\" (not necessarily the main one).\n                    \n                \n                \n                    As to the rules for identification, verification and acceptance of non-face-to-face clients, Article 25 of\n                    FIU Resolution 14/2023 states that \"the identification, verification and acceptance of clients may be carried out in a non-presential\n                    manner, through the use of electronic means that substitute physical presence, using rigorous, storable, auditable and non-manipulable biometric techniques\".\n                \n                As to the exhibition of the ID document, it states that \"the identification and verification of non-presential clients must comply with the provisions of articles 22, 23 and 24, including the display of the required documentation\".\n                The verification must be carried out \"at the time of identification, or, where appropriate, before the client begins to operate\".\n                \n                    Finally, the regulation states that the obliged subjects may establish automated verification mechanisms, provided that there is evidence that its performance in confirming the correspondence and the inalterability of the information or\n                    documentation provided is equal to or greater than that carried out by a human agent.\n                \n                \n                    As to the other industries, there are similar rules. Notably, for crowdfunding institutions, Article 25 of\n                    FIU Resolution 78/2023 states an alternative method:\n                \n                \"Alternatively, non-face-to-face Clients may be accepted, subject to the following rules:\n                \n                    \n                        The Client may request acceptance through the Obliged Subject's website or other alternative channels (telematic, telephone or similar), sending the documents established in Articles 22, 23 and 24, which correspond to its nature and\n                        characteristics.\n                    \n                    The Obliged Subject will provide a personal and non-transferable password, which includes control questions, which must be used by the Client to operate.\n                    The Obliged Subject must consider the need to visit the Client, leaving a record of such fact. The carrying out of such a visit by persons hired by the Obliged Subject will be acceptable\".\n                \n                \n                    In summary, Argentinian regulations impose documentary-oriented requirements for the identification and verification of customers across various sectors, including financial institutions, virtual asset service providers, betting\n                    operators, and crowdfunding entities. The Financial Information Unit (FIU) permits the verification of customer identification against public records and other reliable sources, ensuring that these processes are duly documented with\n                    copies of identification documents. While each sector is governed by specific regulations, the fundamental principles of customer due diligence remain consistent throughout.\n                \n                Regarding address verification, the regulations do not provide detailed instructions; rather, they impose a general requirement for the collection of address data.\n                \n                \n                    \n                        1 — No particular means of verifying an individual’s residential address are prescribed in the 2023 Resolution.\n                    \n                                \n             \n\n            \n                \n                    In Bangladesh, non-documentary identity verification is currently endorsed under the\n                    2019 Guidelines on Electronic Know Your Customer (e-KYC) (\"Guidelines\"), issued by the Bangladesh Financial Intelligence Unit (BFIU) and\n                    applicable to all reporting entities. However, it implies several restrictions:\n                \n                \n                    \n                        the Guidelines only apply to KYC conducted in respect of natural persons holding a valid national ID card (NID) of Bangladesh with biometric data stored therein;\n                    \n                    \n                        fully remote KYC (where the customer does not visit the premises of the reporting entity) prescribes a seamless procedure with the following steps: (i) the NID is captured from both sides, with the data extracted by OCR; (ii) the customer's face is captured with a high-resolution camera; (iii) the necessary identity data (name, parental names, address, phone number, etc.)1 is collected in digital format; (iv) the client's wet signature or electronic signature or digital signature or PIN is collected for future reference; (v) the data is authenticated against the official database held by the NID Wing of Election Commission; and (vi) AML screening is carried out (see Section 3.3 of the Guidelines).\n                    \n                \n                \n                    As for individuals that do not hold a NID, the document-based approach is predominant. For example, the\n                    Guidance Notes on Prevention of Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing for Financial Institutions by the BFIU and the Central Bank of Bangladesh suggest a photo-bearing ID\n                    (which, furthermore, has to be certified and, as per Section 7.3.5.1, supplemented with at least one additional check to \"guard against impersonation\") is a necessary element in the KYC procedure:\n                \n                \n                    \"The original, certified copy of the following Photo ID also plays vital role to identify the customer: (i) Current valid passport; (ii) Valid driving license; (iii) National ID Card; (iv) Employer provided ID Card, bearing the\n                    photograph and signature of the applicant; Identification documents which do not bear photographs or signatures, or are easy to obtain, are normally not appropriate as sole evidence of identity [...]\" (Section 7.3.5).\n                \n                Likewise, in terms of confirming the customer's address, one or more of the following steps is recommended:\n                \n                    \"provision of a recent utility bill, tax assessment or bank statement containing details of the address (to guard against forged copies it is strongly recommended that original documents are examined);\n                    checking the Voter lists;\n                    checking the telephone directory;\n                    visiting home/office;\n                    sending thanks letter\" (Section 7.3.5).\n                \n                \n                    Accordingly, the only explicitly permitted electronic-based KYC solution is limited to NID holders and requires the customer to actually present the NID at the onboarding stage for capturing. Address may be verified via electronic\n                    evidence, but only in a limited number of scenarios as demonstrated above.\n                       \n                \n                \n                    \n                        1 — The full list including: Applicant’s Name; Mother’s Name; Father’s Name; Spouse Name; Gender (M/F/T); Profession; Nationality; Monthly Income and Source of Funds; Mobile Phone Number; Present Address; Permanent Address. The fact that “present address” and “permanent address” are required separately may imply that geolocation / IP data, in combination with other sources, has to be collected so that the obliged entity can obtain both data points (unless it opts for a self-declaration); the Guidelines encourage this, stating that “financial institutions also may collect other complementary data (such as, geolocation, IP addresses, etc.) which could also support ongoing due diligence”. However, no specific procedures for verifying address are prescribed for the NID-based procedure.\n                    \n                                         \n            \n          \n            \n                Côte d'Ivoire adopts and enforces AML/CFT regulations through various regulators, including Banque Centrale des Etats de l'Afrique de l'Ouest (BCEAO) and Commission Bancaire Union Monétaire Ouest Africaine (UMOA).\n                \n                    Pursuant to Article 27 of the ANNEXE A LA DECISION N° 26 du 02/07/2015/CM/UMOA (uniform\n                    AML/CFT Law of the member states of the West African Monetary Union), a valid original official document is required during customer identity verification. Further, a copy of the document should be retained:\n                \n                \n                    \"Identifying a natural person involves obtaining the full first and last name, the date and place of birth and the address of their main residence. Verification of the identity of a natural person requires the presentation of a valid\n                    original official document including a photograph, of which a copy is taken. Verification of their address is carried out by the presentation of a document capable of providing proof or by any other means. The information to be recorded\n                    and kept is the surname, first name, date and place of birth of the person as well as the nature, date and place of issue of the document. The financial institution verifies the authenticity of the document presented\".\n                \n                \n                    Accordingly, even though the uniform AML/CFT Law of the West African Monetary Union directly requires the documentary approach for identity data verification, the customer' address specifically could be verified via alternative\n                    approaches.\n                \n                \n                    It should also be noted that Central Bank of West African States (BCEAO) (issues licenses for credit institutions (banks), financial companies, mobile money institutions) as well as Minister of Finance of Ivory Coast (issues licenses\n                    for capital market actors (brokers, traders, portfolio managers), insurance companies and brokers, microfinance institutions, foreign exchange bureaus, and postal financial services) are empowered to provide alternative requirements in\n                    respect of customer's verification for the licensees.\n                               \n             \n\n            \n                Banks, insurance companies, professional participants in the securities market, VASPs and other obliged institutions shall identify the clients and verify their identity as per Article 5 of the Law of the Republic of Kazakhstan “On combating the legalization (laundering) of proceeds from crime and the financing of terrorism” (the “AML Law”).\n                Pursuant to Article 6 of the AML Law, customer die diligence (the “CDD”) measures shall be completed prior to establishing business relations. However, with respect to certain transactions, CDD measures may be skipped as per Article 5(3-1) of the AML Law:\n                “3-1. The measures provided for in this article shall not be taken in the following cases:\n                1) when carrying out the following one-time transactions: \n                when unidentified owners of electronic money - individuals carry out transactions to acquire and use electronic money that do not exceed the amount specified in paragraph 4 of Article 44 Law of the Republic of Kazakhstan “On Payments and Payment Systems” [approx. USD 380];\n                \n                when a client makes a non-cash payment or transfers money without using a bank account, if the amount of such payment or transfer does not exceed 500,000 tenge [approx. USD 970] or an amount in foreign currency equivalent to 500,000 tenge [approx. USD 970], except in cases where the client makes a suspicious transaction;\n                when a client who is an individual carries out a transaction using a payment card that is not a means of access to the bank account of such client, if the amount of such transaction does not exceed 200,000 tenge [approx. USD 390] or an amount in foreign currency equivalent to 200,000 tenge [approx. USD 390];”.\n                As a general rule, CDD measures shall include “verification of the accuracy of information required to identify the client” (Article 5(3)(6) of the AML Law). However, according to Article 5(3-2) of the AML Law, in certain cases prescribed by law the verification step may be skipped by banks, insurers, professional participants of the securities markets, but not VASPs:\n                “3-2. The subjects of financial monitoring specified in subparagraphs 1) - 5), 11) and 12) of paragraph 1 of Article 3 of this Law, within the framework of remotely established business relations with the client, have the right to carry out transactions, with the exception of cross-border payments, without taking measures to verify the accuracy of the information necessary to identify the client (his representative), the beneficial owner, provided for in subparagraph 6) of part one of paragraph 3 of this article, in the following cases:\n                \n                 1) the implementation by the client of transactions for the payment of taxes, penalties, fines and other mandatory payments to the budget, as well as insurance premiums under compulsory insurance contracts;\n                 2) the transfer of funds to the client’s bank account.”\n                \n                The scope of data by which an individual customer must be identified is established by the industry AML regulations. For example, for banks and professional participants of the securities markets the following list of data is specified as per para. 26 of the Requirements for the Internal Control Rules for the purpose of combating the legalization (laundering) of proceeds from crime, the financing of terrorism and the financing of the proliferation of weapons of mass destruction for second-tier banks, branches of non-resident banks of the Republic of Kazakhstan and the National Postal Operator (the AML Rules for Banks”) and para. 27 of the Requirements for the Internal Control Rules for the purpose of combating the legalization (laundering) of proceeds from crime, the financing of terrorism and the financing of the proliferation of weapons of mass destruction for professional participants in the securities market and the central depository (the “AML Rules for Securities Market”), respectively:\n                “When identifying an individual , organizations establish and record the following data:\n                \n                 last name, first name, patronymic (if any);\n                 citizenship;\n                 date and place of birth;\n                 legal address;\n                 details of the identity document and (or) other document on the basis of which identification is carried out;\n                 type of activity (for individual entrepreneurs);\n                 individual identification number (except for cases when an individual has not been assigned an individual identification number in accordance with the legislation of the Republic of Kazakhstan).”\n                \n                While the Requirements for internal control rules for the purpose of combating the legalization (laundering) of proceeds from crime and the financing of terrorism for persons engaged in the issuance and circulation of secured digital assets (the “AML Rules for VASPs”) do not provide a specific list of data that shall be collected within the course of a CDD, we assume that the same scope of information as established for the banks and professional participants of the securities market will be applicable to them.\n                In addition to the above scope of information, it is necessary to determine whether the client\n                \n                 is a politically exposed person (as per Article 8 of the AML Law, para. 26 of the AML Rules for Banks, para. 27 of the AML Rules for Securities Market, para. 27 of the AML Rules for VASPs);\n                 is included in the lists of organizations and individuals associated with the financing of terrorism and extremism, financing the proliferation of weapons of mass destruction (as per Articles 12 and 12-1 of the AML Law, para. 22(5) of the AML Rules for Banks, para. 27 of the AML Rules for Securities Market, para. 27 of the AML Rules for VASPs).\n                \n                Further, with respect to the non-residents of the Republic of Kazakhstan,\n                \n                 the banks shall confirm the basis for their presence in the Republic of Kazakhstan (e.g., employment contract, training contract, residence permit) as per para. 22 of the AML Rules for Banks. However, pursuant to para. 26 of the AML Rules for Banks, “information on migration cards is not required to be obtained with respect to citizens of states that are members of the Eurasian Economic Union.”;\n                 the VASPs shall require, inter alia, the “document certifying registration with the authorized bodies of the Republic of Kazakhstan for the right of entry, exit and stay of a non-resident individual in the territory of the Republic of Kazakhstan, unless otherwise provided by international treaties ratified by the Republic of Kazakhstan” as per para. 23 of the AML Rules for VASPs.\n                \n                The banks shall also check whether the client is a foreign organizer of the gambling business, whose activities are considered to be illegal in the territory of the Republic of Kazakhstan on the basis of a court decision (para. 22(14) of the AML Rules for Banks).\n                Pursuant to Article 5(4) of the AML Law, a specific approach to the CDD process shall be established by obliged entities in their internal rules. However, with respect to the remote onboarding, the Requirements for due diligence of clients in the case of remote establishment of business relations by financial entities monitoring Resolution of the Board of the National Bank of the Republic of Kazakhstan (the “Remote CDD Rules”) shall be applied. The Remote CDD Rules establish a specific set of rules for remote onboarding of customers by the following types of obliged entities: banks, insurance companies, professional participants in the securities market, and some other entities as per para. 1 of the Remote CDD Rules. However, the Remote CDD Rules do not apply to VASPs.\n                As to VASPs, para. 23 of the AML Rules for VASPs explicitly provides for documented approach:\n                “When conducting due diligence on a client (his representative) and a beneficial owner, entities shall document information about the client (his representative) and the beneficial owner based on originals or notarized copies of documents with an apostille or in the legalized manner established by international treaties ratified by the Republic of Kazakhstan, submitted at the client’s (his representative’s) discretion.”\n                The obliged entities that are allowed to apply the Remote CDD Rules may decide to use remote onboarding of a client on a risk-based approach (para. 2 of the Remote CDD Rules). At the same time, remote onboarding is explicitly prohibited by Article 5(9) of the AML Law if:\n                \n                 “1) the client (its representative) and the beneficial owner are a person included in the list of persons involved in terrorist activities, as well as the list of organizations and persons associated with the financing of the proliferation of weapons of mass destruction, and (or) the list of organizations and persons associated with the financing of terrorism and extremism;\n                 2) the client (its representative) and the beneficial owner are an established person or organization against whom international sanctions are applied in accordance with the resolutions of the Security Council of the United Nations\n                 3) the client is a person who has been assigned a risk level requiring the application of enhanced due diligence measures in accordance with paragraph 7 of this article and the internal control rules, with the exception of the conclusion of insurance organizations of insurance contracts in electronic form, the insurance premium and (or) insurance payment for which are made through bank accounts.”\n                \n                In addition, according to para. 22 of the AML Rules for Banks:\n                “Remote establishment of business relations with clients who are residents of countries with a high risk of ML/FT based on the factor of illegal production, trafficking and (or) transit of drugs, with the exception of the countries of the Eurasian Economic Union, extension of such business relations, as well as issuance and reissue (without the personal presence of the client) of more than one payment card to the specified persons is not allowed.”\n                As per para. 3 of the Remote CDD Rules, a remote onboarding is only possible for the clients who meet all of the following requirements:\n                \n                 “1) an individual or legal entity is assigned with the identification number (except for cases when an individual or legal entity has not been assigned an identification number in accordance with the legislation of the Republic of Kazakhstan) or the number under which an individual who is a non-resident of the Republic of Kazakhstan or a legal entity who is a non-resident of the Republic of Kazakhstan is registered in a foreign state;\n                 2) the client (his representative) and the beneficial owner are not a person included in the list of organizations and persons associated with the financing of terrorism and extremism;\n                 3) the client (his representative) and the beneficial owner are not an established person or organization against whom international sanctions (embargo) are applied in accordance with Security Council resolutions United Nations;\n                 4) the client is not a person who has been assigned a risk level that requires the application of enhanced due diligence measures in accordance with paragraph 7 of Article 5 of the AML/CFT Law and the internal control rules.”\n                \n                In addition, according to para. 4 of the Remote CDD Rules, the following conditions shall be met simultaneously in order to carry out a remote onboarding:\n                \n                 “1) the information provided for in subparagraphs 1) [data with respect to an individual: details of the identity document, individual identification number, legal address], 2), 2-1) and 4) of paragraph 3 Article 5 of the AML/CFT Law is recorded in accordance with paragraph 8 of the Requirements [Remote CDD Rules];\n                 2) the individual client has given consent to the collection, processing, storage and provision, including, if necessary, to third parties, of his/her personal data, confirmed by means of an identification tool;\n                 3) there is an automated information system that enables the collection, processing, storage, provision and protection of personal data of an individual client (his representative) and beneficial owners;\n                 4) the subject of financial monitoring has no suspicions that the purpose of business relations is the performance of transactions for the purposes of illegal financial transactions.”\n                \n                The process for remote CDD measures shall be as follows as per para. 6 of the Remote CDD Rules:\n                \n                 “1) the client inputs into the remote access system of the financial monitoring entity his/her personal or business identification number;\n                 2) the client identification and verification of identity is performed;\n                 3) the information about the client is recorded, as provided for in subparagraphs 1),2) 2-1) and 4) paragraph 3 of Article 5 of the AML/CFT Law.”\n                \n                Pursuant to para. 7 of the Remote CDD Rules,\n                “7. The following methods are used for client identification and verification:\n                \n                 1) electronic digital signature of an individual or legal entity;\n                 2) biometric identification means;\n                 3) payment card details during identification and authentication of an individual (number, expiration date of the payment card, name of the payment card system) issued by a second-tier bank [all banks operating in the country, except for the National Bank of the Republic of Kazakhstan, represent the second level of the banking system and are second-tier banks] or the National Postal Operator with which the financial monitoring entity has concluded an agreement on information exchange, if the individual was previously identified by the financial monitoring entity in person;\n                 4) confirmation of the identity of an individual by verification with government databases;\n                 5) a unique identifier, which is a combination of letters and numbers or symbols or other identifier established by the financial monitoring entity to identify the client and agreed upon with him/her.”\n                \n                The obliged entities may use one or a combination of several methods listed above. However, method referred to in subparagraph 5) cannot be used as a standalone method and shall always be used in combination with one or more methods of identification and authentication of the client, provided for in subparagraphs 1), 2), 3) and 4) above.\n                In case of remote onboarding, the verification of the accuracy of information about the client is carried out in accordance with Article 5(3)(6) of the AML Law and the internal control rules as per para. 9 of the Remote CDD Rules.\n                Therefore, the AML/CFT regulations of Kazakhstan allow banks, insurance companies, professional participants in the securities market, and some other entities as per para. 1 of the Remote CDD Rules to apply non-documentary identity verification in certain cases and subject to the above mentioned limitations, while VASPs shall always use documentary identity verification.               \n                    \n              \n            \n                \n                    Pursuant to Art. 45(1) of Kenya's 2009 Proceeds of Crime and Anti-Money Laundering Act, a natural person's identity must always be\n                    verified based on an official identification document, which should be obtained directly from the customer rather than from an external source:\n                \n                \n                    \"A reporting institution shall take reasonable measures to satisfy itself as to the true identity of any applicant seeking to enter into a business relationship with it or to carry out a transaction or series of transactions with it, by\n                    requiring the applicant to produce an official record reasonably capable of establishing the true identity of the applicant, such as - (a) in the case of an individual - (i) a birth certificate; (ii) a national identity\n                    card; (iii) a driver's license; (iv) a passport; or (v) any other official means of identification as may be prescribed\".\n                \n                \n                    This is supported by the 2013 Prudential Guidelines issued by the Central Bank of Kenya (\"Prudential Guidelines\"):\"An\n                    institution shall take measures to satisfy itself as to the true identity of any applicant seeking to enter into a business relationship with it, or to carry out a transaction or series of transactions with it, by requiring the\n                    applicant to produce an official record for the purposes of establishing the true identity of the applicant. At a minimum the mandatory requirements are as follows: (i) In the case of an individual:\n                    A birth certificate; or Passport; or National identity card; or Driver's licence\" (para. 5.6.5.1).\n                \n                \n                    Likewise, in terms of address verification specifically, obtaining a utility bill or other forms of documentation is recommended under paras. 5.6.5.1 and 5.6.7.7 of the Prudential Guidelines as the best practice and there are no\n                    explicit references to the possibility of relying on electronic sources such as external databases or geolocation / IP address data instead. However, para. 5.6.7.5 suggests this method is not the only permissible one and \"it is for each\n                    institution to decide upon which checks to employ\".\n                \n                \n                    Accordingly, Non-Doc KYC solutions alone appear insufficient for regulatory compliance but, arguably, may be used to verify certain isolated identity attributes such as addresses.\n                \n            \n\n            \n                Banks, professional participants of the securities market, insurance organizations, issuers and agents (distributors) of electronic money, VASPs, legal entities engaged in gambling activities (including online and electronic (virtual) casinos), as well as other obliged entities shall identify and verify their clients as per Article 21(1)(1) of the ,Law of the Kyrgyz Republic “On counteracting the financing of criminal activities and legalization (laundering) of criminal proceeds” (the “AML Law”) and para. 3 of Appendix 12 “Position on the procedure for conducting a proper customer check” (the “CDD Rules”) to the Kyrgyz Republic Government Resolution “On measures to implement the Law of the Kyrgyz Republic “On Counteracting the Financing of Terrorist Activities and Legalization (Laundering) of Criminal Proceeds”.\n                According to para. 16 of the CDD Rules, which, in turn, refers to Annex 1 thereto, the scope of data by which an individual customer must be identified is quite extensive and requires, inter alia, the following elements:\n                \n                 Client status
