(resident / not resident)\n                 Last Name\n                 First Name\n                 Patronymic Name (if applicable)\n                 Date of birth\n                 Place of birth (if specified in the document)\n                 Nationality (if specified in the document)\n                 Gender\n                 Citizenship\n                 Marital status (from the client’s words or if available in the document)\n                 Requisites of the identity document\n                 Requisites of the document confirming the right of a foreign citizen or stateless person to stay (reside) in the Kyrgyz Republic (if applicable)\n                 Personal identification number\n                 Registration address (if specified in the document)\n                 Residence address (from the client’s words)\n                 Telephone number.\n                \n                In addition, it is necessary to determine whether the client:\n                \n                 is a politically exposed person (Article 21(3) of the AML Law, line 18 of the questionnaire as per Annex 1 to the AML Regulation) or\n                 is subject to UN sanctions or national restrictive measures (Article 13(1) of the AML Law, line 24 of the questionnaire as per Annex 1 to the AML Regulation).\n                \n                Appendix 5 to the CDD Rules establishes the procedure for the identification and verification of an individual client remotely by some obliged entities, including the banks, VASPs, issuers and agents (distributors) of electronic money, and professional participants in the securities market1. The relevant procedure  establishes two separate approaches for verifying identity of (i) citizens of the Kyrgyz Republic and (ii) foreign individuals. However, in both cases Appendix 5 to the CDD Rules explicitly requires receiving from the client of a passport image.\n                The procedure for identification and verification of a client (a citizen of the Kyrgyz Republic) establishing business relations remotely is as follows:\n                “9. Digital identification and verification of the client - a citizen of the Kyrgyz Republic is carried out by using up-to-date information from the State Register of National Passports of Citizens of the Kyrgyz Republic, the automated information system “Passport”, “Subsystems for the Face Recognition System”, through the holder’s electronic services through web services (hereinafter referred to as state electronic services).\n                \n                11. Digital identification and verification of a client - a citizen of the Kyrgyz Republic using the platform of a financial institution and state electronic services is carried out in the following order:\n                1) receiving from the client through the platform of the financial institution (in real time):\n                \n                 personal identification number of the citizen (hereinafter referred to as the PIN);\n                 passport series and numbers;\n                 photos of the client or videos with the image of the client;\n                 passport photos;\n                 mobile phone numbers;\n                 e-mail addresses; ”\n                \n                For foreign customers the same verification approach is applicable, however, the information system through which the customer’s data have to be validated should be additionally confirmed with financial intelligence agency of the Kyrgyz Republic:\n                “16. The use of information systems (software) for digital identification and verification of a client - a citizen of a foreign state is carried out in agreement with the financial intelligence agency of the Kyrgyz Republic, as well as the authorized state body of the Kyrgyz Republic in the field of regulation and supervision of the financial market and the National Bank of the Kyrgyz Republic within their competence.\n                \n                19. Digital identification and verification of a client - a citizen of a foreign state is carried out in the following order:\n                1) receiving from the client through the financial institution's platform (in real time):\n                \n                 Citizen's PIN;\n                 Passport series and number;\n                 Client's photograph or video recording with the client's image;\n                 Passport photograph;\n                 Mobile phone number;\n                 Email address; ”\n                \n                Further, Appendix 5 to the CDD Rules specifies that photographs of the both sides of the passport shall be obtained in the course of a remote onboarding:\n                “29. During digital identification and verification of the client:\n                \n                 the photograph and/or video recording must be in color;\n                 the video recording must have sound;\n                 no persons other than the client are allowed in the photograph and/or video recording;\n                 it is necessary to obtain a photograph of the front and back (reverse) sides of the passport; ”\n                \n                It is important to note that the banks may apply the remote onboarding procedure only with respect to the citizens of Kyrgyz Republic as per para. 1 of the Resolution of the Board National Bank Kyrgyz Republic № 2020-P-12\\27-1-(NPA) dated May 13, 2020 On the identification and verification procedure customers in remote mode (as amended in 2021 and in 2024).\n                Therefore, the AML/CFT regulations of Kyrgyz Republic currently only provide for a documentary identity verification together with a validation against specified data sources (in respect of local residents) and approved data sources in case of foreign residents.\n                \n                \n                    \n                        1 — Para. 2 of Appendix 5 to the CDD Rules, para. 54 of the Position about electronic money in the Kyrgyz Republic (Application to the resolution of the Board of the National Bank of the Kyrgyz Republic dated April 20, 2022 № 2022-P-14/25-3-(PS)).\n                                        \n                            \n                        \n          \n            \n                FIAML Regulations 2018 (\"FIAMLR\") are the primary legal source of AML/CFT obligations in Mauritius.\n                As per FIAMLR Article 3(1)(a), the general identification and identity verification duty is formulated broadly:\n                \n                    \"A reporting entity shall: [...] identify its customer whether permanent or occasional and verify the identity of its customer using reliable, independent source documents, data or information, including, where available, electronic\n                    identification means, or any other secure, remote or electronic identification process as may be specified by the relevant regulatory body or supervisory authority\".\n                \n                \n                    More clarification is provided in Article 4, specifying both the identity data to be collected and verified for individual customers and the expected the verification methods:\n                \n                \n                    \"For a customer who is a natural person, a reporting\n                    person shall obtain and verify -\n                        \n                            (a) the full legal and any other names, including, marital name, former legal name or alias;\n                            (b) the date and place of birth;\n                            (c) the nationality;\n                            (d) the current and permanent address; and\n                            (e) such other information as may be specified by a relevant supervisory authority or regulatory body.1\n                        \n                    \n                    \n                        For the purposes of paragraph (1), documentary evidence as may be specified by a relevant regulatory body or supervisory authority shall be used for the purposes of verification of identity requirement\".\n                    \n                \n                \n                    The above-cited requirements are detailed in the 2020 AML/CFT Handbook by the Mauritius Financial Services Commission (FSC), the integrated regulator\n                    for the financial services sector (other than banking). . Specifically, Section 5.3 sets out an exhaustive list of document acceptable as proof in relation to each identity attribute; for instance:\n                \n                \n                    \n                        for name, gender, date of birth etc.: current valid passport; current valid national identity card; current valid driving licence (where the financial institution is satisfied that the driving licensing authority carries out a check\n                        on the holder's identity before issuing the licence). In each case, the document must incorporate photographic evidence of identity;\n                    \n                \n                \n                    \n                        for residential address: any of the identity sources listed above; a recent utility bill issued to the individual by name; a recent bank or credit card statement; or a recent reference or letter of introduction from (i) a financial\n                        institution that is regulated in Mauritius; (ii) a regulated financial services business which is operating in an equivalent jurisdiction or a jurisdiction that complies with the FATF standards; or (iii) a branch or subsidiary of a\n                        group headquartered in a well-regulated overseas country or territory which applies group standards to subsidiaries and branches worldwide, and tests the application of, and compliance with, such standards. \"Recent\" means within the\n                        last three months.\n                    \n                \n                \n                    While Section 5.11 of the AML/CFT Handbook mentions the possibility of electronic identification and verification, this does not negate the overall document-based approach:\"Where a financial institution adopts a system providing for the\n                    electronic verification of natural person identity, the financial institution must assess the veracity of the controls inherent within the system in order to determine whether the financial institution can place reliance on the results\n                    produced, or if additional steps are necessary to complement the existing controls. [...] In all circumstances, the financial institution should adopt a risk-based approach to\n                    ensure that the documents received are adequately verified and that the customer's identity is confirmed such that the financial institution is comfortable with the authenticity of these documents\".\n                \n                \n                    To conclude, in relation to all identity attributes (including the address), non-documentary verification solutions may only be implemented as supplementary checks rather than on a standalone basis.\n                \n                \n                \n                    \n                        1 — As per Section 5.3 of the FSC AML/CFT Handbook, this also includes: gender; any public position held and, where appropriate, nature of employment (including self-employment) and name of employer; government issued personal identification number or other government issued unique identifier.\n                    \n                                \n            \n\n            \n                \n                    The Mexican AML/CFT legislative framework governing the requirements for customer identification primarily includes the\n                    2012 Federal Law of the Prevention and Identification of Operations with Resources of Illicit Origin (the \"LFPIORPI\") and the\n                    2013 General Rules referred to therein (the \"General Rules\").\n                \n                \n                    Although neither the LFPIORPI nor the General Rules set out a detailed procedure for remote KYC, the LFPIORPI appears to endorse the documentary KYC approach:\"Those who carry out the Vulnerable Activities [...] will have the following\n                    obligations: I. Identify the clients and users with whom they carry out their own Activities subject to monitoring and verify their identity based on credentials[term frequently used to mean \"identity document\"]\n                    or official documentation, as well as obtain a copy of the documentation\" (Art. 18(I)).\n                \n                \n                    At the same time, pursuant to Art. 12 of the General Rules, the scope of identification data to be collected and verified depends on whether the customer is a local citizen or permanent resident and whether they have their current place\n                    of residence abroad:\n                \n                \n                    \n                        (i) for a customer who \"is a natural person and who declares to be of Mexican nationality or foreign nationality with the conditions of temporary resident or permanent resident\", the required information includes, inter alia, \"data\n                        of the identification [document previously used for identification], consisting of: name of the identification; authority that issued it, and its number\" (Annex 3(a)(x));\n                    \n                    \n                        (ii) for a customer as set out in section (i) above, who has \"their place of residence abroad and at the same time have an address in national territory where they can receive correspondence\", it is\n                        additionally required to obtain \"identification, which must be, in any case, an official original document issued by a competent authority, valid on the date of its presentation, containing the\n                        photograph, signature and, where applicable, address\" (Annex 3(b)(i));\n                    \n                    \n                        (iii) for a customer who is \"who is a foreign natural person with the conditions of stay of a visitor or other than those established [in sections (i) and (ii) above\", among other data and documents, a \"\n                        passport or official original document issued by the competent authority of the country of origin, valid on the date of its presentation, containing the photograph, signature and, where applicable, address of the\n                        aforementioned Client or User, which proves their nationality\" is always necessary (Annex 5(b)(i)).\n                    \n                \n                \n                    In addition, when the customer provides identification documents with deletions or amendments, another type of identification document must be obtained or, where that is not possible, two bank or commercial references and two personal\n                    references must be requested, which must include the telephone number, paternal and maternal names and surnames, and address (General Rules, Art. 12).\n                \n                \n                    Therefore, it can be argued that the General Rules differentiate between an official photo-bearing ID and other documents that may serve for identification, and that only the latter may be sufficient in relation to local citizens or\n                    permanent residents who do not live abroad (so long as all the other required information is presented).\n                \n                \n                    Electronic KYC is increasingly making it possible for financial institutions to comply with AML regulations while concurrently enabling more of Mexico's population to participate in the country's financial system via online banking and\n                    other applications:\n                \n                \n                    \n                        according to the Digital Identification of Mexico guidelines, the National Banking and Securities Commission\n                        (CNBV) is entitled to \"approve additional identity mechanisms for remote accounts, provided that such technology proves to be reliable and could verify the official credentials (the CURP and INE card)\". For example, for Level 3 bank\n                        accounts and consumer credit below 3,000 UDIs, the following data should be collected and subsequently verified using the INE credential: (i) full name, (ii) date of birth, (iii) gender, (iv) nationality, (v) the CURP, (vi) mobile\n                        number, and (vii) address, including street, number, municipality, province, postal code, and country1;\n                    \n                    \n                        in 2021, separate procedures for digital onboarding of persons of Mexican nationality, depending on their transactional volume, were\n                        introduced (including \"liveness\", biometric data match against official sources (e.g., the National Electoral Institute or\n                        the Ministry of Foreign Affairs), and video identification); however, all of them imply it is necessary to observe and collect an image of the customer's ID as part of the KYC flow;\n                    \n                    \n                        also in 2021, it was prescribed that financial institutions track the real-time\n                        geolocation of their customers with an otherwise unknown location when they perform remote operations via their devices.\n                    \n                \n                \n                    Accordingly, whereas in some scenarios it may be permissible or even mandatory to refer to external data sources during customer onboarding, an identification document would almost invariably be required in the process. However,\n                    electronic checks may be additionally incorporated to verify isolated identity attributes (such as the customer's residential address) where the obliged entity considers it necessary.\n                \n                \n                \n                    \n                        1 — Additional data (country of birth etc.) may be required as per Annex 1 to the General Rules, depending on, e.g., whether the customer is a resident of Mexico.\n                    \n                                               \n                      \n\n            \n                \n                    The primary AML/CFT legislation of Saudi Arabia - namely, the Anti-Money Laundering Law (along with the\n                    Implementing Regulations thereto) and the\n                    Law on Combating the Financing of Terrorism (along with the\n                    Implementing Regulations thereto) - do not lay emphasis on the\n                    acceptable methods of identity verification, while stipulating that certain data must always be collected from individual customers and validated via \"reliable and independent sources, documents, data or information\":\n                \n                \n                    \"the financial institution or designated non-financial business and profession shall obtain and verify the full legal name, residential or the national address, date and place of birth, and nationality\"1 (Implementing Regulations to the\n                    AML Law, section 7/2(a); Implementing Regulations to the CFT Law, section 17(3)(a)).\n                \n                \n                    The matter is regulated more precisely in relation to the respective industries by the Saudi Central Bank (SAMA), the Capital Market Authority (CMA), and other bodies such as the Ministry of Commerce and Investment (MOCI), which all\n                    demonstrate a divergence of approaches to non-documentary KYC:\n                \n                (i) CMA:\n                As per the CMA AML/CFT Rules (addressed to the securities and investment sector):\n                \n                    individual customer's identities must be verified \"using the original documents\" (copies are only acceptable in case of reliance on a third party) as follows:\n                    \n                        Saudi nationals:\n                        \n                            the client's National Identification Card or family record;\n                            the client's residential address & place of work and work address;\n                        \n                        individual expatriates:\n                        \n                            a residence permit (Iqamah) or a five-year special residence permit or a passport, and a National Identification for Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) nationals or a diplomatic identification card for diplomats;\n                            the client's residential address & place of work and work address (Article 8(2), 8(4));\n                        \n                    \n                \n                \n                    furthermore, based on Articles 7(4) and 8(5), face-to-face identity verification is mandatory except when there is reliance on a third party;\n                \n                \n                    \n                        in turn, Articles 14(1) and 14(3) specify that a third party eligible for reliance must \"either be a commercial bank or financial institution that engages in securities activities\" and may only be engaged \"to perform the CDD if the\n                        client is located in a country other than Saudi Arabia\".\n                    \n                \n                (ii) MOCI:\n                \n                    The Manual on AML-CFT (addressed to certain Designated Non-Financial Businesses and Professions (DNFBPs), specifically\n                    dealers in precious metals and precious stones, real estate agents, and chartered accountants), while not explicitly requiring face-to-face KYC, replicates the CMA AML/CFT Rules provision on the necessary documentary evidence to be\n                    collected from individual customers:\"Establishing the identity of the client and continuously verifying the identity of all dealers against valid officially certified original documents proving their identity as\n                    follows:\n                \n                \n                    \n                        Saudi nationals:\n                        \n                            National identification card or family record.\n                            Address of the person, place of residence and place of work.\n                        \n                    \n                    \n                        Individual expatriates:\n                        \n                            Residence permit (Iqamah) or a five-year special residence permit or a passport or National identification for GCC nationals or a diplomatic identification card for diplomats.\n                            Address of the person, place of residence and place of work\" (Section 3(1)).\n                        \n                    \n                \n                (iii) SAMA:\n                \n                    Pursuant to Section 3.3 of the 2019\n                    \n                        Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing (AML/CTF) Guide\n                    \n                    (\"AML Guide\"), addressed to SAMA-regulated financial institutions, \"information and documents issued by government bodies are considered to be from reliable and independent sources\". Sections 3.9-3.10 futher imply the\n                    possibility of non-documentary identity verification, so long as it is conducted via \"reliable and independent electronic services\", such as the National Information Center:\n                \n                \n                    \"3.9 The customer is not required to come to the financial institution when updating and reviewing their information for identity verification\n                    as long as electronic authentication services approved by the National Information Center are used. However, the financial institution shall determine the need for further documentation or the customer's presence based\n                    on the level of risk posed by the customer.\n                \n                \n                    3.10 When using reliable and independent electronic services to verify a customer's identity, the financial institution shall determine if more documentation is required based on the level of risk posed by the customer\n                    . In addition, it must implement the necessary preventive measures to mitigate business relationship risks and set the necessary procedures and measures to verify and review the customer information obtained, including\n                    the information provided by the customer, using reliable and independent electronic services\".\n                \n                \n                    It follows that non-documentary identity verification is permissible for SAMA-regulated financial institutions to the extent it is carried out via \"reliable and independent\" government-maintained electronic sources, the only example\n                    explicitly named in the AML Guide being the National Information Center.\n                  \n                \n                \n                    \n                        1 — More information may be required under industry-specific regulations. E.g., source of income is necessary as per Article 3.3 of the 2019 Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing (AML/CTF) Guide; the\n                        2022 Rules for Bank Accounts prescribe to collect ID number and expiry date and employer name (if any); etc.\n                    \n                                          \n            \n              \n            \n                \n                    The\n                    \n                        Swiss Financial Market Supervisory Authority (\"FINMA\") Circular 2016/7 on \"Video and online identification\"\n                    \n                    (\"Circular\") sets out detailed guidance on remote client onboarding. Its provisions on the acceptable KYC procedures as regards natural persons are largely accumulated in Section III(A) (\"Video identification on a par\n                    with in-person identification for individuals\") and Section IV (\"Online identification\").\n                \n                \n                    Whereas Section III(A) requires \"audio-visual real-time (live transmission) communication\" and active verbal interaction between a user and the obliged entity, Section IV(B)(a) (\"Electronic copy of an identification document\n                    authenticated by the financial intermediary\") allows (subject to certain conditions) for an automated KYC flow. However, both Section III(A) and IV(B)(a) demand, as one of the necessary steps, nuanced analysis of the user's identity\n                    document; in particular, the obliged entity must:\n                \n                \n                    (i) read and decrypt the document's machine-readable zone;\n                    (ii) examine the document's optical security features, such as holographic-cinematic marks or printed elements with latent image / tilting effects;\n                    (iii) match the document with the other data provided by the user; and,\n                    (iv) if not familiar with the document, compare it with reference samples from an ID database referring to security features, character type and size, and layout.\n                \n                \n                    Likewise, alternative procedures described in Section IV(B)(b) (\"Electronic copy of an identification document with qualified electronic signature\") and IV(B)(c) (\"Digital authentication\") imply that the presentation of an identity\n                    document copy is a mandatory element of the KYC process.\n                \n                However, with regard to residential address specifically, Section IV(B)(a) of the Circular provides an exhaustive list of means by which it can be confirmed, naming among other things non-documentary evidence:\n                \"The financial intermediary must also confirm the contracting party's residential address by means of a:\n                \n                    tax invoice or any other official invoice or power, water or telephone invoices (utility bill);\n                    postal delivery;\n                    a public register, or a trustworthy, privately managed database/directory; or\n                    geolocation\".\n                \n                \n                    The same requirement is, however, not explicitly reiterated for the other forms of non-face-to-face KYC permitted by FINMA, specifically: (i) \"Video identification\"; or (ii) online identification performed via (a) \"Electronic copy of an\n                    identification document with qualified electronic signature\" or (b) \"Digital authentication\":\n                \n                \n                    \n                        for (ii)(b), FINMA provides that, \"if the authentication provider confirms the identification document's authenticity at the same address which was provided by the contracting party to the financial intermediary during the\n                        onboarding process as the contracting party's place of residence, and if confirmation of authenticity can be verifiably demonstrated from documentation as having been performed at that address, the authentication issued in this way\n                        also serves as a confirmation of domicile under Article 45 para. 2 AMLO-FINMA\" - without explicitly limiting other alternative options for verifying residential address;\n                    \n                    \n                        for (i) and (ii)(a), FINMA does not elaborate on address verification at all. Accordingly, two interpretations are possible:\n                        \n                            \n                                obliged entities should defer to Article 45(2) of the AMLO-FINMA (Anti-Money Laundering Ordinance-FINMA), prescribing that, \"if the business relationship is\n                                initiated without a personal consultation, the financial intermediary will also check the residential address by postal delivery or in another equivalent manner and will keep a certified copy of the\n                                identification document on file\"; or\n                            \n                            obliged entities may choose any means of address verification recognized by FINMA at their discretion unless the Circular explicitly prescribes otherwise.\n                        \n                    \n                \n                The latter approach appears more reflective of the regulator's intentions. As follows from Section I of the Circular, it is issued \"in application of Article 3 para. 2 of AMLO-FINMA\", which, in turn, states:\n                \n                    \"When applying this Ordinance, FINMA may take into account the particularities of the business activities of financial intermediaries and, in particular, allow for relaxations or order stricter measures based on the money laundering\n                    risk of an activity or the size of the company. It may also take into account the development of new technologies that offer equivalent security for the implementation of due diligence obligations\".\n                \n                \n                    Therefore, the Circular should be regarded as the source potentially prevailing over the AMLO-FINMA on matters related to remote identity verification. This is supported by Section VIII stating that \"the wording in [certain] AMLO-FINMA\n                    articles extends to the following formats in a digital context\" and, inter alia, listing its Section IV(B) as a respective adjustment of Article 45(2) of the AMLO-FINMA. From this, it may be inferred that FINMA considers such methods of\n                    address confirmation as geolocation detection or reference to external databases as \"equivalent to postal delivery\" where a customer is onboarded remotely, even if they are only explicitly mentioned in Section IV(B)(a).\n                \n                \n                    Therefore, the Circular does not envisage non-face-to-face onboarding procedures that do not involve compulsory document-based checks; it also does not mention the possibility of verifying a client's identity solely by referring to\n                    external data sources (with the exception of address verification). Since all the KYC measures considered fully compliant are listed in the Circular, any alternative solutions (such as Non-Doc KYC) may only be implemented in combination\n                    with those explicitly prescribed by FINMA.\n                \n            \n\n            \n                To verify the identity and address of natural persons in Taiwan, financial institutions and other obliged entities must follow specific Customer Due Diligence (the “CDD”) procedures,  primarily governed by the Money Laundering Control Act and Financial Supervisory Commission Regulations Governing Anti-Money Laundering (the “Regulations”).\n                According to paragraph 4 of Article 3 of the Regulations:\n                “The CDD measures to be taken by a financial institution shall be as follows:\n                (1) Identifying the customer and verifying that customer’s identity using reliable, independent source documents, data or information. In addition, a financial institution shall retain copies of the customer’s identity documents or record the relevant information thereon.”\n                The specific requirements to identity verification methods to be used in the course of CDD are further detailed in industry-specific regulations as described below.\n                1. E-payment institutions \n                According to Article 8 of the Regulations Governing Identity Verification Mechanism and Transaction Limits for Users of Electronic Payment Processing Institutions (the ‘e-payment account Regulations’) (the “E-payment Account Regulations”), e-payment institutions shall gather at least the following information on users:\n                \n                  Name\n                  Nationality\n                  Type and number of identification document\n                  Date of birth.\n                \n                The CDD process for opening e-payment accounts is prescribed by the E-payment Account Regulations.\n                Even though the Regulations do not limit the CDD measures to documentary method only, the specific E-payment Account Regulations do establish the documentary process for verification of the user’s identity.\n                The E-payment Account Regulations establish different flows for the identity verification process depending on the account type (Type 1, Type 2, or Type 3), however, irrespective of the account type, the e-payment institution shall obtain a document to verify the identity information.\n                “Article 9\n                When an electronic payment institution accepts individual users to register and open a Type 1 e-payment account, its identity verification process shall comply with the following provisions:\n                \n                 Verifying the mobile phone number provided by the user: Verify that the user can use the said mobile phone number to operate and receive messages and notifications.\n                 Verifying the veracity of the user’s identity information. The verification method shall comply with any one of the criteria set forth below:\n                 \n                   (1) When a national ID card is provided, the institution shall check the user’s ID card issuance records, name, and data with JCIC to verify data veracity;\n                   (2) When a resident certificate, vertical household registration certificate, or other identity document issued by a government agency is provided, the institution must check such documents with the Ministry of the Interior or the issuers of the said documents, the name and data with JCIC, or use other appropriate methods to verify the user’s name.\n                   \n                   \n                 Confirming the financial payment instrument used by the user.\n                 Verifying the user identity via over-the-counter review, a certificate that complies with the Electronic Signature Act, or video teller machine.\n                 \n                \n                The payment instrument referred to in Subparagraph 3 of the Paragraph 1 and Subparagraph 2 of the preceding paragraph shall be deposit accounts, credit cards, or other financial payment instruments recognized by the competent authority only. The said deposit accounts excludes Type 3 digital bank deposit accounts.\n                Article 10\n                When an electronic payment institution accepts individual users to register and open a Type 2 e-payment account, its identity verification process shall comply with the provisions in Subparagraphs 1 to 3, Paragraph 1 of the preceding article. The provisions in Paragraphs 2 and 3 of the preceding article shall apply mutatis mutandis to conditions specified in the preceding paragraph.\n                Article 11\n                When an electronic payment institution accepts individual users to register and open a Type 3 e-payment account, its identity verification process shall comply with the provisions in Subparagraphs 1 and 2, Paragraph 1 of Article 9.”\n                Therefore, for e-payment accounts opening the document verification is always required. Moreover in addition to the document verification, the following verification measures shall be performed, depending on account type:\n                \n                 for Type 1 electronic accounts:\n                  \n                   Verifying the mobile phone number\n                   Confirming the financial payment instrument (i.e. “penny-drop”)\n                   Verifying the user identity via\n                    \n                     over-the-counter review, or\n                     a certificate that complies with the Electronic Signature Act, or\n                     video teller machine (i.e. video verification)\n                     \n                    \n                 for Type 2  electronic accounts:\n                  \n                   Verifying the mobile phone number\n                   Confirming the financial payment instrument\n                    \n                 for Type 3 electronic accounts:\n                  \n                   Verifying the mobile phone number.\n                    \n                 \n                2. Virtual Asset Services\n                The minimum scope of identity verification to be performed by the VASPs is outlined in Article 3(4) of the Regulations Governing Anti-Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism for Enterprises or Persons Providing Virtual Asset Services (the “VASP Regulations”):\n                \n                 Name\n                 Official identity document number\n                 Date of birth\n                 Nationality\n                 Household registration or residence address.\n                \n                VASPs shall comply with the CDD measures that are stipulated by the VASP Regulations.\n                In more detail, Article 3 of the VASP Regulations mandates the following:\n                “\n                3. CDD measures shall be taken as follows:\n                A.Identifying the customer and verifying the customer’s identity using reliable, independent source documents, data or information.\n                \n                4. When the customer specified in the preceding subparagraph is a natural person, the VASP shall obtain at least the following information on the customer to identify and verify the customer’s identity:\n                \n                 Name.\n                 Official identity document number.\n                 Date of birth.\n                 Nationality.\n                 Household registration or residence address.\n                  \n                ”\n                Although Article 3(4) of the VASP Regulations does not explicitly mention the documents (unlike, for example, the E-payment Account Regulations), it does require key identity details (name, ID number, DOB, nationality, address) to be obtained and verified — in our view, this does amount to documentary verification, unless a reliable non-documentary equivalent is clearly authorized. At the moment, we are not aware of such equivalents to be available in Taiwan.\n                3. Banking accounts\n                Paragraph IV of Article 4 of the Model Guidelines for Banks’ Anti-Money Laundering and Counter Terrorism Financing Policies and Procedures (the “Model Guidelines for Banks”) establishes the following scope of data by which an individual customer must be identified (please note that additional data may be required with respect to the customers that are identified as high-risk):\n                “VIII. For a customer with whom a bank establishes business relationship, the bank should take following measures to verify the identity of the customer :\n                \n                 Name\n                 Date of birth\n                 Permanent or residence address\n                 Official identification number\n                 Nationality\n                 The purpose of residence of a foreign person.\n                  \n                Paragraph VIII of Article 4 of the Model Guidelines for Banks establishes  the documentary verification method as principal one, while and non-documentary methods could be applied if necessary:\n                “VIII. For a customer with whom a bank establishes business relationship, the bank should take following measures to verify the identity of the customer :\n                (I) Verification through documents:\n                1. Individual:\n                \n                 (1) Verification of identity or date of birth: obtain an unexpired official identification document that bears a photograph of the individual (e.g. identification card, passport, residence card, driving license, etc.). If there is doubt as to the validity of such documents, a bank should obtain certification provided by an embassy official or a public notary. \n                 (2) Verification of address: obtain bills, account statements, or official documents, etc. from the individual.\n                \n                (II) Verification through nondocumentary methods (if necessary), for example:\n                \n                 Contacting the customer by telephone or letter after an account has been opened.\n                 Checking references provided by other financial institutions.\n                 Cross-checking information provided by the customer with other reliable public information or private database, etc.”\n                \n                Therefore, the banks shall also use the documentary verification methods, while the non-documentary methods could be used as complementary to the former methods.\n                General conclusion\n                Although the primary legislation seem to permit non-documentary verification in principle, industry-specific regulations either explicitly limit the acceptable verification methods to documentary ones, or impose practical limitations that make documentary verification a functional necessity.\n\n\n            \n          \n            \n                \n                    The Tanzanian Anti-Money Laundering Regulations, 2022 (\"AML Regulations\") delineate specific \"basic personal\n                    information\" that must undergo verification and subsequent record-keeping, with a particular focus on documentary evidence:\n                \n                \"basic personal information\" means information concerning a natural person including-\n                \n                    (a) full name;\n                    (b) gender;\n                    (c) nationality;\n                    (d) date of birth;\n                    (e) place of birth;\n                    (f) the residential address which may be expressed in terms of physical address or in the absence of a physical address, such details as neighborhood or locality or village, town or city, district or region and country;\n                    (g) identification document indicating the type of document and number;\n                    (h) means of communication;\n                    (i) signature or biometric information (Part I(2)).\n                \n                \n                    Part III(8) of the AML Regulations further reiterates that customer identification and verification measures must be conducted based on an identity document, while Part I(2) prescribes how exactly such a document is supposed to be\n                    analysed:\n                \n                \n                    \"(a) the primary identification document shall be the national identity card;\n                    \n                        (b) in the absence of a document referred in subparagraph (a), other identification documents as defined in these Regulations1 shall be accepted in the following exceptional circumstances:\n                        \n                            (i) where the customer is not a citizen or resident of the United Republic and there is no\n                            possibility for them to get the national identity card;\n                            (ii) where existing laws do not permit the customer to acquire a national identity card, including the customer having not attained the permissible age to get a national identity card;\n                            (iii) the national identity card is lost and the customer has presented to the reporting person a Police loss report;\n                            (iv) where the customer is categorized as low risk in money laundering, terrorist financing and proliferation financing risk assessment\" (Part III(8));\n                        \n                    \n                \n                \"basic verification\" means verifying the identity of the customer as follows:\n                \n                    \n                        (a) taking reasonable measures to inspect and confirm visually and through touch and feel, the authenticity of the original or copy of the identification document or entity registration documents;\n                    \n                    (b) ensuring that the photograph and any other information on the identification document, entity registration documents or its copy are clearly visible and legible;\n                    (c) taking reasonable measures to compare and confirm that the photograph is indeed that of the natural person;\n                    (d) where a customer is a natural person, ensuring that the personal information provided is accurate, through comparison of such information with information on the identification documents\" (Part I(2)).\n                \n                \n                    Therefore, the outlined approach, requiring that an ID copy be always submitted and subsequently inspected as a necessary precondition for customer onboarding, essentially rules out the possibility of relying on non-documentary KYC as a\n                    standalone method.\n                \n                \n                \n                    1 — As per Part I(2) of the AML Regulations, \"identification document\" includes -\n                    \n                        (a) national identity card;\n                        (b) passport or an equivalent travel document;\n                        (c) driving license;\n                        (d) voter's registration card;\n                        (e) employee identity document;\n                        (f) student identity document.\n                    \n                                \n            \n\n            \n                \n                    Vietnam's 2022Law on Anti-Money Laundering(\"AML Law\") establishes сustomer due diligence (CDD) procedures\n                    applicable to AML-subject entities, including those related to customer identification and identity verification.\n                \n                Pursuant to Article 10 of the AML Law, reporting subjects must collect identity data of individual customers depending on their nationality and residence:\n                \"1. Customer identification information, including information about the individual customer's representative (if any):\n                \n                    \n                        a) For individual customers whose nationality is Vietnamese: full name; date of birth; nationality; profession, job position; phone number; ID card number or Citizen Identification Number or personal identification number or\n                        passport number, date of issue, place of issue; permanent residence registration address and other current residence (if any);\n                    \n                    \n                        b) For individual customers with one nationality who are foreigners residing in Vietnam: full name; date of birth; nationality; profession, job position; phone number; passport number, date of issue, place of issue; entry visa\n                        number, except in cases of visa exemption as prescribed by law; residential address abroad and registered residence address in Vietnam;\n                    \n                    \n                        c) For individual customers with one nationality who are foreigners not residing in Vietnam: full name; date of birth; nationality; profession, job position; passport number or identification number issued by a foreign competent\n                        authority, date of issue, place of issue; residential address abroad;\n                    \n                    d) For individual customers who have two or more nationalities: corresponding information specified in point a, b or c of this clause; nationality, residential address in the country of the other nationality;\n                    \n                        dd) For individual customers who are stateless: full name; date of birth; profession, job position; number of the document valid for international travel (if any), visa number; entry visa-issuing agency, except in cases of visa\n                        exemption as prescribed by law; residence address abroad (if any), residence registration address in Vietnam\".\n                    \n                \n                In addition, Article 12 of the AML Act lists the following means for verifying the information referred to above:\n                \n                    \n                        Reporting subjects use documents and other data to verify customer identification information, including:\n                        \n                            a) For individual customers: ID card, citizen identification card or valid passport; other documents issued by competent authorities; [...]\n                        \n                    \n                    \n                        Reporting subjects can exploit information in national databases according to the provisions of law, through competent state agencies and other organizations specified in Article 13 [a third-party provider engaged\n                        by the reporting subject] or regulated third parties specified in Article 14 [a financial institution or a legal entity in a related non-financial industry that has established relationships with customers (excluding agency and\n                        outsourcing relationships); conducts CDD according to the AML Act of, for foreign entities, the FATF recommendations; is subject to the management and supervision of a competent authority] of this Law to compare and verify\n                        information provided by customers\".\n                    \n                \n                \n                    It is not entirely obvious from the text of Article 12 whether (i) paras. 1 and 2 are alternative or (ii) para. 1 is obligatory, while para. 2 only sets out a possible supplementary means of data validation. At the same time, secondary\n                    legislation and industry-specific guidance, together with the current market practice, seem to suggest that, while reliance on national identity databases is permissible and even obligatory in certain instances, the only way of\n                    validating the customer's identity via such databases remains to present an identity document. .\n                \n                \n                    For instance, the\n                    \n                        2023 Decision on Implementing Safety and Security in Online Payment and Bank Card Payment\n                    \n                    requires \"e-authentication\" for certain types of transactions, e.g.:\"Credit institutions, foreign bank branches, and payment intermediary service providers must implement solutions to minimize risks in online payments as follows:\n                \n                \n                    \n                        For individual customers, before making the first transaction using the Mobile Banking application or before making a transaction on a device different from the device that made the last Mobile Banking transaction, the customer must\n                        be authenticated:\n                    \n                \n                \n                    \n                        By the customer's biometric identification mark: (i) matching the biometric data stored in the chip of the customer's CCCD card issued by the Public Security agency; (ii) or through authentication of the customer's electronic\n                        identification account created by the electronic identification and authentication system;\n                    \n                    \n                        Or by the customer's biometric identification mark matching the biometric data stored in the collected and verified customer biometric database, combined with the OTP authentication method sent via SMS/Voice or Soft OTP/Token OTP\"\n                        (Art. 2).\n                    \n                \n                \n                    Likewise, Art. 12 of\n                    Circular 17/2024/TT-NHNN\n                    (State Bank of Vietnam, Regulations on opening and Using Payment Accounts at Payment Service Provider) further implies that, for both Vietnamese and foreign citizens, identity verification should be achieved either via documentary or a\n                    combination of documentary and electronic means:\n                \n                \n                    \n                        The payment account opening file includes the following documents, information and data:\n                        \n                            a) Agreement to open and use payment accounts as prescribed in Article 13 of this Circular;\n                            b) Documents, information, and data to verify customer identification information as prescribed in Clauses 2 and 3 of this Article; [...]\n                        \n                    \n                    \n                        Documents, information, data on personal identification papers of individual customers:\n                        \n                            \n                                a) In case the individual is a Vietnamese citizen: Citizen Identification Card, Identity Card or Electronic Identity Card (through accessing the level 02 electronic identification account) or Identity Card\n                                or Birth Certificate for people under 14 years old;\n                            \n                            b) In case the individual is of Vietnamese origin and nationality is not yet determined: Identity card;\n                            \n                                c) In case the individual is a foreigner:\n                                \n                                    (i) Passport, for foreigners residing in Vietnam, there must be an additional entry visa or document of equivalent value to a visa or document proving visa exemption; or\n                                    (ii) Electronic identity (through access to a level 02 electronic identification account)\".\n                                \n                            \n                        \n                    \n                \n                \n                    This is further confirmed in Art. 161 of the same Circular, specifying that regulated entities' internal procedures on electronic account opening should include, inter alia, collection of the account owners' (where they are natural\n                    persons) biometric data, as well as its subsequent matching against:\n                \n                \n                    \n                        \"(i) Biometric data stored in the encrypted information storage unit of the citizen identification card or the identity card that has been accurately authenticated as issued by the police agency\n                        or through authentication of that person's electronic identification account created by the Electronic Identification and Authentication System; or\n                    \n                    \n                        (ii) Biometric data has been collected and checked (ensuring the correct match between the person's biometric data and the biometric data in the encrypted information storage of the citizen identification card or the identity card\n                        has been accurately authenticated as being issued by the Public Security agency or with the person's biometric data through the\n                        authentication of the electronic identification account created by the Electronic Identification and Authentication System)\".\n                    \n                \n                \n                    In turn, the meaning behind \"level 02 electronic identification account\" is clarified in Art. 20 of Decree 69/2024/ND-CP as\n                    follows:\n                \n                \"1. The electronic identification and authentication system provides the following levels of electronic identification account authentication: [...]\n                \n                    b) Level 02: Electronic identification account authentication is performed based on two different authentication factors [authentication means used to confirm and accurately affirm the electronic identity subject before accessing and\n                    exploiting information in the electronic identification and authentication system] and the corresponding authentication means in Clause 8, Article 3 of this Decree [methods that allow users to use to perform electronic authentication:\n                    password, secret code, barcode, terminal, one-time password device or software, cryptographic device or software, identity card, citizen identification card, passport, facial photo, fingerprint, voice, iris or other tools and methods\n                    used for the purpose of electronic authentication], which does not include biometric information\".\n                \n                Importantly, Arts. 3(9) and 22(2) of the same Decree emphasize that electronic authentication services may only be conducted by \"a public service unit or enterprise [authorized by] the Ministry of Public Security\".\n                \n                    Consequently, e-verification and e-authentication appear not only permissible, but mandatory in at least two scenarios: (i) the customer opens a payment account using their Electronic Identity or Electronic Identity Card; and (ii) the\n                    customer conducts certain types of transactions as listed in the 2023 Decision on Implementing Safety and Security in Online Payment and Bank Card Payment. However, this KYC method must be enabled by an entity duly authorized by the\n                    Ministry of Public Security.\n                    \n                        Furthermore, at present, the underlying national databases leveraged to perform e-verification and e-authentication are only accessible by using the data extracted from an eID with an embedded NFC chip (which makes a fully\n                        non-documentary flow impossible in practice)\n                    \n                    . Also, additional mechanisms (e.g., a questionnaire) may need to be implemented in order to collect the necessary identity information that might not be contained in the consulted data source - this may apply to, e.g., residential\n                    address (which may be verified by both documentary and non-documentary means, subject to the regulated entity's choice).\n                     \n                \n                \n                    \n                        1 — Notably, Art. 16(3) also contains a carve-out for cases where an account may not be opened via electronic means: “a) Joint payment account; b) Foreign currency payment account; c) Individual customers as prescribed in Point b, c Clause 1 Article 11 of this Circular [persons under 15 years of age; persons with limited or lost civil act capacity according to the provisions of Vietnamese law; persons with difficulty in cognition and behavior control according to the provisions of Vietnamese law], individual customers who are people from 15 years old to under 18 years old”.\n                    \n                                       \n            \n\n        \n    \n"
}
[/block]

================================================================================

# Non-Doc Address Verification
URL: https://docs.sumsub.com/docs/non-doc-address-verification

Sumsub’s Non-Document Address Verification solution helps businesses verify their customers’ residency data by leveraging reliable government and commercial sources in more than 30+ countries worldwide.

It works by either asking the user to submit a few unique identifiable elements, which are used to instantly extract their complete address information, or by matching their detailed residency data against one or two authoritative sources. The result enables Sumsub’s clients to simplify and speed up their customers' onboarding processes while simultaneously keeping operational costs at a minimum.

The overall solution helps regulated organizations meet their regulatory compliance requirements on verifying customer residency data against authoritative sources and determining both the legitimacy as well as liveness of their identity. At the same time, it helps optimize traditional address verification methods by increasing customer conversion rates up to 85%, minimizing the processing time to an average of 4.5 seconds, and reducing all operational efforts required for manual document reviews. 

These values make Non-Document Address Verification a reliable alternative to document-based residency checks that not only delivers our clients a high level of identity data assurance and fraud prevention but also enables maximum legitimate customer coverage through an effortless user experience, all while staying compliant with national regulatory requirements.

The supported data verification methods for this solution are:

- Data Enrichment — comprehensive user address data enrichment that verifies unique personal identifiers against authoritative government, banking, and credit sources.
- 1x1/2x2 Matching — screening user’s address data against a number of reliable databases until it matches either one or two of the sources in their country.

> 👍 Ideal for:
> 
> - Ensuring regulatory compliance
> - Streamlining customer onboarding
> - Preventing Identity fraud 
> - Reducing operational costs and resources

# Check availability by country

The following is a list of countries where Non-Document Address Verification is currently available:

> Note that this list is constantly updated and expanded.

> 📘 Note
> 
> Sumsub currently supports two data verification methods: Enrichment and 1x1/2x2 Matching. You can customize these on the go based on the country, population coverage, and regulatory requirements.
> 
> - To get started with Enrichment, reach out to your Customer Success Manager or Support team.
> - To get started with 1x1/2x2 Matching, follow the instructions provided in this article. Read more about the decision-making logic behind these methods in this article.

# Get started with Non-Doc Address Verification

compliance assessment of our Non-Doc Address Verification solution conducted for over 35\n        markets.\n    \n  \n  \n\n"
}
[/block]

# Explore alternative methods for Address Verification

================================================================================

# Get started with Non-Doc Address Verification
URL: https://docs.sumsub.com/docs/get-started-address-verification

To start using Non-Doc Address Verification, you can opt for:

- Code-free integration involving our SDKs.
- API integration.

> 📘 Note
> 
> Non-Doc Address Verification comes at an additional cost regardless of the integration method. Make sure to contact us before starting the integration.

# Verify address via SDK

Use our Web and Mobile SDKs to conduct Non-Doc Address Verification code-free:

1. Set up the integration, as described in the WebSDK integration article and MobileSDK integration article.
2. Create a verification level, add an Applicant data verification step, and submit the data as per table references.
3. Conduct verification and handle verification results.

# Verify address via API

You can utilize our public API endpoints to conduct Address Verification using the Non-Doc solution:

1. Create a verification level with an Applicant data step.
2. Include the Required fields as specified in the table and add Recommended/Optional fields to increase the positive impact on pass rates. 
3. Create an applicant and pass the level name created at step 1 as the `levelName` query parameter.

```curl Request
curl -X POST \
    'https://api.sumsub.com/resources/applicants?levelName=non-doc-address-2x2' \
    -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
    -d '{
          "externalUserId": "someUniqueUserId",
          "metadata": [
            {
              "key": "documentNumber",
              "value": "12345678"
            }
          ],
          "fixedInfo": {
            "firstName": "Franco Manuel",
            "lastName": "Martinez",
            "dob": "1990-02-13",
            "country": "ARG",
            "phone": "+54 1234 5678",
            "addresses": [
              {
                "street": "Corrientes",
                "buildingNumber": "1538",
                "town": "Villa Carlos Paz",
                "state": "Córdoba",
                "postCode": "X5152",
                "country": "ARG"
              }
            ]
          }
        }'
```
```json Response
{
  "id": "668b827860f280647fa07091",
  "createdAt": "2024-07-08 06:08:56",
  "key": "DGATQKRNEXWUCD",
  "clientId": "ClientName",
  "inspectionId": "668b827860f280647fa07092",
  "externalUserId": "htr23wq435trujasdgdgdf",
  "info": { },
  "fixedInfo": {
    "firstName": "Franco Manuel",
    "lastName": "Martinez",

```

> 🚧 Important
> 
> Pay attention to the following fields from the table reference:
> 
> - The National ID number must be provided in the metadata as the `documentNumber` field.
>   ```json
>   "metadata": [
>     {
>       "key": "documentNumber",
>       "value": "12345678"
>     }
>   ]
>   ```
> - The Tax Identification number must be provided as `TIN` in the `.fixedInfo` object.

4. Request an applicant check.

```http Request
curl -X POST \
  'https://api.sumsub.com/resources/applicants/5b73b82f0a975a3b46429758/status/pending'
```
```json Response
{
  "ok": 1
}
```

5. Handle verification results.

# Fallback to standard verification

In case something goes wrong during Non-Doc Address Verification or you just want to verify applicants using the standard document verification approach:

- For SDKs, use the Workflow Builder to configure the verification flow as necessary. 
- For the API, If you already have a complex logic on your side, move the applicants to a desired level by using this API method.

================================================================================

# Handle Non-Doc Address Verification results
URL: https://docs.sumsub.com/docs/handle-results-address-verification

Once the check is performed, you will receive a webhook with the verification status:

- The `reviewResult` object contains extra information about verification.
- The `reviewAnswer` field contains the final verification result, displaying the GREEN or RED status: 
  - GREEN — the applicant has passed Non-Doc Address Verification, and their data has been successfully obtained from the government database.
  - RED — the applicant has not passed verification; the `reviewRejectType` is set to `RETRY` or `FINAL`.

# Get verification check details

To get a structured view of the applicant data, use this API method with the `type` parameter set to `RESIDENCYCHECK`.

```curl
curl --request GET \
     --url 'https://api.sumsub.com/resources/checks/latest?type=RESIDENCYCHECK&applicantId=668b827860f280647fa07091' \
```

Example response:

# Clarify rejection reason

For every rejection reason, we have a particular `buttonId` attached to the applicant or photo. To get a list of rejection reasons based on the applicant ID, use this API method.

| Rejection type | Value                         | Rejection label     | Rejection reason                                                     |
| -------------- | ----------------------------- | :------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `FINAL`        | `dbNetRejectdataNotFound`    | `DBDATANOTFOUND` | The data provided was not found.                                     |
| `FINAL`        | `dbNetRejectdataMismatch`    | `DBDATAMISMATCH`  | The provided data does not match the data from the sources.          |
| `FINAL`        | `dbNetRejectnameMismatch`    | `DBDATAMISMATCH`  | The provided name does not match the data from the sources.          |
| `FINAL`        | `dbNetRejectaddressMismatch` | `DBDATAMISMATCH`  | The provided address does not match the data from the sources.       |
| `FINAL`        | `dbNetRejectdobMismatch`     | `DBDATAMISMATCH`  | The provided date of birth does not match the data from the sources. |

> 😀 Did you know?
> 
> When applicants skip or fail Non-Doc Address Verification, they can be moved to another flow for passing verification using documents.

================================================================================

# Explore Matching methods
URL: https://docs.sumsub.com/docs/matching-configurations

This section indicates the decision-making rules (matching configurations) for our Non-Doc Address Verification solution that are applicable to both 1x1 and 2x2 Matching methods. These rules have been pre-set to deliver the highest user approval rates while maintaining strong levels of data verification security:

- The 1x1 Matching method under the Non-Doc Address Verification solution requires matching the user’s personal and residency data against a single source to receive a Full Match on their address. The total number of available sources for data screening is dependent on the user’s country of onboarding.
- The 2x2 Matching method under the Non-Doc Address Verification solution requires matching the user’s personal and residency data against two sources to receive a Full Match on their address. The total number of available sources for data screening is dependent on the user’s country of onboarding.

> The matching rules are standard for all Sumsub clients and not available for individual configuration. Nonetheless, Sumsub is continuously improving the solution to maximize population coverage and increase the match rates regarding data validation.

# Non-Doc Address Verification

# Non-Doc Address Verification in the UK

# Data category and data attribute matching

The following are case scenarios which result in a Full Match regarding Name, Date of Birth, ID, and Address data categories.

A Partial Match on the Name and Address data categories is considered such when a Full Match is discovered on either one of the two data attributes from the case scenarios below. A Partial Match on the Name category based solely on the First Initial data attribute is not possible. A Partial Match on the ID and Date of Birth categories is not possible.

## Name category matching scenarios

A Full Match on the Name category is considered such when any of the following data attribute matching scenarios is met:

- First Name Full Match + Last Name Full Match
- First Name Full Match + Maternal Name Full Match
- First Name Full Match + Paternal Name Full Match
- First Initial Full Match + Maternal Name Full Match
- First Initial Full Match + Paternal Name Full Match
- First Initial Full Match + Last Name Full Match
- First Name Full Match + Maternal Name Full Match + Paternal Name Full Match
- First Initial Full Match + Maternal Name Full Match + Paternal Name Full Match

A Partial Match on the Name category is considered such when any of the following data attribute matching scenarios is met:

- First Name Full Match
- Last Name Full Match
- Paternal Name Full Match
- Maternal Name Full Match
- Maternal Name Full Match + Paternal Name Full Match

## Address category matching scenarios

A Full Match on the Address category is considered such when any of the following data attribute matching scenarios is met:

- Building Number Full Match + Postal Code Full Match
- Street Full Match + City Full Match
- Street Full Match + Postal Code Full Match
- Street Full Match + Building Number Full Match + City Full Match
- Street Full Match + Building Number Full Match + Postal Code Full Match

A Partial Match on the Address category is considered such when any of the following data attribute matching scenarios is met:

- Street Full Match
- Building Number Full Match
- Postal Code Full Match
- City Full Match

> Note that certain address attributes can be specific to regions/countries.

## Date of birth category matching scenarios

A Full Match on the Date of Birth category is considered such when the following data attribute matching scenario is met:

- Year of Birth Full Match + Month of Birth Full Match + Day of Birth Full Match

> Note that certain Date of Birth attribute matching exceptions may be applicable to regions/countries.

## ID category matching scenarios

A Full Match on the ID category is considered such when the following data attribute matching scenario is met:

- Identification Number Full Match

> A Partial data category Match is considered such when a Match is discovered on either one of the two data attributes from the case scenarios above. A Partial ID category Match is not possible. A Partial Match in the Name category based solely on the First Initial data attribute is not accepted.

# Fuzzy matching for data attributes

[block:html]

  "html": "\n    .footer-container {\n        display: grid;\n        grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr);\n        gap: 0px;\n        margin-bottom: 0px;\n        padding-top: 0px;\n  }\n\n    --- .table-text {\n        margin: 24px 0;\n        border: 1px solid #e5e5e5;\n        border-radius: 8px;\n        overflow: hidden;\n    }\n\n    .wide-column {\n        min-width:      border: 1px solid #e5e5e5;\n        }\n    }\n\n    [data-color-mode=\"dark\"] th {\n        background-color: #303b42;\n        border: 1px solid #e5e5e5;\n    }\n\n    td {\n        border: 1px #f8f8f8;\n    }\n\n    .table-text h2 {\n        font-size: 14px;\n        margin: 0px;\n        color: #384248;\n    }\n\n    @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {\n        [data-color-mode=\"auto\"] .table-text h2 {\n            color: #ffff;\n        }\n    }\n\n    [data-color-mode=\"dark\"] .table-text h2 {\n        color: #ffff;\n    }\n\n    .table-text p {\n        font-size: 14px;\n        margin: 0;\n        color: #414552;\n    }\n\n    @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {\n        [data-color-mode=\"auto\"] .table-text p {\n            color: #ffff;\n        }\n    }\n\n    [data-color-mode=\"dark\"] .table-text p {\n        color: #ffff;\n    }\n\n    .table-text a {\n        color: #377cf7;\n        text-decoration: underline;\n    }\n\n    @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {\n        [data-color-mode=\"auto\"] .table-text a {\n            color: #ffff;\n        }\n    }\n\n    [data-color-mode=\"dark\"] .table-text a {\n        color: #ffff;\n    }\n\n    .table-text tr:first-child {\n        margin-bottom: 20px;\n    }\n\nThese are the criteria for fuzzy matching on different data attributes against country sources:\n\n\n    \n        \n            Name Data Attributes \n            \n            \n                For a Name category data attribute to generate a Full Match, it should be within 70% of the Levenshtein character similarity in comparison to the records in the data sources. E.g., if the Name attribute input is\n                ‘Christophel’ and the actual name is ‘Christopher’, Global Database Verification returns a Full Match based on the fuzzy matching logic. This system is applicable to the First Name and Last Name data\n                attributes.\n            \n        \n        \n            Address Data Attributes\n            \n            \n                For Address category data attributes like Street, City, State, and Postal Code, the acceptable Levenshtein character similarity percentage for a Full Match is also 70% in comparison to the\n                records in the data sources. E.g., a Street and House Number attribute input of ‘200 Kingslee Court’ would Match against a record in the data source for ‘200 Kingsley Court’.\n            \n        \n        \n            Date of Birth Data Attributes\n            \n            Sumsub does not employ any kind of fuzzy matching on data attributes within the ID data category (Year of Birth, Month of Birth, Day of Birth).\n        \n        \n            ID Data Attributes \n            \n            Sumsub does not employ any kind of fuzzy matching on data attributes within the ID data category (Identification Number).\n        \n    \n\n\n\n    Examples\n\n    Below are examples of data attribute inputs that would satisfy the criteria of Global Database Verification fuzzy matching logic and generate a Full Match.\n\n    \n        \n            \n                \n                    Data Attribute\n                    Input\n                    Data Source Record\n                    Result\n                \n                        state\n                    \n                    \n                        Perip\n                    \n                    \n                        Paris\n }
[/block]

================================================================================

# Solution compliance assessment
URL: https://docs.sumsub.com/docs/solution-compliance-assessment-address-verification
