500;\n        font-size: 10px;\n        line-height: 16px;\n        color: #613400;\n        margin-top: 4px;\n        text-wrap: nowrap;\n    }\n\n\n\n    Country:\n    \n        🇦🇹 Austria\n        🇧🇪 Belgium\n        🇨🇿 Czech Republic\n        🇫🇷 France\n        🇩🇪 Germany\n        🇮🇹 Italy\n        🇳🇱 Netherlands\n        🇵🇱 Poland\n        🇪🇸 Spain\n        🇨🇭 Switzerland\n    \n\n    \n        1. Acceptable KYC methods for AML/CFT purposes:\n        Pursuant to Article 6(4) subsection 1-4 of the Financial Markets Anti-Money Laundering Act (FM-GwG - Finanzmarkt-Geldwäschegesetz), the following remote identity verification methods are permissible:\n        \n            the presentation in a video-based electronic procedure of the official photo identification document (online identification),\n            a statutorily prescribed procedure, which ensures that the same information would be made available as would be if an official photo identification document were presented (electronic ID card),\n            the submission of a legally binding declaration by the customer in the form of a qualified electronic signature in accordance with point 12 of Article 3 of Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 or the delivery of a\n                legally binding declaration by the obliged entity via registered mail to the customer address given as the place of residence or place of incorporation [...]; or\n            the first payment during transactions is settled through an account opened in the customer's name with a credit institution [...] and copies of customer documents are available, on the basis of which the information provided\n                by the customer or the natural person authorised to represent the customer may be verified in a credible manner. In lieu of such copies, a written declaration from the credit institution through which the first payment is\n                intended to be made shall be sufficient for determining and verifying the identity of the customer as defined in this federal act or Directive (EU) 2015/849\".\n        \n        \n            In further implementation of Article 6(4), the FMA Online Identification Regulation provides for two means of video-based online identification:\n        \n        \n            \n                live video conference with a human operator, including taking screenshots of the customer's identity document and face, requesting the customer to move their head in a specific manner and communicate certain information,\n                analysing the ID images, etc.; and\n            \n        \n        \n            \n                biometric identification processes, or \"online identification processes, in which the entire online identification process or individual steps thereof are conducted by means of an automated electronic process without the\n                participation of a staff member\" - corresponding to a liveness check along with face match and ID authenticity analysis, provided that only an ID \"for which the content has been electronically signed by the issuing\n                authority\" shall be acceptable.\n            \n        \n        2. Additional use cases for Qualified Signing:\n        \n            Civil Transactions\n            \n                Section 886 of the Austrian Civil Code (Allgemeines Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch or ABGB) states that a QES fulfills the requirement of written form and is equivalent to a\n                handwritten signature.\n            \n            Furthermore, certain transactions or contracts require a QES compulsorily where written form is demanded by law and there is no handwritten signature, for example:\n            \n                stand-alone promises to fulfill a third party's obligation if made by individuals outside their commercial or professional activities;\n                agreements concerning Austrian real estate, e.g. mortgage agreements, sale and purchase agreements;\n                certain documents concerning Austrian limited liability companies;\n                \n                    powers of attorney concerning transactions with special-form requirements, e.g. sale of real estate, establishment of a limited liability company, powers of sale under real estate mortgages or under share pledge\n                    agreements, voting proxies under a share pledge agreement; and\n                \n                certain sureties and guarantees.\n            \n        \n        \n            Employment Agreements with Apprentices\n            QES is required for signing employment agreements with apprentices according to the Vocational Training Act (Berufsausbildungsgesetz), section 20(7).\n        \n        \n            Ownership of Employee Inventions agreements\n            \n                QES is necessary for agreements regarding ownership of inventions made by employees as specified in the\n                Austrian Patent Act.\n            \n        \n\n        \n            Administrative and judicial documentation\n            \n                AES is mandated for documents created by judiciary and administrative authorities under the\n                Judicial Organization Act, Section 89.\n            \n        \n    \n\n    \n        1. Acceptable KYC methods for AML/CFT purposes:\n        \n            Pursuant to Art. 27(1) of the\n            Law on the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing and on the restriction of the use of cash (\"AML Law\"), reporting\n            entities are required to verify the identity of their customers against:\n        \n        \n            one or more supporting documents or reliable and independent sources of information enabling them to confirm [the identification data such as full name, date of birth, etc.];\n            where applicable, the information obtained through electronic identification means such as those provided or recognised within the authentication service as referred to in Articles 9 and 10 of the Law of 18 July 2017 on\n                electronic identification [implementing the eIDAS regulation and providing a regulatory framework for electronic identification in connection with digital public services in Belgium], confirming the identity of persons online;\n            \n            \n                where applicable, information obtained through relevant trust services referred to in Regulation 910/2014\".\n            \n        \n        Additionally:\n        \n            \n                Art. 28 of the AML Law provides a mechanism for remote identity verification by professional associations designated by the King, which are authorised to: \"1° use the identification number from the National Register; 2°\n                access the data of the National Register of natural persons referred to in Article 3 of the Law of 8 August 1983 establishing a National Register of natural persons; 3° make a paper or electronic copy of the information\n                consulted in said Register\";\n            \n            \n                the National Bank of Belgium Object of the identification and identity verification guidance (\"NBB\n                Guidance\") adopts a technologically neutral approach, emphasising that \"neither the Anti-Money Laundering Law nor the Anti-Money Laundering Regulation of the NBB lists in a precise, uniform and prescriptive manner the\n                supporting documents or the reliable and independent sources of information that can be used to fulfil the obligation to verify the identity of the persons involved\", while nevertheless explicitly endorsing certain specific\n                methods such as \"electronic identification schemes notified in accordance with Article 9 of the eIDAS Regulation and meeting the requirements of \"substantial\" or \"high\" levels of assurance\".\n            \n        \n        2. Additional use cases for Qualified Signing:\n        \n            \n                \n                    Civil Code of Belgium\n                \n                , Art. 5.30, establishes the functional equivalence between an e-signature of any type and a handwritten signature: \"the requirement of a writing is satisfied by a set of alphabetical signs or any other intelligible signs\n                affixed to a medium which allows access to it for a period of time appropriate to the purposes for which the information is intended and which preserves its integrity, whatever the medium and the means of transmission\".\n            \n            At the same time, QES is mandatory:\n            \n                as per Art. 8.15 of the Civil Code, for \"authentic instruments drawn up, received or served in electronic form by a public or ministerial officer\";\n                as per Art. 763 of the Judicial Code, where \"a record, report or other document is to be signed at the hearing by a person appearing at the hearing by videoconference\";\n                as per Art. 782 of the Judicial Code, where a judgment is established in electronic form;\n                as per Art. 3bis of the Employment Contracts Act of 3 July 1978, for employment contracts (where there is no handwritten signature);\n                as per Art. VII.78 of the Code of Economic Law, for consumer credit contracts (where there is no handwritten signature); etc.\n            \n        \n    \n\n    \n        1. Acceptable KYC methods for AML/CFT purposes:\n        \n            Section 8 of the AML Act states that the first identification of a customer who is an individual should be performed with (i) the said customer present in person and (ii) the obliged entity \"recording identification data and\n            verifying them from an identity card should they be included thereon, and subsequently recording the type and serial number of the identity card, the issuing country or issuing authority and the card's validity; at the same\n            time, [...] verifying the holder's appearance and the holder's facial image as pictured on the identity card\".\n        \n        \n            However, Section 8a(1) provides for an alternative so long as the substituting solution is either compliant with Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council on electronic identification and trust\n            services for electronic transactions in the internal market (\"eIDAS Regulation\") and the implementing regulations or prescribed by another legal act:\n        \n        \"An obliged entity may replace the process pursuant to section 8[...] by identification of a natural person who is a customer [...] performed by means of electronic identification which comply with the following:\n        \n            a) technical specification, standards, and procedures for a high level of assurance given by the directly applicable regulation of the European Union regulating minimum technical specifications, standards and\n            procedures for levels of assurance of means of electronic identification) and which is issued and applied pursuant to the qualified system in line with the Act on Electronic Identification, or\n        \n        \n            b) conditions pursuant to which means of electronic identification can be used for verification of identity required by a legal regulation or discharge of administrative responsibility outside the scope of the qualified system\n            pursuant to the Bank Act\".\n        \n        \n            Therefore, in order for Qualified Electronic Signatures (QES) to serve as an appropriate means of verification, they have to be integrated into an electronic identification scheme that adheres to the eIDAS standards for high\n            assurance. The eIDs currently notified by the Czech Republic that meet this criterion are the National Electronic Identity Card and the digital eID scheme MojeID.\n        \n        2. Additional use cases for Qualified Signing:\n        \n            \n                Civil Code\n            \n        \n        \n            As per § 561(1), handwritten signatures may in general be replaced with \"mechanical means where this is customary\", including e-signatures, provided that it should be possible to capture the content of the deed and identify the\n            signatory subsequently. Furthermore, handwritten signatures are typically not legally required, meaning that, in most cases, any form of e-signature can be a substitute. Notwithstanding this, AES or QES may, in the absence of a\n            \"wet\" signature, be recognized as stronger evidence compared to a simple e-signature where a contract or transaction must be drawn up in written form as per law, e.g.:\n        \n        \n            agreement on cancellation of co-ownership of real estate or business enterprise (Section 1141 of the Civil Code);\n            standalone acknowledgment of debt (Section 2053 of the Civil Code);\n            power of attorney unless the representation is related only to a particular legal act (441 of the Civil Code);\n            commercial agency agreement (Section 2483 of the Civil Code);\n            contract on a lease of residential real estate and notice on termination of lease of residential real estate (Sections 2237 and 2286 of the Civil Code); etc.\n        \n        \n            \n                Act No. 297/2016 Coll on trust services for electronic transactions\n            \n        \n        As per §§5-6, only a QES may be used for electronic documents signed by a legal entity in relation to a public law signatory or another person in connection with the exercise of their powers.\n    \n\n    \n        1. Acceptable KYC methods for AML/CFT purposes:\n        Under Article R561-5-1 of the French Monetary and Financial Code, the following methods of remote verification are allowed:\n        \n            \n                \"an electronic identification means certified or attested by the National Agency for the Security of Information Systems in accordance with the level of guarantee, either substantial or high, set by article 8 of Regulation\n                (EU) No 910/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 23 July 2014 on electronic identification and trust services for electronic transactions in the internal market\"; or\n            \n            \n                \"an electronic identification means issued within the framework of a scheme notified to the European Commission by a Member State of the European Union under the conditions provided for in paragraph 1 of Article 9 of this\n                regulation and whose level of guarantee corresponds to the level either substantial or high set by article 8 of the same regulation\".\n            \n        \n        If the methods listed above cannot be implemented, at least 2 measures out of those specified in Article R561-5-2 need to be selected instead:\n        \n            \"obtain a copy of a document mentioned in 3° or 4° of article R. 561-5-1 [valid official document including the customer's photograph]\";\n        \n        \n            \n                \"implement measures to verify and certify the copy of an official document or an extract from the official register mentioned in 3° or 4° of Article R. 561-5-1 by a third party independent of the person to be identified\n                [e.g., notaries]';\n            \n        \n        \n            \n                \"require that the first payment for transactions be made from or to an account opened in the client's name with a person mentioned in 1° to 6° bis of Article L. 561-2 [certain types of AML-regulated entities] that is\n                established in a Member State of the European Union or in a State party to the agreement on the European Economic Area or in a third country imposing equivalent obligations in terms of the fight against money laundering and\n                the financing of terrorism\";\n            \n        \n        \n            \n                \"obtain confirmation of the customer's identity directly from a third party fulfilling the conditions set out in 1° or 2° of I of Article L. 561-7\" [third party itself subject to AML/CFT laws and located in an EU/EEA country\n                or a third country imposing obligations equivalent to those contained in the Code, including those related to exchange of personal information];\n            \n        \n        \n            \n                \"use a service certified as compliant by the National Information Systems Security Agency, or a certification body authorized by this agency, at the level of substantial guarantee of the requirements relating to proof and\n                verification of identity, provided for in the appendix to the implementing regulation (EU) 2015/1502 of 8 September 2015\";\n            \n        \n        \n            \n                \"collect an advanced or qualified electronic signature or a valid advanced or qualified electronic seal based on a qualified certificate or use a qualified electronic registered delivery service bearing the identity of the\n                signatory or the creator of the seal and issued by a qualified trust service provider registered on a national trust list pursuant to Article 22 of Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 of July 23, 2014\" (Art. R561-5-2, 1-6°).\n            \n        \n        2. Additional use cases for Qualified Signing:\n        \n            \n                Articles 1366 and 1367 of the Civil Code of France provide a description of AES (advanced\n                electronic signature), which is a default necessary alternative to handwritten signature for civil transactions;\n            \n        \n        \n            \n                Décret n° 2017-1416 du 28 septembre 2017 relatif à la signature électronique establishes specific obligatory use cases for QES (where handwritten\n                signature is not used):\n            \n        \n        \n            \n                issuance of extracts or certificates by the Trade and Companies Register;\n                submission of documentation from organisations / individual entrepreneurs to state registration authorities electronically (does not include business creation files);\n                judgements drawn up on electronic media;\n                \n                    interaction between the National Public Health Agency, National Commission for Medical Accidents, National Agency for the Safety of Medicines and Health Products and organisations accountable to them (documentation,\n                    contacts, transfer of information on medical confidentiality);\n                \n                conclusion of contracts related to energy saving operations;\n                acts drawn up by notaries.\n            \n        \n    \n\n    \n        1. Acceptable KYC methods for AML/CFT purposes:\n        Pursuant to Section 12 of the German Anti-Money Laundering Act (GwG), the verification of a natural person's identity must be carried out on the basis of:\n        \n            \n                (1) \"a valid official identity document which includes a photograph of the holder and satisfies the passport and identification requirements in Germany, in particular a German passport, identity card or substitute of a\n                passport or identity card, or a passport, identity card or substitute of a passport or identity card recognised or accepted under German provisions for foreign nationals\" [the document must be presented on-site as per\n                Section 13 if it is the only ground for establishing identity];\n            \n            (2) \"an electronic proof of identity under section 18 of the Act on Identity Cards and Electronic Identification, section 12 of the eID Card Act or section 78 (5) of the Residence Act\",\n            (3) \"a qualified electronic signature under Article 3 No. 12 of the [eIDAS Regulation]\";\n            (4) \"a notified electronic identification scheme under Article 8(2)(c) [high level of assurance] in conjunction with Article 9 of Regulation (EU) No 910/2014\"; or\n            (5) \"documents under section 1 (1) of the Regulation determining documents admissible for the identification of a person to be identified under the Money Laundering Act for the purpose of opening of a payment account\".\n        \n        \n            Where the obliged entity relies on option (3), it must also ensure that \"a transaction is executed directly from a payment account within the meaning of section 1 (3) of the Payment Services Supervision Act which is held in the\n            name of the contracting party, with an obliged entity under section 2 (1) sentence 1 no. 1 or no. 3 or with a credit institution located in [a reliable country]\".\n        \n        \n            Additionally, Circular 3/2017 (GW) - video identification procedures by BaFIN prescribes a detailed video\n            conference procedure, with the active participation of a human operator, as an alternative means of remote KYC.\n        \n        2. Additional use cases for Qualified Signing:\n        \n            \n                the German Civil Code (BGB), Arts. 126 and 126(a), generally allows the use of QES (as well as other electronic signatures) in civil transactions as an alternative to handwritten signatures. Art. 126(a) further emphasizes\n                that, if a document that the law normally prescribes to be in writing is drawn up electronically, the handwritten signature can only be replaced with QES. Examples of such documents could include, e.g.:\n            \n            \n                real estate purchases;\n                notarial acts;\n                consumer loan agreements;\n                contracts of surety;\n                acknowledgement of debt; etc.\n            \n            \n                the Administrative Procedure Act (VwVfG), Art. 3a, likewise specifies (in relation to electronic applications submitted to government agencies) that legally required written form can be substituted with electronic form,\n                provided that the latter is affixed with a QES;\n            \n            \n                the Code of Civil Procedure, Art. 130(a), similarly provides that documents such as \"preparatory pleadings and their attachments, applications, and declarations by the parties to be submitted in writing, as well as\n                information, statements, expert opinions, translations, applications and declarations by third parties to be submitted in writing\" may be filed electronically so long as they are signed by a QES.\n            \n        \n    \n\n    \n        1. Acceptable KYC methods for AML/CFT purposes:\n        \n            As per Part II, Section III of the Bank of Italy Provisions on Customer Due Diligence implementing the Legislative Decree (\n            \n                Disposizioni in materia di adeguata verifica della clientela per il contrasto del riciclaggio e del finanziamento del terrorismo\n            \n            as amended on June 13, 2023):\n        \n        \n            \n                collection of a notarized identity document / an identity document authenticated with a QES - \"[a flow where] identification data result from [...] authenticated private deeds or qualified certificates used for the\n                generation of a digital signature associated with electronic documents\";\n            \n        \n        \n            \n                verification of digital identity with a \"high\" assurance level as per the national legislation or the eIDAS regulation / a digital identity with an eIDAS-compliant QES embedded - \"[the customer must be] in possession of a\n                digital identity, with a maximum level of security, within the System referred to in Article 64 of Legislative Decree No. 82 of 7 March 2005 and the related implementing legislation, or of a digital identity with a maximum\n                level of security or a certificate for the generation of a digital signature, issued within the scope of an electronic identification regime included in the list published by the European Commission pursuant to Article 9 of\n                Regulation (EU) No. 910/2014\";\n            \n        \n        \n            \"methods identified in Section VIII for remote operations\":\n            \n                \n                    Identity Document Verification combined with at least one additional check, including, but not limited to, Liveness Detection and Facematching, Video Identification, and Penny Drop, to mitigate fraud risks, chosen in\n                    accordance with the risk-based approach: \"In cases of remote operation, the recipients:\n                \n            \n        \n        \n            a) acquire the identification data of the customer and the executor and verify it on a copy - obtained by fax, post, in electronic format or with similar methods - of a valid identity document, in accordance with current\n            legislation;\n        \n        \n            b) carry out checks in addition to those provided for in Section V on the data acquired, according to the most appropriate methods in relation to the specific risk. By way of example, the following methods are indicated:\n            telephone contact on a fixed line (welcome call); sending communications to a physical address with return receipt; transfer made by the customer through a banking and financial intermediary based in Italy or in an EU country;\n            request to send countersigned documentation; verification of residence, domicile, activity carried out, through requests for information to the competent offices or through on-site meetings, carried out using its own personnel\n            or third parties.\n        \n        \n            In compliance with the risk-based approach, recipients can use feedback mechanisms based on innovative and reliable technological solutions (e.g. those that provide forms of biometric recognition), as long as they are assisted\n            by robust security measures [...]\".\n        \n        2. Additional use cases for Qualified Signing:\n        \n            \n                as per Art. 21 of the Italian Digital Administration Code - electronic signature of all contracts listed under Art. 1350,\n                No. 1-12 of the Italian Civil Code (unless such contracts are notarized):\n            \n        \n        \n            \n                contracts that transfer the ownership of immovable property;\n                contracts that constitute, modify or transfer the right of usufruct on immovable property, the right of surface, the right of the grantor and the emphyteuta;\n                contracts that constitute the community of rights indicated by the preceding numbers;\n                contracts that constitute or modify predial servitudes, the right of use on immovable property and the right of habitation;\n                deeds of renunciation of the rights indicated by the preceding numbers;\n                contracts for the redemption of the emphyteutic land;\n                contracts of antichresis;\n                contracts for the lease of immovable property for a period exceeding nine years;\n                company or association contracts which grant the enjoyment of immovable property or other immovable real rights for a period exceeding nine years or for an indefinite period;\n                deeds constituting perpetual or life annuities, except for the provisions relating to State income;\n                deeds of division of immovable property and other immovable real rights;\n                transactions which have as their object disputes relating to the legal relationships mentioned in the preceding numbers.\n            \n        \n        \n            electronic signature of other types of contracts / deeds / binding documents where explicitly required by law, e.g.:\n        \n        \n            \n                power of attorney for litigation.\n            \n        \n    \n\n    \n        1. Acceptable KYC methods for AML/CFT purposes:\n        \n            According to Dutch AML/CFT laws and regulations, identity verification in respect of a natural person may be carried out on the basis of documents, data or information from a reliable and independent source, with the following\n            caveats:\n        \n        \n            \n                as per Art. 4(1) of the Regulation implementing the Anti-Money Laundering and Anti-Terrorist Financing Act (\"Wwft Implementing Regulation\"), the documents\n                suitable for identity verification are: a valid passport; a valid Dutch identity card; a valid identity card issued by the competent authority in another EU Member State and bearing a photograph and the name of the holder; a\n                valid Dutch driving license; a valid driving license issued by the competent authority in another EU Member State and bearing a photograph and the name of the holder; travel documents for refugees and foreigners; alien\n                documents issued under the Aliens Act 2000; or a sufficiently reliable means of identification;\n            \n            \n                the \"\n                \n                    Q&amp;A Electronic identification means and client identification\n                \n                \" section on the De Nederlandsche Bank (DNB) website further clarifies that an eID shall also be considered sufficiently reliable when it meets the \"substantial\" or \"high\" level of assurance as per the eIDAS Regulation;\n            \n        \n        \n            \n                likewise, as per section 5.2.5, point (i) of the General Guideline on the Anti-Money Laundering and Anti-Terrorist Financial Act by\n                the Ministry of Finance, applicable to all AML/CFT-regulated entities:\n            \n        \n        \n            \n                the list of documents provided under Art. 4(1) of the Wwft Implementing Regulation is not exhaustive;\n                \n                    a \"sufficiently reliable means of identification\" as per Art. 4(1) of the Wwft Implementing Regulation is defined as \"a means of identification that meets the assurance level \"substantial\" or \"high\" as\n                    referred to in [the eIDAS Regulation]\";\n                \n                \"other means of identification can also be used, but it must be assessed on a case-by-case basis whether they meet the reliability level. It is up to the institution to determine whether this is the case\";\n            \n        \n        2. Additional use cases for Qualified Signing:\n        \n            \n                As a general rule, Book 3 Article 15a (3:15a BW) of the Dutch Civil Code (Burgerlijk Wetboek) states that \"electronic signature [regardless of whether is it a\n                QES, AES, or simple e-signature] has the same legal consequences as a handwritten signature, if the method of signature used for the electronic signature is sufficiently reliable, having regard to the purpose for which the\n                electronic signature is used and all other circumstances of the case\". For certain types of contracts and transactions (such as mortgage deeds or final wills), the notarial form may be required, in which case no electronic\n                signature serves as a substitute.\n            \n            Other laws may also establish possible uses of electronic signatures, without specifying any certain type of signature:\n        \n        \n            Tax Act (Belastingwet) (Article 6.5): filing tax returns;\n            Applications according to Social Security Act (Wet structuur uitvoeringsorganisatie werk en inkomen - SUWI) (Article 4:2);\n            \n                Civil Registration Act (Wet op de gemeentelijke basisadministratie persoonsgegevens) (Article 4:9): specifies the need to update information using an electronic\n                signature;\n            \n            Tenders Act (Aanbestedingswet) (Article 2.98): Describes the possibility of using an electronic signature for tender applications.\n        \n    \n\n    \n        1. Acceptable KYC methods for AML/CFT purposes:\n        \n            Pursuant to Article 37. 1 of the Act of 1 March 2018 on counteracting money laundering and financing of terrorism the verification of\n            the identity of a customer [or a person acting on its behalf and the beneficial owner] shall be based on confirmation of determined identification data based on:\n        \n        \n            A document confirming the identity of a natural person,\n            A document containing valid data from the extract of the relevant register or\n            \n                Other documents, data or information originating from a reliable and independent source,\n                including, whereavailable, electronic identification means or relevant trust services as set out in Regulation (EU) No 910/2014.\n            \n        \n        \n            Furthermore, as per the official\n            \n                stance\n            \n            of the Polish Financial Supervisory Authority (Komisja Nadzoru Finansowego), the mere fact that a customer is not physically present for identity verification is not necessarily indicative of high risk where \"the associated\n            increased risk of money laundering and financing of terrorism has [...] been otherwise mitigated, including, through the use of notified electronic identification means adequately to the substantial security level, referred to\n            in Regulation (EU) No. 910/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 23 July 2014 on electronic identification and trust services for electronic transactions in the internal market and repealing Directive 1999/93/EC\n            (EU OJL257, 28.8.2014, p. 73), or the requirement to use a qualified electronic signature or a signature confirmed by the ePUAP trusted profile\".\n        \n        2. Additional use cases for Qualified Signing:\n        \n            \n                Civil Code\n            \n        \n        \n            Generally, only QES is considered equivalent to a \"wet-ink\" signature, meaning that other forms of e-signature do not suffice where written form is required by law for a contract or transaction and the said contract or\n            transaction needs to be drawn up electronically (see Art. 78(1) of the Civil Code). However, where written form is not mandatory, both AES and simple e-signatures are normally recognized.\n        \n        Examples of contracts / transactions requiring written form:\n        \n            debt take-over contract (Art. 522 of the Civil Code);\n            subcontracting of construction works (Art. 647 of the Civil Code);\n            termination of tenancy (Art. 678 of the Civil Code);\n            leasing contract (Art. 709-2 of the Civil Code);\n            loan contact above a certain value (Art. 720 of the Civil Code);\n            annuity contract (Art. 903-1 of the Civil Code); etc.\n        \n        \n            Other specific use cases:\n        \n        \n            medicine prescriptions issued electronically (Art. 96a of the Pharmaceutical Law of 6 September 2001);\n            \n                confirmation of an ePUAP trusted profile by the applicant, which consists in verifying the compliance of the data contained in the user profile with the actual state and granting rights resulting from having an ePUAP trusted\n                profile, as well as extending the validity and invalidation of the ePUAP trusted profile (Art. 20c of the Act of 17 February 2005 on the computerization of the activities of entities performing public tasks);\n            \n            electronic documents sent to and issued by customs authorities (Art. 10b of the Customs Law of 19 March 2004);\n            application for the issue of a certified copy of a civil status record (Arts. 28 and 48, Act of 6 August 2010 on identity cards); etc.\n        \n    \n\n    \n        1. Acceptable KYC methods for AML/CFT purposes:\n        \n            Based on Article 12(1) of the Prevention of Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing Law 10/2010 of 28 April and Article 21 of the\n            Regulation on the Prevention of Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing approved by Royal Decree 304/2014 of 5 May, the following\n            non-face-to-face identity verification methods are acceptable:\n        \n        \n            \n                \"qualified electronic signature regulated by Regulation (EU) No. 910/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 23 July 2014 on electronic identification and trust services for electronic transactions in the\n                internal market and repealing Directive 1999/93/EC\";\n            \n            \"the first deposit comes from an account in the name of the same client opened in an entity domiciled in Spain, the European Union or equivalent third countries\";\n            \"the customer's identity is evidenced by means of a copy of the relevant identity document [...], provided that the copy is issued by a notary public\";\n            \n                \"the customer's identity is evidenced by other secure procedures for customer identification in remote transactions, provided that such procedures have been previously authorised by the Executive Service of the Commission\n                for the Prevention of Money Laundering and Monetary Offences [this includes (i) SEPBLAC specifications regarding authorization of remote identification by videoconference, 12 February, 2016, and (ii) SEPBLAC specifications\n                regarding authorization of remote identification by video recording, 11 May, 2017]\".\n            \n        \n        Notably, except where QES is used, an ID copy needs to be obtained from the customer within one month of the establishment of the business relationship.\n        2. Additional use cases for Qualified Signing:\n        \n            \n                Spanish Law 6/2020 of November 11, regulating certain aspects of electronic trust services (Art. 326), establishes different statuses for qualified (QES\n                is required) and non-qualified (AES is required) trust services used in private documents;\n            \n            \n                Law 39/2015 of October 1, on the Procedural Procedure of the Public Administration Community (Arts. 9 and 10), allows to use QES (as well as AES) to carry\n                out administrative procedures (such as submission of applications or requests) electronically;\n            \n            \n                Law 24/2001 of December 27, on fiscal, administrative, and social order measures (Art. 109), prescribes QES for notaries and registrars of commercial and\n                personal property;\n            \n            \n                Law 18/2011 of July 5, regulating the use of information and communication technologies in the Administration of Justice (Art. 14), specifies that QES\n                or AES may be used for procedural acts carried out by judicial bodies and justice professionals.\n            \n        \n    \n\n    \n        1. Acceptable KYC methods for AML/CFT purposes:\n        \n            The\n            \n                Swiss Financial Market Supervisory Authority (\"FINMA\") Circular 2016/7 on \"Video and online identification\"\n            \n            provisions on the acceptable KYC procedures as regards natural persons are largely accumulated in Section III(A) (\"Video identification on a par with in-person identification for individuals\") and Section IV (\"Online\n            identification\"):\n        \n        \n            Section III(A) requires \"audio-visual real-time (live transmission) communication\" and active verbal interaction between a user and the obliged entity, along with the analysis of the user's ID as detailed below;\n        \n        \n            \n                Section IV(B)(a) (\"Electronic copy of an identification document authenticated by the financial intermediary\") allows (subject to certain conditions) for an automated KYC flow. This includes, inter alia, nuanced analysis of\n                the user's identity document; in particular, the obliged entity must: (i) read and decrypt the document's machine-readable zone; (ii) examine the document's optical security features, such as holographic-cinematic marks or\n                printed elements with latent image / tilting effects; (iii) match the document with the other data provided by the user; and, (iv) if not familiar with the document, compare it with reference samples from an ID database\n                referring to security features, character type and size, and layout;\n            \n        \n        \n            \n                Section IV(B)(b) (\"Electronic copy of an identification document with qualified electronic signature\") prescribes the following procedure: \"the financial intermediary obtains an electronic copy of all the relevant pages of\n                the contracting party's identification document transmitted electronically by the contracting party and an authentication by means of a qualified electronic signature from a provider of certification services recognised in\n                Switzerland under the Federal Certification Services Act (ZertES; SR 943.03). The financial intermediary checks the information in the identification document against the qualified electronic signature profile\". Importantly,\n                to date, no agreement on the mutual recognition of electronic signatures has been concluded between Switzerland and another third country (or the European Union). Where no such agreement exists, a qualified electronic\n                signature based on a qualified certificate under foreign law is not recognized as being equivalent to a qualified electronic signature under Swiss law.\n            \n            \n                finally, as per Section IV(B)(c) (\"Digital authentication\"), \"the authentication provider as defined in Article 49 AMLO-FINMA can also issue confirmations of authenticity electronically to the financial intermediary. To do\n                so, the provider makes an electronic copy of all the relevant pages of the identification document, inseparably attaches a confirmation that its contents match those of the identification document, adds a digital timestamp\n                and an employee signature to the file, and provides it to the financial intermediary\".\n            \n        \n        2. Additional use cases for Qualified Signing:\n        \n            \n                Swiss Code of Obligations or Obligationenrecht, Art, 14, establishes QES as an equivalent to the handwritten signature where the latter is legally required -\n                including, for example, the following transactions and contractual relationships:\n            \n            \n                applications to the commercial register;\n                assignment of rights;\n                subscription forms regarding the subscription of newly issued shares;\n                pledge of claims;\n                promises of a gift;\n                incorporation of an association;\n                contract of surety (subject to certain conditions);\n                agreements on contribution in kind;\n                transfer declaration of registered or uncertified securities; etc.\n            \n            \n                Federal Law on Debt Enforcement and Bankruptcy (SchKG), Art. 33a, considers QES necessary for documents \"submitted electronically to the debt collection and\n                bankruptcy offices and the supervisory authorities\".\n            \n        \n    \n"
}
[/block]

# Penny Drop Verification
URL: https://docs.sumsub.com/docs/penny-drop-verification

Penny Drop Verification is Sumsub's document-free data verification solution designed to deliver a real-time API connection to customer banking information on a pan-European level through a nominal monetary transfer. 

The solution is based on the Payment Initiation Service (PIS) under the EU/EEA open banking technical infrastructure, which allows authorized Third Party Providers (TPPs) to securely access consolidated information from a customer's payment account in 28 countries and over 550 financial institutions. As a result, Penny Drop Verification covers over 90% of the adult population in each supported jurisdiction, as it successfully represents all residents that have an active account in any one of the available banks.

Penny Drop Verification solution works by simply having the applicant authenticate themselves on their selected banking platform via a standard login process and confirm a nominal payment to Sumsub's digital account, which is instantly processed and refunded shortly after. The result delivers comprehensive customer account ownership and transaction details, as well as a confirmation of their real-time presence in compliance with a range of use cases under the AML/CTF and the Revised Payment Services Directive (PSD2), including Identity, Payment Method, and Qualified Electronic Signature (QES) Verification.

Overall, Penny Drop Account offers one of the most well-recognized solutions to customer banking data verification that helps businesses achieve international AML/CTF and PSD2 compliance, prevent identity fraud, and optimize due diligence procedures, all through a seamless, document-free user experience.

> 📘 Note
> 
> - The open banking infrastructure under the Directive (EU) 2015/2366, officially referred to as the Revised Payment Services Directive (PSD2), is a technological and legal framework which mandates Account Servicing Payment Service Providers (ASPSPs) in the European Economic Area (EEA), including banks, credit institutions, and electronic money institutions, to allow authorized Third Party Providers (TPPs) access to financial customer data via application programming interfaces (APIs) on the basis of explicit customer consent. TPPs consist of Payment Initiation Service Providers (PISPs), Account Information Service Providers (AISPs), and Card-Based Payment Instrument Issuers (CBPIIs).
> - Sumsub offers its solution by leveraging the Payment Initiation Service (PIS) through Volt, a licensed and PSD2-compliant PISP. As a result, Sumsub itself is not subject to the PSD2 regulatory obligations.
> - All payment transactions are conducted using Sumsub's Virtual Account on the Volt platform by default. Clients that would like to set up and use their own Virtual Account with Volt should inform their Customer Success Manager about it. This option requires passing an additional due diligence process and executing a supplementary Service Agreement with Volt, Sumsub acting as the supporting intermediary. The process takes around 2-3 weeks to complete. Once the Virtual Account is created, Sumsub will use the client's API key to enable the solution.
> - The solution currently facilitates connectivity to banking institutions that process Euro (EUR) currency transactions.
> - The current nominal transaction amount is set to 0.10 EUR; however, this may be increased in the future, provided Sumsub encounters cases with banks that do not process such small transactions.

# Use cases

Penny Drop Verification satisfies three primary use cases for Sumsub clients.

# Solution benefits

Penny Drop Verification is valuable for all businesses enrolling new customers.

Key benefits of the Sumsub Penny Drop Verification solution include the following:

- Coverage. Reach verified records of over 90% of the national adult population in each of the 28 supported countries with the use of a single solution.
- Compliance. Meet international CDD and EDD requirements under AML/CTF, PSD2, and upcoming European regulatory mandates.
- Scalability. Onboard customers on a pan-European level, without having to comply with country-specific standards, such as ANSSI in France, SEPBLAC in Spain, and ADR in Romania.
- Conversion. Improve the speed and user experience of user verification in comparison to traditional methods, leading to a direct increase in both customer conversion and approval rates.
- Fraud Prevention. Verify customer data legitimacy, minimizing the risk of identity fraud, such as account takeover or illicit use of payment credentials via SCA and data cross-matching features.
- Seamless Integration. Seamlessly integrate Sumsub's solution into your system via our lightweight Mobile and Web SDK frameworks.

# How Penny Drop Verification works

Sumsub's Penny Drop Verification is an advanced customer banking data verification solution that enables a real-time API connection to customer banking information on a pan-European level via a nominal monetary transfer.

## Step-by-step product journey

1. Country and bank account selection. An applicant selects their country and preferred banking platform for verification.
2. Bank account login. The applicant logs into their bank account using Strong Customer Authentication (SCA).
3. Payment confirmation. The applicant authorizes a nominal payment, typically of 0.10 EUR, to be processed from their account. 
4. Real-time presence. The relevant applicant identity and account data are retrieved to confirm the applicant's real-time presence. 
5. Account ownership validation. Sumsub cross-checks the information to ensure a complete identity match and validates the account ownership.
6. Payment refund. The system automatically refunds the payment amount to the applicant.

# Supported jurisdictions

The Penny Drop Verification solution is supported by the banks of the following jurisdictions:

Explore our Coverage Sheet for a full breakdown of ASPSPs enabled by our Penny Drop Verification solution within each jurisdiction for payment transaction and refund execution.

# Compliance overview

The Sumsub Penny Drop Verification solution aligns with critical European regulatory frameworks, ensuring businesses meet compliance requirements while enhancing the security of financial transactions:

1. Identity Verification. Fully automated user onboarding in compliance with the national AML/CTF regulatory requirements for customer due diligence in Europe(see the breakdown by country below).
2. QES Verification in Germany. Under the requirements of the German Money Laundering Act (GwG § 12 (1) 3.), when a person's identity is verified by means of a Qualified Electronic Signature, the obliged entity is also required to ensure that a transaction is executed directly from a payment account, which is held in the name of the verified individual.
3. Payment Method Verification. Under the 4th Anti-Money Laundering Directive (Directive (EU) 2015/849), all regulated institutions whose operations involve payment transaction processing are advised to perform payment method ownership verification in order to combat both money laundering and the financing of terrorist activities. The Penny Drop Verification solution helps businesses ensure the legitimacy and ownership of the payer’s account, providing accurate identification and traceability of any executed transaction. This verification process plays a pivotal role in safeguarding the financial ecosystem against fraud and illicit activities.
4. Instant payment processing. All payment service providers (PSPs) in the Single Euro Payments Area (SEPA) are required to offer instant payment transactions as a standard service under the Instant Payments Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2024/886), ensuring they are as accessible as traditional SEPA Credit Transfers. The regulation will become applicable to all EU member countries in the eurozone in October 2025, as well as those outside of the eurozone in July 2027.

For more information on the solution compliance assessment, refer to this article.

      min-width: 200px;\n    }\n\n    .table-text h2 {\n        font-size: 14px;\n        margin: 0px;\n        color: #384248;\n    }\n\n    .table-text p {\n        font-size: 14px;\n        margin: 0;\n        color: #414552;\n    }\n\n    .table-text a {\n        color: #377cf7;\n        text-decoration: underline;\n    }\n\n    .table-text tr:first-child {\n        margin-bottom: 20px;\n    }\n\n\n    \n        What is the difference between SEPA Credit Transfer and SEPA Instant payment transfer methods?\n        \n            The Single Euro Payments Area (SEPA) is a pan-European payment system that enables international bank transfers in the Euro currency across 36 participating countries, including all 27 member states of the EU and Andorra, Iceland, Lichtenstein, Monaco, Montenegro, Norway, San Marino, Switzerland, United Kingdom and the Vatican City. SEPA Credit Transfers typically take 1–2 business days to settle. SEPA Instant, on the other hand, facilitates real-time payments, settling transactions in a matter of seconds, even on weekends and holidays. Currently, each financial institution decides internally on the payment system they adopt, therefore, to avoid delays in customer verification and session drop-offs, Sumsub's Penny Drop Verification solution covers only those banks that process at least 80% of all payment transactions within 30 minutes.\n        \n    \n    \n        To whose bank account will the payment transaction ultimately be sent?\n        \n            There are two options:\n        \n\n        \n            If the business opts to use Sumsub's virtual settlement account, the payment will be received by Sumsub and automatically refunded to the end-user.\n            \n                If the business chooses to use their own settlement account, the payment will be sent directly to the client’s account. This option requires passing an additional due diligence process and executing a supplementary Service Agreement with Sumsub's partnering Payment Initiation Service Provider (PISP) Volt, Sumsub acting as the supporting intermediary. The process takes around 2-3 weeks to complete.\n            \n        \n    \n    \n        Which currencies does Penny Drop Verification support for payment transactions?\n        \n            Currently, Penny Drop Verification only supports transactions made in the Euro (€) currency.\n        \n    \n    \n        What is the possible range of payment amounts that the end-user may be charged?\n        \n            The Penny Drop Verification payment amount depends directly on the minimum that is accepted by the internal policies of the customer's ASPSP. This amount typically ranges between 0.10 to 1 EUR.\n        \n    \n    \n        Are Sumsub clients required to perform any additional actions to enable the Penny Drop Refund feature?\n        \n           No, this feature will be enabled for all clients using the Penny Drop Verification solution and relying on Sumsub's Virtual Account with Volt. \n        \n    \n    \n        Will the end-user be charged an additional transfer fee for the payment transaction?\n        Both SEPA Credit Transfers and SEPA Instant international transfers generally cost the same as local domestic bank transfers, and are often free of charge. However, some financial institutions may charge additional fees, depending on their internal policy. Since Penny Drop Verification only supports Euro-to-Euro transactions, there will be no currency conversion fees involved.\n    \n    \n        What is the maximum time for payment transaction processing?\n         The exact processing time depends on the payment system used by the financial institution. Within the SEPA Instant infrastructure, settlements occur within seconds. However, for standard SEPA Credit Transfers or other non-instant methods, processing can take up to 72 hours. \nTo avoid delays in customer verification and session drop-offs, Sumsub's Penny Drop Verification solution covers only those banks that process at least 80% of all payment transactions within 30 minutes. Nonetheless, our system allows for a maximum of 24 hours for the transaction to be either successfully approved by the payer's ASPSP and received by Sumsub, or rejected due to one of the applicable reasons.\n        \n    \n    \n        What is the maximum time for refund transaction processing?\n        \n           While Sumsub's solution processes refunds instantly, the settlement time on the beneficiary’s side may vary, depending on the bank’s system. Within the SEPA Instant infrastructure, refunds are completed the same day, while with SEPA Credit Transfers the process may take up to 48–72 hours. Sumsub's Penny Drop Verification solution covers only those banks that process at least 80% of all payment transactions within 30 minutes.\n        \n    \n    \n        What are the possible reasons why a payment transaction could take longer to process?\n        \n           A payment transaction could take longer to process due to:\n        \n\n        \n            Payer's ASPSP processing the transactions in batch, instead of real-time.\n            Ongoing fraud and compliance checks conducted by the payer's ASPSP.\n            The transaction being processed by the payer's ASPSP outside business hours.\n        \n\n    \n     \n        What are the possible reasons why a payment transaction could be rejected?\n        \n            A payment transaction may be rejected due to:\n        \n\n        \n            User not completing the SCA on the ASPSP's platform.\n            User denying the payment transaction authorization.\n          Payment transaction being refused by the payer's ASPSP.\n          User having insufficient funds in their bank account.\n            User revoking the payment via their ASPSP after authorizing it.\n            Transaction processing exceeding the 24-hour threshold.\n        \n    \n    \n        What are the possible reasons why a refund transaction could be rejected?\n        \n            There are no known scenarios in which the refund transaction could be left unprocessed. Sumsub will ensure that the user's payment is either automatically or manually returned to them. \n        \n       \n    \n        Does Sumsub charge its clients per initiated or completed Penny Drop Verification session?\n        \n            Sumsub's clients are charged per completed Penny Drop Verification sessions, once the end-user authorizes the transaction.\n        \n    \n    \n        How can Sumsub's clients request an addition to the list of covered financial institutions? \n        \n            Clients can submit a request to Sumsub via their dedicated Customer Success Manager to check if the desired ASPSP is available within the EU/EEA or other open banking ecosystems and supports SEPA Credit Transfers or SEPA Instant. If the bank is not already supported, Sumsub will assess processing times and the payment methods available to determine whether the ASPSP can be added.\n        \n    \n"
}

# Get started with Penny Drop Verification
URL: https://docs.sumsub.com/docs/get-started-with-penny-drop-verification

The Penny Drop Verification solution is currently supported via the WebSDK framework.

You are not required to perform any technical steps to enable the Penny Drop Verification feature, but you need to contact your Customer Success Manager to turn it on after you create your first verification level with this feature. 

> 📘 Note
> 
> - MobileSDK is not available for Penny Drop Verification solution.
> - If you would like to set up and use your own virtual account with Volt, inform your Customer Success Manager about it, as this would require passing an additional due diligence process and executing a supplementary Service Agreement with Volt.

# Set up Penny Drop Verification level

To use Penny Drop Verification:

1. Set up the WebSDK integration.
2. Contact your CSM to turn Penny Drop Verification on.
3. Set up a verification level to be used for Penny Drop Verification:
   1. On the Verification Steps tab, add an Identity document step and select any documents to be used as a verification basis.
   2. Add other steps you normally use to verify the identity of your customers, such as selfies, AML screening, and so on.
   3. Add the Non-Doc Verification step. 
   4. Add Penny Drop check for each country whose banks support the Penny Drop Verification solution.
4. Test your configuration in Sandbox mode:
   1. At the Non-Doc step, select the Penny Drop verification type. You will be redirected to the Volt Sandbox page. 
   2. In the Banks section, select Volt Mock Bank.
   3. Click Continue on Desktop to confirm the test payment transaction.
5. Switch to Production mode to make your verification level live.
6. Review verification results.

# Review verification results

You can get Penny Drop Verification results in either of the following ways:

- Using the Sumsub Dashboard
- Using the Sumsub API

## View verification results in the Dashboard

To view the results of Penny Drop Verification in the Dashboard:

1. Navigate to the Applicants page and open the target applicant profile.
2. Scroll down to the Penny Drop Verification section and review the results.

Available verification statuses are:

- Green — user verification is successfully completed.
- Yellow — user verification is currently in progress.
- Error — user verification was not completed because the service is currently unavailable.

## Get verification results via API

To get the results of Penny Drop Verification via the API, send a `GET` request, as shown below.

Request

```curl
curl -X GET \
  'https://api.sumsub.com/resources/checks/latest?type=BANKDETAILSCHECK&applicantId={applicantId}'// Applicant ID
```

Response

The response will include the transaction details, sender and beneficiary bank information, and refund details when using the Penny Drop Verification Refund (intlcrdpennydrop) product:

```json
{
  "checks": [
    {
      "answer": "GREEN", // Verification result
      "checkType": "BANKDETAILSCHECK", // Check type
      "createdAt": "2025-03-09 19:25:07", // Verification date
      "id": "aa3b1641-cf91-4d19-95e1-62d30d7f401a", // User ID
      "inputDoc": {
        "idDocType": "PROOFOFPAYMENT", // The document used for IDV
        "country": "DEU" // The ID doc issuing country
      },
      "extractedDoc": { // The information extracted from the doc
        "firstName": "Jane Doe", // Applicant name
        "additionalFields": [ // Additional information extracted from the doc
          {
            "name": "bank", // Applicant payment institution type
            "value": "Volt Mock Bank" // Applicant payment institution name
          }
        ]
      },
      "ekycCheckInfo": {
        "confirmationId": "oOp4x5Rm72yeFOx3kfs5FCFv", // Confirmation ID
        "confirmationType": "oAuth" // Confirmation type (oAuth/OTP)
      },
      "bankDetailsCheckInfo": { // Includes BANKDETAILSCHECK check results
        "accountDetails": { // Includes the objects representing the sender details
          "personFullName": "Jane Doe", // Sender full name
          "iban": "DE53500105177415947516", // Sender IBAN number
          "bankName": "Volt Mock Bank", // Sender bank name
          "bankBranchName": "Volt Mock Bank", // Sender bank branch name
          "bic": "COBADEFF", // Sender bank BIK
          "country": "DEU" // Sender bank country. Presented as an alpha-3 country code (for example, DEU, GBR, ARG, and so on)
        },
        "transferInfo": { // Transfer common details
          "transferDetails": { // Transfer details
            "paymentId": "7356dafb-67e2-473c-acb7-baa9d3fd7736", // Payment ID
            "reference": "PNLykibtDB", // Unique payment reference from the merchant
            "createdAt": "2025-03-09 19:24:35", // Payment date
            "amount": 0.1, // Payment amount
            "currency": "EUR", // Payment currency
            "status": "RECEIVED", // Payment status
            "originalStatus": "RECEIVED", // Detailed payment status
            "type": "OTHER", // Transaction type
            "refundStatus": "COMPLETED", // Refund status
            "originalRefundStatus": "REFUNDCONFIRMED",
            "refundReference": "026cefd0-a174-4ca3-a1e6-533a129d9c32" // Unique payment reference from the merchant
          },
          "beneficiaryDetails": { // Beneficiary information
            "personFullName": "Trade Ltd", // Beneficiary full name
            "iban": "DE89370400440532013000", // Beneficiary bank IBAN
            "bic": "COBADEFF", // Beneficiary bank BIC
            "country": "DEU" // Beneficiary bank country
          }
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Available verification statuses are:

- Green — user verification is successfully completed.
- Yellow — user verification is currently in progress.
- Error — user verification was not completed because the service is currently unavailable.

================================================================================

# Supported ASPSPs in the EU/EEA
URL: https://docs.sumsub.com/docs/aspsps-covered-under-penny-drop-verification

Below, you will find the list of both the jurisdictions as well as the financial institutions supported by Sumsub's Penny Drop Verification solution. The table additionally provides information about the percentage of all transactions that are processed within a 30-minute period (if available).

> 📘 Note
> 
> - 
> - If you would like to include an additional ASPSP to the supported list, request this via your dedicated Customer Success Manager at csm@sumsub.com. The Sumsub team will assess if the desired ASPSP is available within the EU/EEA open banking infrastructure and supports SEPA Credit or SEPA Instant Transfers, and determine whether it can be enabled.

# Solution compliance assessment
URL: https://docs.sumsub.com/docs/penny-drop-solution-compliance-assessment

Sumsub offers its tailored Penny Drop Verification solution to deliver seamless and compliant customer identification, qualified signing, and payment method verification journeys for the European market. Sumsub's solution is recognized within the national regulatory frameworks in a number of EU/EEA jurisdictions, which enables our clients to effortlessly fulfill their compliance requirements without compromising their customer conversion and approval rates in the process.

The following analyses elaborate on the compliance of Penny Drop Verification with national AML/CTF regulations of multiple jurisdictions in Europe through a detailed examination of the requirements applicable to customer risk assessment, data collection, and data verification.

# Regulatory recognition

================================================================================

# Bank Account Verification
URL: https://docs.sumsub.com/docs/bank-account-verification

Bank Account Verification is Sumsub's document-free data verification solution designed specifically for regulated businesses to enable a real-time connection to customer banking information on a pan-European level, including account ownership, balance details, and transaction history. 

It is based on the Account Information Service (AIS) that allows authorized Third Party Providers (TPPs) to securely access consolidated information from a customer's payment account in 28 countries and over 2200 financial institutions by using the EU/EEA open banking technical infrastructure. As a result, the solution covers over 90% of the adult population in each supported jurisdiction, as it successfully represents all residents that have an active account in any one of the available banks.

Bank Account Verification solution works by simply having the applicant authenticate themselves on their selected banking platform via a standard login process, which leverages Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) tools, ensuring protected personal and financial data sharing. The result delivers comprehensive customer information in compliance with a range of use cases under the AML/CTF and the Revised Payment Services Directive (PSD2), including Payment Method, Financial Vulnerability, Creditworthiness, and Source of Funds/Wealth Checks, as well as a confirmation of their real-time presence and data owners.

Overall, Bank Account Verification offers one of the most well-recognized solutions to customer banking data verification that helps businesses achieve international AML/CTF and PSD2 compliance, prevent identity fraud, and optimize due diligence procedures, all through a seamless, document-free user experience.

> 📘 Note
> 
> - The Open Banking infrastructure under the Directive (EU) 2015/2366, officially referred to as the Revised Payment Services Directive (PSD2), is a technological and legal framework which mandates Account Servicing Payment Service Providers (ASPSPs) in the European Economic Area (EEA), including banks, credit institutions, and electronic money institutions, to allow authorized Third Party Providers (TPPs) access to financial customer data via application programming interfaces (APIs) on the basis of explicit customer consent. TPPs consist of Payment Initiation Service Providers (PISPs), Account Information Service Providers (AISPs), and Card-Based Payment Instrument Issuers (CBPIIs).
> - The Account Information Service (AIS) is an online service regulated under the PSD2 that allows authorized or registered AISPs to access and consolidate financial customer information from ASPSPs on behalf of the customer.
> - The solution can be enabled both via the Standard level type used for customer onboarding, as well as the Applicant actions level type, leveraging the Payment methods verification infrastructure, to trigger Bank Account Verification after a specific event or customer action.

# Use cases

Bank Account Verification satisfies three primary use cases for Sumsub clients.

# Solution benefits

Key benefits of the Sumsub Bank Account Verification solution include the following:

- Efficiency. Automate customer data verification, eliminating the need for manual document inspection and authenticity checks.
- Coverage. Reach verified records of over 90% of the national adult population in each of the 28 supported countries with the use of a single solution.
- Compliance. Meet international CDD and EDD requirements under AML/CTF, PSD2, and upcoming European regulatory mandates.
- Conversion. Simplify the end-user journey and eliminate drop-offs by leveraging a simple and familiar online banking authentication process.
- Fraud Prevention. Verify customer data legitimacy, minimizing the risk of identity fraud, such as account takeover or illicit use of payment credentials.
- Onboarding Optimization. Implement early-stage customer segmentation, allowing low-risk applicants through a simplified onboarding flow.
- Seamless integration. Seamlessly integrate Sumsub's solution into your system via our lightweight SDK and API frameworks.

# How Bank Account Verification works

Sumsub's Bank Account Verification is an advanced customer banking data verification solution that enables a real-time API connection between businesses and financial institutions via the EU/EEA open banking infrastructure, in full compliance with international AML/CTF and PSD2 regulations.

The solution can be implemented through 2 different steps within the verification flow:

1. Non-Doc step. Include Bank Account Verification as part of the initial onboarding by using the Non-Doc step in a Standard  level type.
2. Payment methods step. Configure Bank Account Verification as an action triggered by a specific event using the Payment methods verification step in the Applicant actions level type.

## Bank Account Verification via Non-Doc step

1. Applicant selects their country and preferred banking platform for verification.
2. Applicant logs into their bank account and provides consent for data sharing.
3. The system automatically retrieves all relevant data stored on the account.
4. Sumsub cross-checks the applicant's information to ensure a complete identity match.

[/block]

## Bank Account Verification via Payment methods step

1. Applicant selects the Bank account payment verification method.
2. Applicant selects Online banking as the option for bank account verification.
3. Applicant selects their country and preferred banking platform for verification.
4. Applicant logs into their bank account and provides consent for data sharing.
5. The system automatically retrieves all relevant data stored on the account.
6. Sumsub cross-checks the applicant's information to ensure a complete identity match.

# Supported jurisdictions

The Bank Account Verification solution is supported by the banks of the following jurisdictions:

Explore our Coverage Sheet for a full breakdown of ASPSPs enabled by our Bank Account Verification solution within each jurisdiction for individual and business account data enrichment.

# Compliance overview

The Sumsub Bank Account Verification solution aligns with critical European regulatory frameworks, ensuring businesses meet compliance requirements while enhancing the security of financial transactions:

- Payment Method Verification. Under the 4th Anti-Money Laundering Directive (Directive (EU) 2015/849), all regulated institutions whose operations involve payment transaction processing are advised to perform payment method ownership verification in order to combat both money laundering and the financing of terrorist activities. The Bank Account Verification solution helps businesses ensure the legitimacy and ownership of the payer’s account, providing accurate identification and traceability of any executed transaction. This verification process plays a pivotal role in safeguarding the financial ecosystem against fraud and illicit activities.
- Financial Vulnerability and Creditworthiness Checks:
  - Financial Institutions. Verifying Financial Vulnerability and Creditworthiness is a crucial compliance measure across regulated industries, including under the 4th Anti-Money Laundering Directive (Directive (EU) 2015/849), European Banking Authority (EBA) Guidelines on loan origination and monitoring (EBA/GL/2020/06), and Markets in Financial Instruments Directive II (MiFID II) (2014/65/EU). These checks aim to ensure that individuals have the financial capacity to engage in transactions or commitments, reducing risks associated with over-indebtedness, fraud, and financial abuse. Bank account verification solutions support this process by confirming account ownership, monitoring transaction history, and validating financial stability, enabling institutions to assess creditworthiness and comply with regulatory standards effectively.
  - Gambling Operators. Gambling Operators under most national regulatory frameworks are required to implement Financial Vulnerability and Affordability Checks to assess whether customers can afford their gambling activity, including analyzing their income, spending patterns, and other financial data, which indicates both the necessary deposit as well as spending limits within the platform. That includes, but is not limited to, the UK Gambling Commission License Conditions and Codes of Practice (LCCP), Ontario Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario (AGCO) Standards, British Columbia Lottery Corporation (BCLC), Australian Communications and Media Authority (ACMA)  Interactive Gambling Act 2001, and USA State Gambling Regulations, including the New Jersey Division of Gaming Enforcement (NJDGE) Regulations, and Nevada Gaming Control Board (NGCB) Regulations.
- Strong Customer Authentication. To validate the financial account ownership the solution leverages Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) methods that have been adopted by all regulated banking institutions in line with the 5th Anti-Money Laundering Directive (Directive (EU) 2018/843) and PSD2 (Directive (EU) 2015/2366). This ensures a protected identity, account, and transaction data sharing access for the end user and regulatory compliance with the directive’s requirements for businesses concerning ownership verification.

# Setup and configuration

The provided Bank Account Verification solution scheme demonstrates the following: 

- Overall solution functionality
- Required steps of the end-user journey
- Integration architecture between Sumsub and the Open Banking Infrastructure

The Bank Account Verification solution is currently supported via Sumsub's WebSDK framework.

Refer to the complete technical integration guide to get started with the implementation process of Bank Account Verification.
